D: Original: Statesman - Plato (Benjamin Jowett translation)
D: Narrative: Statesman - Plato - Narrative
D: Refashioned: Statesman - Plato - Refashioned

D: Original:
SOCRATES: I owe you many thanks, indeed, Theodorus, for the acquaintance both of Theaetetus and of the Stranger.

THEODORUS: And in a little while, Socrates, you will owe me three times as many, when they have completed for you the delineation of the Statesman and of the Philosopher, as well as of the Sophist.

SOCRATES: Sophist, statesman, philosopher! O my dear Theodorus, do my ears truly witness that this is the estimate formed of them by the great calculator and geometrician?

THEODORUS: What do you mean, Socrates?

SOCRATES: I mean that you rate them all at the same value, whereas they are really separated by an interval, which no geometrical ratio can express.

THEODORUS: By Ammon, the god of Cyrene, Socrates, that is a very fair hit; and shows that you have not forgotten your geometry. I will retaliate on you at some other time, but I must now ask the Stranger, who will not, I hope, tire of his goodness to us, to proceed either with the Statesman or with the Philosopher, whichever he prefers.

STRANGER: That is my duty, Theodorus; having begun I must go on, and not leave the work unfinished. But what shall be done with Theaetetus?

THEODORUS: In what respect?

STRANGER: Shall we relieve him, and take his companion, the Young Socrates, instead of him? What do you advise?

THEODORUS: Yes, give the other a turn, as you propose. The young always do better when they have intervals of rest.
D: Narrative: Socrates expresses his gratitude to Theodorus for introducing him to both Theaetetus and the Eleatic Stranger. Theodorus replies that Socrates will owe him three times the thanks once the Stranger has completed his delineation of the Statesman and the Philosopher, having already finished with the Sophist. Socrates seizes on this, noting that Theodorus -- the great calculator and geometrician -- seems to rate all three figures at the same value, when in truth they are separated by an interval no geometrical ratio can express. Theodorus laughs at the hit, swearing by the god Ammon of Cyrene, and concedes the point, but turns the conversation forward: the Stranger should proceed to whichever inquiry he prefers, the Statesman or the Philosopher. The Stranger agrees he must continue what he has begun and not leave the work unfinished, but raises a practical question: shall they relieve Theaetetus and take his companion, Young Socrates, as the respondent instead? Theodorus approves, observing that the young always do better when they have intervals of rest.
D: Refashioned: Having just completed their investigation of the Sophist, the group turns to the next question: what is the Statesman? Socrates teases Theodorus for treating the Sophist, the Statesman, and the Philosopher as equivalent figures -- as though a great mathematician would fail to see they are separated by immeasurable distances. Theodorus takes the joke in stride and asks the Stranger to continue with whichever inquiry he prefers. The Stranger agrees -- the work must not be left unfinished -- but suggests swapping in a fresh respondent. Young Socrates will take over for the tired Theaetetus.
D: Elegant: The Sophist has been defined. Now the Stranger must pursue the Statesman. Socrates notes that no geometrical ratio can measure the distance between the Sophist, the Statesman, and the Philosopher -- they may share a surface resemblance, but they inhabit different orders of being. A fresh respondent, Young Socrates, steps in for the inquiry ahead.
D: Summary: Following the investigation of the Sophist, the group agrees the Stranger should next define the Statesman. Young Socrates replaces Theaetetus as the respondent. Socrates observes that the Sophist, Statesman, and Philosopher are far more different from each other than they appear.
D: Distilled: The Sophist, the Statesman, and the Philosopher may look alike on the surface, but no ratio can express the true distance between them.
Notes: This dialogue is explicitly framed as a sequel to the Sophist, which itself followed the Theaetetus. All three dialogues share the same dramatic setting: a conversation in Athens involving Socrates, Theodorus of Cyrene (a mathematician), and an unnamed Stranger from Elea. The Stranger is associated with the Eleatic school of Parmenides and Zeno, known for rigorous logical analysis. Theodorus swears by Ammon, the Egyptian god worshipped at the oracle of Siwa in Cyrene (modern Libya), his home city. The planned trilogy (Sophist, Statesman, Philosopher) was never completed -- the Philosopher dialogue was either never written or has been lost. The method of division (diairesis) introduced in the Sophist will be carried forward as the primary analytical tool.

D: Original: SOCRATES: I think, Stranger, that both of them may be said to be in some way related to me; for the one, as you affirm, has the cut of my ugly face (compare Theaet.), the other is called by my name. And we should always be on the look-out to recognize a kinsman by the style of his conversation. I myself was discoursing with Theaetetus yesterday, and I have just been listening to his answers; my namesake I have not yet examined, but I must. Another time will do for me; to-day let him answer you.
D: Narrative: Socrates adds a personal note before stepping aside. He observes that both young men are related to him in some way: Theaetetus, the Stranger has remarked, bears the cut of Socrates' own ugly face, while the other simply shares his name. One should always be ready to recognize a kinsman by the quality of his conversation. Socrates himself examined Theaetetus yesterday and has just been listening to his answers; his namesake he has not yet tested, but another time will serve. For today, let Young Socrates answer the Stranger.
D: Refashioned: Before stepping back, Socrates notes a personal connection to both young men. Theaetetus resembles him physically -- the famous Socratic ugliness -- while Young Socrates shares his name. The real test of kinship, though, is conversation. Socrates has already examined Theaetetus; now it is his namesake's turn to be tested through dialogue.
D: Elegant: Socrates claims kinship with both young men -- one by face, the other by name. But the true test of family resemblance is how a person thinks. Having examined Theaetetus, he hands his namesake over to the Stranger's questioning.
D: Summary: Socrates notes his connection to both young men and steps aside, having already examined Theaetetus. He leaves Young Socrates to be tested by the Stranger in the investigation of the Statesman.
D: Distilled: The truest kinship is intellectual -- recognized not by appearance or name, but by the quality of one's thinking.
Notes: The reference to Socrates' ugliness (snub nose, bulging eyes) appears in multiple dialogues, notably the Theaetetus and the Symposium (where Alcibiades compares him to a Silenus statue). Young Socrates (Socrates the Younger) was a historical figure, a student in Plato's circle who also appears briefly in the Theaetetus. He should not be confused with the elder Socrates. The remark about recognizing kinsmen through conversation reflects the Socratic conviction that philosophical dialogue reveals character more reliably than any external marker.

D: Original:
STRANGER: Very good. Young Socrates, do you hear what the elder Socrates is proposing?

YOUNG SOCRATES: I do.

STRANGER: And do you agree to his proposal?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly.

STRANGER: As you do not object, still less can I. After the Sophist, then, I think that the Statesman naturally follows next in the order of enquiry. And please to say, whether he, too, should be ranked among those who have science.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Yes.

STRANGER: Then the sciences must be divided as before?

YOUNG SOCRATES: I dare say.

STRANGER: But yet the division will not be the same?

YOUNG SOCRATES: How then?

STRANGER: They will be divided at some other point.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Yes.
D: Narrative: The Stranger turns to Young Socrates and confirms he has heard the elder Socrates' proposal. Young Socrates agrees to serve as respondent. The Stranger then sets the terms of the inquiry: after the Sophist, the Statesman naturally follows next in order. He asks whether the Statesman should be ranked among those who possess knowledge. Young Socrates says yes. The Stranger notes that the sciences must be divided as before -- meaning through the same method of division used in the Sophist -- but the division will not fall at the same point. The cut will come somewhere different this time. Young Socrates agrees, and the inquiry is ready to begin.
D: Refashioned: With Young Socrates confirmed as respondent, the Stranger frames the investigation. The Statesman, like the Sophist, must be defined through systematic division of knowledge. But though the method is the same, the dividing lines will fall differently. The Sophist was found by cutting through one set of distinctions; the Statesman will require different ones. The question that launches the inquiry: does the Statesman possess a form of knowledge? Yes -- and so the task is to locate exactly which kind.
D: Elegant: The method is the same: divide knowledge systematically until the target stands alone. But the cuts will fall differently for the Statesman than they did for the Sophist. The starting premise is that statesmanship is a form of knowledge -- the question is which form, and how it differs from every other.
D: Summary: The Stranger establishes that the Statesman will be defined using the same method of division as the Sophist, but with different dividing points. The key premise is that statesmanship is a form of knowledge.
D: Distilled: The same method of division that revealed the Sophist will now be turned on the Statesman, but the cuts through knowledge will fall at entirely different points.
Notes: The method of division (diairesis) is Plato's systematic technique of classification: begin with a broad category and repeatedly divide it into two until the target definition emerges. It was introduced formally in the Phaedrus (265d-266b) and practiced extensively in the Sophist. The Stranger's insistence that the Statesman belongs among 'those who have science' (episteme) reflects the Socratic-Platonic commitment to the idea that genuine governance requires knowledge, not merely power or popular support -- a theme central to the Republic and the Gorgias.

D: Original:
STRANGER: Where shall we discover the path of the Statesman? We must find and separate off, and set our seal upon this, and we will set the mark of another class upon all diverging paths. Thus the soul will conceive of all kinds of knowledge under two classes.

YOUNG SOCRATES: To find the path is your business, Stranger, and not mine.

STRANGER: Yes, Socrates, but the discovery, when once made, must be yours as well as mine.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Very good.

STRANGER: Well, and are not arithmetic and certain other kindred arts, merely abstract knowledge, wholly separated from action?

YOUNG SOCRATES: True.

STRANGER: But in the art of carpentering and all other handicrafts, the knowledge of the workman is merged in his work; he not only knows, but he also makes things which previously did not exist.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly.

STRANGER: Then let us divide sciences in general into those which are practical and those which are purely intellectual.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Let us assume these two divisions of science, which is one whole.

STRANGER: And are 'statesman,' 'king,' 'master,' or 'householder,' one and the same; or is there a science or art answering to each of these names? Or rather, allow me to put the matter in another way.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Let me hear.

STRANGER: If any one who is in a private station has the skill to advise one of the public physicians, must not he also be called a physician?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Yes.

STRANGER: And if any one who is in a private station is able to advise the ruler of a country, may not he be said to have the knowledge which the ruler himself ought to have?

YOUNG SOCRATES: True.

STRANGER: But surely the science of a true king is royal science?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Yes.

STRANGER: And will not he who possesses this knowledge, whether he happens to be a ruler or a private man, when regarded only in reference to his art, be truly called 'royal'?

YOUNG SOCRATES: He certainly ought to be.

STRANGER: And the householder and master are the same?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Of course.

STRANGER: Again, a large household may be compared to a small state:—will they differ at all, as far as government is concerned?

YOUNG SOCRATES: They will not.
D: Narrative:
The Stranger poses the opening question: where shall they discover the path of the Statesman? They must find it, separate it off, and mark all diverging paths with different classifications, so that the mind can conceive of all knowledge under two primary classes. Young Socrates defers -- finding the path is the Stranger's business. The Stranger corrects him: the discovery, once made, must belong to them both.

The first division is between theoretical and practical knowledge. Arithmetic and its kindred arts are purely abstract -- knowledge separated entirely from action. But in carpentry and the other handicrafts, the workman's knowledge is inseparable from his making; he produces things that did not previously exist. So the sciences divide into those that are purely intellectual and those that are practical.

The Stranger then asks a crucial question: are the statesman, the king, the master of a household, and the slave-master all the same thing, or does each name correspond to a different art? He reframes the point with an analogy: if a private citizen has the medical knowledge to advise a public physician, we call that citizen a physician regardless of his official position. Likewise, if a private citizen possesses the knowledge a ruler ought to have, he deserves to be called royal in reference to his art, whether or not he actually holds power. The science of a true king is royal science. And since a large household is simply a small state, household management and political rule differ not in kind but only in scale.
D: Refashioned:
The Stranger launches the investigation with a fundamental question: what kind of knowledge does the Statesman possess? To answer it, they must divide all knowledge into categories and trace the Statesman's art to its proper location.

The first cut separates theoretical knowledge from practical knowledge. Arithmetic judges and calculates but produces nothing physical. Carpentry, by contrast, embeds knowledge in the act of making -- the craftsman's understanding is inseparable from the objects he creates. All sciences fall on one side or the other: pure intellect or hands-on production.

Where does the Statesman fall? The Stranger presses further with a key insight: the titles of king, statesman, master, and household manager all refer to the same underlying expertise. If a private citizen has the knowledge to advise a physician, we call that person a physician. If a private citizen understands what a ruler needs to know, that person possesses royal science whether or not they hold office. Knowledge defines the role, not the title. And governing a large household is no different in kind from governing a small state -- they differ only in scale.
D: Elegant: All knowledge divides into the theoretical and the practical -- what the mind grasps versus what the hands produce. The Statesman's art belongs to the mind. And here is the deeper point: the knowledge that makes someone a ruler is the same whether they govern a household or a nation, whether they hold office or advise from private life. The title is irrelevant. What matters is whether you possess the science of rule.
D: Summary: The Stranger divides all knowledge into theoretical and practical, placing the Statesman's art on the theoretical side. He then argues that king, statesman, and household manager all exercise the same fundamental expertise, differing only in scale, not in kind.
D: Distilled: The art of ruling is a form of knowledge, not a function of office -- whoever possesses it is a ruler in truth, whether they govern a household or a nation.
Notes: The theoretical/practical division of knowledge (episteme gnostike vs. episteme praktike) is a foundational move in Platonic epistemology. It will be refined further in this dialogue. The claim that household management (oikonomia) and political rule (politike) are the same art at different scales was a common Greek assumption, articulated by Xenophon in his Oeconomicus and discussed critically by Aristotle in Politics I.1-2, where Aristotle explicitly rejects this identification. The argument that knowledge rather than office defines the ruler echoes Republic 473c-d, where Plato argues that philosophers must become kings or kings become philosophers. The analogy of the private physician parallels Gorgias 464b-465a.

D: Original:
STRANGER: Then, returning to the point which we were just now discussing, do we not clearly see that there is one science of all of them; and this science may be called either royal or political or economical; we will not quarrel with any one about the name.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly not.

STRANGER: This too, is evident, that the king cannot do much with his hands, or with his whole body, towards the maintenance of his empire, compared with what he does by the intelligence and strength of his mind.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Clearly not.

STRANGER: Then, shall we say that the king has a greater affinity to knowledge than to manual arts and to practical life in general?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly he has.

STRANGER: Then we may put all together as one and the same—statesmanship and the statesman—the kingly science and the king.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Clearly.

STRANGER: And now we shall only be proceeding in due order if we go on to divide the sphere of knowledge?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Very good.

STRANGER: Think whether you can find any joint or parting in knowledge.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Tell me of what sort.

STRANGER: Such as this: You may remember that we made an art of calculation?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Yes.

STRANGER: Which was, unmistakeably, one of the arts of knowledge?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly.

STRANGER: And to this art of calculation which discerns the differences of numbers shall we assign any other function except to pass judgment on their differences?

YOUNG SOCRATES: How could we?

STRANGER: You know that the master-builder does not work himself, but is the ruler of workmen?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Yes.

STRANGER: He contributes knowledge, not manual labour?

YOUNG SOCRATES: True.

STRANGER: And may therefore be justly said to share in theoretical science?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Quite true.

STRANGER: But he ought not, like the calculator, to regard his functions as at an end when he has formed a judgment;—he must assign to the individual workmen their appropriate task until they have completed the work.

YOUNG SOCRATES: True.

STRANGER: Are not all such sciences, no less than arithmetic and the like, subjects of pure knowledge; and is not the difference between the two classes, that the one sort has the power of judging only, and the other of ruling as well?

YOUNG SOCRATES: That is evident.
D: Narrative:
The Stranger recaps: there is one science common to all these roles -- call it royal, political, or economical, the name does not matter. What matters is its nature. A king maintains his rule not primarily through physical labor or bodily strength but through the intelligence and strength of his mind. Therefore the king belongs on the intellectual side of the original division, not the practical.

With this settled, statesmanship and kingship collapse into a single art: the royal science of intellectual command. The next task is to divide the sphere of knowledge further. The Stranger asks Young Socrates to find a joint or natural division point within theoretical knowledge.

Consider arithmetic, the Stranger suggests. It is clearly a science of knowledge. Its function is to discern and judge differences among numbers -- and nothing more. It passes judgment but does not issue orders. Now consider the master-builder: he too contributes knowledge rather than manual labor, and so shares in theoretical science. But unlike the arithmetician, the master-builder does not stop at forming a judgment. He must assign tasks to individual workmen and direct their labor until the work is complete.

Here is the dividing line within theoretical knowledge: some sciences have the power of judging only, while others both judge and command. Pure judgment on one side, directive authority on the other.
D: Refashioned:
Having established that statesmanship is a theoretical rather than practical art, the Stranger now divides theoretical knowledge itself.

Some forms of knowledge only judge. Arithmetic evaluates the relationships between numbers and stops there -- it pronounces verdicts but gives no orders. Other forms of knowledge judge and then command. The master-builder, like the arithmetician, works with his mind rather than his hands. But unlike the arithmetician, the master-builder cannot simply declare his conclusions and walk away. He must assign specific tasks to specific workers and oversee them until the project is finished.

This is the crucial division within theoretical knowledge: judging versus commanding. The pure judge evaluates and stops. The commander evaluates and then directs action based on that evaluation. The Statesman clearly belongs on the commanding side.
D: Elegant: Within theoretical knowledge lies a decisive split. The arithmetician judges -- evaluates, distinguishes, pronounces -- and stops. The master-builder also judges, but then he commands: assigning tasks, directing workers, overseeing completion. One art stops at the verdict. The other carries the verdict into action through authority over others. The Statesman's knowledge is of the second kind.
D: Summary: Theoretical knowledge divides into two types: the purely evaluative (like arithmetic, which only judges) and the directive (like architecture, which judges and then commands workers). Statesmanship belongs to the directive class -- knowledge that both evaluates and issues orders.
D: Distilled: The difference between a judge and a ruler is that one stops at the verdict while the other carries it into action through command.
Notes: The distinction between critical/judging knowledge (gnostike kritike) and directive/commanding knowledge (gnostike epitaktike) is original to this dialogue and becomes one of its most important conceptual contributions. The master-builder (architekton) example is significant -- the Greek word literally means 'chief craftsman' and is the root of 'architect.' Plato uses it because the architect occupies an intermediate position: clearly intellectual (not manual labor) yet clearly directive (not purely contemplative). Aristotle later picks up this distinction in Metaphysics I.1, ranking the architect above the manual laborer precisely because he knows the 'why' and not merely the 'that.'

D: Original:
STRANGER: May we not very properly say, that of all knowledge, there are two divisions—one which rules, and the other which judges?

YOUNG SOCRATES: I should think so.

STRANGER: And when men have anything to do in common, that they should be of one mind is surely a desirable thing?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Very true.

STRANGER: Then while we are at unity among ourselves, we need not mind about the fancies of others?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly not.

STRANGER: And now, in which of these divisions shall we place the king?—Is he a judge and a kind of spectator? Or shall we assign to him the art of command—for he is a ruler?

YOUNG SOCRATES: The latter, clearly.

STRANGER: Then we must see whether there is any mark of division in the art of command too. I am inclined to think that there is a distinction similar to that of manufacturer and retail dealer, which parts off the king from the herald.

YOUNG SOCRATES: How is this?

STRANGER: Why, does not the retailer receive and sell over again the productions of others, which have been sold before?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly he does.

STRANGER: And is not the herald under command, and does he not receive orders, and in his turn give them to others?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Very true.
D: Narrative:
The Stranger confirms the division: all knowledge has two branches -- one that rules, and one that merely judges. Young Socrates agrees, and the Stranger notes that when two people are working together on a shared inquiry, being of one mind matters more than worrying about what outsiders might think of their terminology.

Now the question: in which division does the king belong? Is he a judge, a kind of spectator who evaluates? Or does he exercise the art of command? Clearly the latter -- he is a ruler. But the art of command itself admits further division. The Stranger draws an analogy between manufacturers and retail dealers. The retailer receives goods that others have produced and sells them secondhand. Similarly, a herald receives commands from above and transmits them to others below. The herald commands, but he commands with borrowed authority -- he passes along orders that originated elsewhere, just as the retailer sells goods he did not make.

Shall they then place the king in the same class as heralds, interpreters, boatswains, prophets, and all others who merely relay commands? Or should they recognize a distinct class: those who command on their own authority, who are, as it were, rulers-for-themselves?
D: Refashioned:
The king belongs to the commanding branch of knowledge, not the judging branch. But commanding itself needs further division.

Consider the difference between a manufacturer and a retailer. The manufacturer creates original goods; the retailer merely resells what others have produced. The same distinction applies to authority. A herald receives orders from a superior and passes them along -- he exercises command, but it is secondhand command, borrowed authority. Prophets, interpreters, and naval officers often function the same way: they transmit directives that originated with someone else.

The true ruler does not relay borrowed commands. He issues original directives from his own judgment. This is the difference between derivative authority and sovereign authority -- between those who pass along someone else's decisions and those who generate the decisions themselves. The king belongs in the latter class: the commander-for-himself.
D: Elegant: Not all command is equal. A herald relays orders from above. A retailer sells goods someone else made. Both exercise a kind of authority, but it is borrowed -- secondhand power. The true ruler commands from his own judgment, not from someone else's script. The distinction is between derivative authority and sovereign authority, and the Statesman stands on the sovereign side.
D: Summary: The art of command divides further into derivative command (like a herald relaying orders from above) and original command (issuing directives from one's own authority). The true ruler commands on his own authority, not as a relay for someone else's decisions.
D: Distilled: The difference between a herald and a king is the difference between passing along someone else's orders and originating your own.
Notes: The manufacturer/retailer analogy (autopoles/kapelos) reflects the Greek economic distinction between the producer who sells his own goods and the middleman who trades in others' goods. This had moral overtones in Greek thought -- Aristotle in Politics I.9-10 treats retail trade (kapeleia) as an inferior, potentially corrupting form of commerce. The herald (keryx) was an important figure in Greek political and military life, serving as an official messenger between cities, armies, and assemblies. Prophets (manteis) in Greek religion often served as interpreters of divine will rather than originators of it -- they relay the gods' commands, making them analogous to heralds. The term 'rulers-for-themselves' (autepitaktike) is coined here by the Stranger.

D: Original: STRANGER: Then shall we mingle the kingly art in the same class with the art of the herald, the interpreter, the boatswain, the prophet, and the numerous kindred arts which exercise command; or, as in the preceding comparison we spoke of manufacturers, or sellers for themselves, and of retailers,—seeing, too, that the class of supreme rulers, or rulers for themselves, is almost nameless—shall we make a word following the same analogy, and refer kings to a supreme or ruling-for-self science, leaving the rest to receive a name from some one else? For we are seeking the ruler; and our enquiry is not concerned with him who is not a ruler.
D: Narrative: The Stranger crystallizes the point. They should not lump the king together with heralds, interpreters, boatswains, prophets, and the many kindred officials who all exercise command under someone else's authority. The class of supreme rulers -- those who rule for themselves -- is so rarely distinguished that it almost lacks a name. Following the analogy of manufacturers who sell for themselves versus retailers who sell others' goods, they should refer kings to a supreme or self-ruling science, leaving the subordinate commanders to be classified separately. The inquiry is about the ruler, not about those who merely transmit the ruler's will.
D: Refashioned: The king must not be confused with the many officials who exercise delegated authority -- heralds, interpreters, military officers, prophets. These all command, but on someone else's behalf. The class of genuine, sovereign rulers is so rarely isolated in thought that it barely has a name. Yet this is precisely the class the inquiry seeks: those who command from their own knowledge and on their own authority. Everything else -- the entire apparatus of delegated command -- can be set aside.
D: Elegant: The world is full of officials who command on someone else's behalf. The class of those who command from their own authority is so rare it barely has a name. That nameless class is what the inquiry seeks.
D: Summary: The Stranger distinguishes the king from all officials who exercise delegated authority. The true ruler belongs to a nearly nameless class: those who command from their own knowledge rather than relaying someone else's orders.
D: Distilled: True sovereignty is so rare that the class of those who genuinely rule from their own authority barely has a name.
Notes: The observation that 'self-ruling science' (autepitaktike) is 'almost nameless' reflects a recurring Platonic concern with the inadequacy of ordinary language to capture philosophical distinctions. In the Sophist (218c), the Stranger similarly notes the difficulty of naming things that common usage has not distinguished. The list of subordinate commanders -- heralds, interpreters, boatswains, prophets -- represents the typical command structure of a Greek city-state, where many officials exercised authority delegated from the assembly, the council, or the generals.

D: Original:
YOUNG SOCRATES: Very good.

STRANGER: Thus a very fair distinction has been attained between the man who gives his own commands, and him who gives another's. And now let us see if the supreme power allows of any further division.

YOUNG SOCRATES: By all means.

STRANGER: I think that it does; and please to assist me in making the division.

YOUNG SOCRATES: At what point?
D: Narrative: Young Socrates approves the distinction. The Stranger summarizes: a clear line has been drawn between the person who gives his own commands and the person who transmits another's. Now the question is whether the supreme power -- the self-commanding art -- permits any further division. Young Socrates is ready to proceed and asks where the next cut should be made.
D: Refashioned: With the distinction between sovereign and delegated command firmly established, the question becomes whether sovereign command itself can be divided further. The Stranger believes it can. The next cut in the analysis is about to be made.
D: Elegant: Sovereign command has been isolated from its many imitators. Now the question sharpens: can this supreme art itself be divided further?
D: Summary: Having separated sovereign command from delegated command, the Stranger prepares to divide sovereign command itself into further categories.
D: Distilled: Once you have isolated genuine authority from its many counterfeits, the real work of definition begins.
Notes: This brief transitional passage signals the move from Chapter 2 (The Art of Ruling) to Chapter 3 (The King as Herdsman), where the method of division will be applied to the objects over which the ruler commands rather than to the nature of command itself. The structure mirrors the Sophist, where each successful division opens onto a new question requiring further division.

D: Original:
STRANGER: May not all rulers be supposed to command for the sake of producing something?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly.

STRANGER: Nor is there any difficulty in dividing the things produced into two classes.

YOUNG SOCRATES: How would you divide them?

STRANGER: Of the whole class, some have life and some are without life.

YOUNG SOCRATES: True.

STRANGER: And by the help of this distinction we may make, if we please, a subdivision of the section of knowledge which commands.

YOUNG SOCRATES: At what point?

STRANGER: One part may be set over the production of lifeless, the other of living objects; and in this way the whole will be divided.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly.

STRANGER: That division, then, is complete; and now we may leave one half, and take up the other; which may also be divided into two.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Which of the two halves do you mean?

STRANGER: Of course that which exercises command about animals. For, surely, the royal science is not like that of a master-workman, a science presiding over lifeless objects;—the king has a nobler function, which is the management and control of living beings.

YOUNG SOCRATES: True.

STRANGER: And the breeding and tending of living beings may be observed to be sometimes a tending of the individual; in other cases, a common care of creatures in flocks?

YOUNG SOCRATES: True.

STRANGER: But the statesman is not a tender of individuals—not like the driver or groom of a single ox or horse; he is rather to be compared with the keeper of a drove of horses or oxen.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Yes, I see, thanks to you.

STRANGER: Shall we call this art of tending many animals together, the art of managing a herd, or the art of collective management?

YOUNG SOCRATES: No matter;—whichever suggests itself to us in the course of conversation.
D: Narrative:
The Stranger proposes the next principle of division: all rulers command for the sake of producing or managing something. The things they oversee can be divided into those that have life and those that are without life. This gives them two branches of directive knowledge: one governing the production of lifeless objects, the other governing living beings.

They set aside the lifeless branch immediately, since the royal science is clearly not like the art of a master-workman presiding over inert materials. The king has a nobler function: the management and control of living beings.

Within the management of living things, there is a further distinction: tending individuals versus tending groups. The statesman is not like a groom caring for a single horse; he is more like the keeper of a drove of horses or a herd of oxen. His art concerns the collective management of living creatures -- the management of herds. Young Socrates grasps the point, and the Stranger is satisfied with whatever name suggests itself for this art of tending many animals together.
D: Refashioned:
The next division concerns what the ruler's command is directed toward. All command aims at producing or managing something, and those objects divide into the living and the non-living.

The non-living side -- engineering, construction, manufacturing -- can be set aside immediately. The Statesman does not preside over inert materials. His domain is the management of living beings.

But not all management of living beings is the same. A groom tends a single horse. The Statesman, by contrast, manages groups -- herds, flocks, populations. His art is collective management, not individual care. The relevant analogy is not the horse-trainer but the herdsman: someone responsible for the welfare and direction of an entire group of living creatures.
D: Elegant: The ruler's domain is living beings, not lifeless materials. And within that domain, the Statesman is not the groom of a single horse but the herdsman of a flock -- his art is collective management, the direction of groups rather than individuals.
D: Summary: The Stranger divides the objects of command into living and non-living, placing the Statesman on the living side. He then distinguishes individual care from collective management, identifying the Statesman as a herdsman of groups rather than a tender of individuals.
D: Distilled: The Statesman's art is the collective management of living beings -- he is a herdsman, not a groom.
Notes: The herdsman analogy (poimenike) for political rule was widespread in Greek thought. Homer's standard epithet for Agamemnon is 'shepherd of the people' (poimen laon). The analogy will be central to this dialogue and will ultimately be subjected to serious criticism. The distinction between tending individuals and tending groups maps onto a broader Greek distinction between private arts (like medicine, which treats one patient at a time) and political arts (which manage populations). Xenophon's Cyropaedia and Memorabilia both develop the ruler-as-herdsman comparison extensively.

D: Original:
STRANGER: Very good, Socrates; and, if you continue to be not too particular about names, you will be all the richer in wisdom when you are an old man. And now, as you say, leaving the discussion of the name,—can you see a way in which a person, by showing the art of herding to be of two kinds, may cause that which is now sought amongst twice the number of things, to be then sought amongst half that number?

YOUNG SOCRATES: I will try;—there appears to me to be one management of men and another of beasts.

STRANGER: You have certainly divided them in a most straightforward and manly style; but you have fallen into an error which hereafter I think that we had better avoid.

YOUNG SOCRATES: What is the error?
D: Narrative: The Stranger commends Young Socrates for not being too particular about names -- a trait that will make him wiser in old age. Setting aside the question of terminology, the Stranger asks whether the art of herding can be divided into two kinds, which would narrow their search by half. Young Socrates tries: one kind of herding manages humans, the other manages beasts. The Stranger acknowledges this is a straightforward and confident division, but warns that Young Socrates has fallen into an error they should learn to avoid.
D: Refashioned: Young Socrates attempts the next division: herd management splits into the management of humans and the management of animals. The Stranger acknowledges the confidence of this move but flags it as an error. Dividing 'humans versus everything else' feels natural, but it is methodologically flawed. The Stranger will explain why -- and the explanation will reveal one of the most important principles of the entire method of division.
D: Elegant: Young Socrates' instinct is to divide herds into humans and animals. It feels obvious. But the obvious cut is often the wrong one, and the Stranger warns that this error contains a lesson more valuable than the answer itself.
D: Summary: Young Socrates divides herding into human management and animal management. The Stranger warns this seemingly natural division commits a methodological error that must be understood and avoided.
D: Distilled: The most obvious way to carve up a category is often the most misleading.
Notes: The Stranger's praise of Young Socrates for being 'not too particular about names' (me lian akribologoumenos peri ta onomata) reflects a pragmatic approach to philosophical inquiry: definitions matter more than labels. This contrasts with the Cratylus, where the relationship between names and things is explored at length. The error Young Socrates commits -- dividing by singling out one species and lumping everything else together -- will be diagnosed in the following passages as a violation of proper classificatory method.

D: Original: STRANGER: I think that we had better not cut off a single small portion which is not a species, from many larger portions; the part should be a species. To separate off at once the subject of investigation, is a most excellent plan, if only the separation be rightly made; and you were under the impression that you were right, because you saw that you would come to man; and this led you to hasten the steps. But you should not chip off too small a piece, my friend; the safer way is to cut through the middle; which is also the more likely way of finding classes. Attention to this principle makes all the difference in a process of enquiry.
D: Narrative: The Stranger explains the error. In dividing a class, one should not cut off a single small portion from many larger portions. The part separated must itself be a genuine species -- a natural kind, not merely a convenient label. Young Socrates divided by isolating humans and lumping all other animals together as 'beasts,' but this is like chipping off a small piece rather than cutting through the middle. The safer and more productive method is always to cut through the middle of a class, dividing it into roughly equal halves, because this is far more likely to reveal genuine natural kinds. Attention to this principle makes all the difference in the process of inquiry.
D: Refashioned:
The error is this: Young Socrates isolated one small group (humans) and lumped everything else together (all other animals). This is not a proper division -- it is just chipping off a piece. A valid division must cut a class into genuine species, not separate one fragment from an undifferentiated remainder.

The principle is: always divide through the middle. When you cut a category into roughly equal halves, you are far more likely to find natural kinds -- real joints in the structure of things. When you chip off one small piece because you already know where you want to end up, you skip over the real structure and arrive at your destination without understanding the terrain. This principle is what separates rigorous classification from mere labeling.
D: Elegant: Do not chip off a small piece and call the remainder a class. Cut through the middle. The goal of division is not to reach your answer quickly but to find the real joints in the structure of things. Haste in classification is a guarantee of error.
D: Summary: The Stranger articulates a core principle of proper division: always cut through the middle of a class into genuine species rather than chipping off one small portion and treating the remainder as a single kind. This yields natural classifications rather than artificial ones.
D: Distilled: The purpose of division is to find the natural joints in reality, and this requires cutting through the middle of categories rather than chipping off whatever piece you already want.
Notes: This passage articulates the key methodological principle of the Statesman: division must follow natural joints (kat' arthra), not convenience. The metaphor of 'cutting at the joints' appears explicitly in the Phaedrus (265e), where Socrates compares the dialectician to a butcher who carves along natural articulations rather than hacking through bone. The principle of 'dividing through the middle' (meson temnein) is an innovation of this dialogue -- the Sophist employed division but did not theorize about optimal cutting strategy. This passage has been influential in the philosophy of classification and natural kinds.

D: Original:
YOUNG SOCRATES: What do you mean, Stranger?

STRANGER: I will endeavour to speak more plainly out of love to your good parts, Socrates; and, although I cannot at present entirely explain myself, I will try, as we proceed, to make my meaning a little clearer.

YOUNG SOCRATES: What was the error of which, as you say, we were guilty in our recent division?
D: Narrative: Young Socrates asks the Stranger to explain more clearly. The Stranger promises to try, out of appreciation for Young Socrates' abilities, though he admits he cannot fully explain himself at present. The full meaning will become clearer as they proceed. Young Socrates presses: what exactly was the error in their recent division?
D: Refashioned: Young Socrates asks for clarification. The Stranger promises the full meaning will emerge as the inquiry continues -- the error will be easier to understand once they see what a correct division looks like by contrast.
D: Elegant: The principle will become clear through practice. Seeing what proper division looks like will illuminate why the earlier cut was wrong.
D: Summary: Young Socrates asks for further clarification of the error. The Stranger promises the principle will become clearer as the inquiry progresses and they practice correct division.
D: Distilled: Some principles of method can only be understood by watching them work in practice.
Notes: The Stranger's patience and pedagogical sensitivity here is notable. Rather than lecturing abstractly, he promises to demonstrate the principle through the inquiry itself -- a teaching method consistent with the Socratic tradition of learning through guided discovery rather than passive instruction. The phrase 'out of love to your good parts' (dia ten sen euphyian) suggests that philosophical aptitude is recognized through the quality of one's errors as much as through one's correct answers.

D: Original: STRANGER: The error was just as if some one who wanted to divide the human race, were to divide them after the fashion which prevails in this part of the world; here they cut off the Hellenes as one species, and all the other species of mankind, which are innumerable, and have no ties or common language, they include under the single name of 'barbarians,' and because they have one name they are supposed to be of one species also. Or suppose that in dividing numbers you were to cut off ten thousand from all the rest, and make of it one species, comprehending the rest under another separate name, you might say that here too was a single class, because you had given it a single name. Whereas you would make a much better and more equal and logical classification of numbers, if you divided them into odd and even; or of the human species, if you divided them into male and female; and only separated off Lydians or Phrygians, or any other tribe, and arrayed them against the rest of the world, when you could no longer make a division into parts which were also classes.
D: Narrative:
The Stranger illustrates the error with vivid examples. Suppose someone wanted to divide the human race and did so the way people do in Greece: Hellenes on one side, and all other peoples -- innumerable, with no shared language or customs -- lumped together as 'barbarians.' Because they share a single name, they are treated as a single species. But this is absurd. It would be like dividing all numbers by cutting off ten thousand as one class and grouping all the rest under a different name -- just because you gave them one label does not make them one kind.

A far better division of numbers would be into odd and even. A far better division of the human race would be into male and female. One should only separate off a specific group like the Lydians or Phrygians when no further division into genuine classes is possible -- when one has reached the level of actual species, not before.
D: Refashioned:
The Stranger gives the key example. Greeks divide humanity into 'Hellenes' and 'barbarians' -- one specific group on one side, and every other people on earth lumped together on the other. Because the remainder shares a single label ('barbarian'), it gets treated as a single kind. But sharing a name does not make something a species. You could divide all numbers by putting ten thousand on one side and everything else on the other, but that tells you nothing about the structure of numbers. The real division of numbers is odd versus even. The real division of the human race is male versus female.

The lesson: never separate off a particular group until you have exhausted all the genuine structural divisions. Isolating one species and calling the remainder a class is the fundamental error of lazy classification.
D: Elegant: Greeks call themselves 'Hellenes' and everyone else 'barbarians,' as if a shared label made countless unrelated peoples a single species. This is the error of lazy classification: treating a leftover pile as a natural kind. The real division of numbers is odd and even, not 'ten thousand and everything else.' The real division of humanity is male and female, not 'us and everyone else.'
D: Summary: The Stranger illustrates the error with the Greek division of humanity into Hellenes and barbarians: lumping unrelated peoples under one name does not make them a genuine class. Proper division follows structural features (like odd/even in numbers) rather than isolating one group and treating the remainder as a single kind.
D: Distilled: A shared label does not make a real class -- calling everyone who is not Greek a 'barbarian' tells you nothing about the actual structure of humanity.
Notes: The Hellene/barbarian example is philosophically significant: Plato is critiquing Greek ethnocentrism through the lens of logical method. This argument was later taken up directly by Aristotle in Politics I.2 and III.14. The Lydians were a people of western Anatolia (modern Turkey) whose last king, Croesus, was famous for his wealth and dealings with the Greeks. The Phrygians occupied central Anatolia and were known to the Greeks through mythology (Midas, Gordius) and through the cult of Cybele. The odd/even division of numbers was fundamental to Pythagorean mathematics and philosophy, where odd and even were considered basic principles of reality.

D: Original:
YOUNG SOCRATES: Very true; but I wish that this distinction between a part and a class could still be made somewhat plainer.

STRANGER: O Socrates, best of men, you are imposing upon me a very difficult task. We have already digressed further from our original intention than we ought, and you would have us wander still further away. But we must now return to our subject; and hereafter, when there is a leisure hour, we will follow up the other track; at the same time, I wish you to guard against imagining that you ever heard me declare—

YOUNG SOCRATES: What?

STRANGER: That a class and a part are distinct.

YOUNG SOCRATES: What did I hear, then?

STRANGER: That a class is necessarily a part, but there is no similar necessity that a part should be a class; that is the view which I should always wish you to attribute to me, Socrates.

YOUNG SOCRATES: So be it.

STRANGER: There is another thing which I should like to know.

YOUNG SOCRATES: What is it?

STRANGER: The point at which we digressed; for, if I am not mistaken, the exact place was at the question, Where you would divide the management of herds. To this you appeared rather too ready to answer that there were two species of animals; man being one, and all brutes making up the other.

YOUNG SOCRATES: True.

STRANGER: I thought that in taking away a part, you imagined that the remainder formed a class, because you were able to call them by the common name of brutes.

YOUNG SOCRATES: That again is true.

STRANGER: Suppose now, O most courageous of dialecticians, that some wise and understanding creature, such as a crane is reputed to be, were, in imitation of you, to make a similar division, and set up cranes against all other animals to their own special glorification, at the same time jumbling together all the others, including man, under the appellation of brutes,—here would be the sort of error which we must try to avoid.

YOUNG SOCRATES: How can we be safe?

STRANGER: If we do not divide the whole class of animals, we shall be less likely to fall into that error.

YOUNG SOCRATES: We had better not take the whole?

STRANGER: Yes, there lay the source of error in our former division.

YOUNG SOCRATES: How?
D: Narrative:
Young Socrates asks for the distinction between a part and a class to be made still plainer. The Stranger resists -- they have already digressed too far from the main inquiry and should not wander further. But he offers one crucial clarification: a class is necessarily a part, but a part is not necessarily a class. Having a name and being a portion of something does not automatically make that portion a genuine species. This is the principle Young Socrates should remember.

The Stranger then returns to where they went wrong. The question was how to divide the management of herds, and Young Socrates jumped too quickly to the answer that there were two species: humans and all brutes. Taking away 'humans' as a part, he assumed the remainder ('brutes') formed a genuine class simply because it had a common name. The Stranger illustrates the absurdity with a striking image: suppose a crane, being wise and proud, divided all animals into cranes and non-cranes, lumping humans in with every other creature under the label 'brutes.' The crane would be committing exactly the same error of classification that Young Socrates committed. To avoid this error, they must not divide the whole class of animals at once by singling out humans; the first division produced the source of their mistake.
D: Refashioned:
Young Socrates wants the distinction between a 'part' and a 'class' spelled out. The Stranger provides the essential principle: every class is a part, but not every part is a class. Just because you can carve off a piece and give it a name does not make it a genuine species.

This is exactly what went wrong. Young Socrates divided herding into 'human management' and 'animal management,' treating 'all other animals' as a single class just because they share the label 'brutes.' But imagine a crane doing the same thing: cranes on one side, everything else -- humans included -- on the other, all lumped together as 'brutes.' The crane would be just as wrong as the Greek who calls everyone a barbarian.

The fix: do not begin by singling out the group you are looking for. Divide the whole class at its natural joints, even if this means the target species does not emerge until several divisions later.
D: Elegant: Every class is a part, but not every part is a class. Imagine a crane dividing all animals into 'cranes' and 'non-cranes' -- humans lumped in with the rest as brutes. It would be committing exactly the same error as the human who separates 'humans' from 'all other animals.' The antidote is patience: divide at natural joints, even if the species you seek does not appear until the final cut.
D: Summary: The Stranger clarifies that not every part of a category is a genuine class. He illustrates the error with the image of a crane classifying all non-cranes as 'brutes,' showing that singling out one species and lumping the rest together is always methodologically wrong, no matter who does it.
D: Distilled: A crane dividing all animals into cranes and non-cranes would be committing the same error as a human who divides them into humans and brutes.
Notes: The crane example is one of Plato's most memorable illustrations and has been widely discussed in the history of philosophy and biology. It anticipates modern concerns about anthropocentrism in taxonomy. The part/class (meros/eidos) distinction is a significant contribution to the theory of classification: Plato is arguing that a legitimate taxonomic group must be defined by shared positive features, not merely by exclusion from another group. This principle influenced Aristotle's biological classifications in the Parts of Animals and History of Animals. The Stranger's reluctance to pursue the theoretical point further -- 'we have already digressed further than we ought' -- reflects the dialogue's self-conscious attention to methodological pacing.

D: Original:
STRANGER: You remember how that part of the art of knowledge which was concerned with command, had to do with the rearing of living creatures,—I mean, with animals in herds?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Yes.

STRANGER: In that case, there was already implied a division of all animals into tame and wild; those whose nature can be tamed are called tame, and those which cannot be tamed are called wild.

YOUNG SOCRATES: True.

STRANGER: And the political science of which we are in search, is and ever was concerned with tame animals, and is also confined to gregarious animals.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Yes.

STRANGER: But then we ought not to divide, as we did, taking the whole class at once. Neither let us be in too great haste to arrive quickly at the political science; for this mistake has already brought upon us the misfortune of which the proverb speaks.

YOUNG SOCRATES: What misfortune?

STRANGER: The misfortune of too much haste, which is too little speed.

YOUNG SOCRATES: And all the better, Stranger;—we got what we deserved.

STRANGER: Very well: Let us then begin again, and endeavour to divide the collective rearing of animals; for probably the completion of the argument will best show what you are so anxious to know. Tell me, then—

YOUNG SOCRATES: What?

STRANGER: Have you ever heard, as you very likely may—for I do not suppose that you ever actually visited them—of the preserves of fishes in the Nile, and in the ponds of the Great King; or you may have seen similar preserves in wells at home?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Yes, to be sure, I have seen them, and I have often heard the others described.

STRANGER: And you may have heard also, and may have been assured by report, although you have not travelled in those regions, of nurseries of geese and cranes in the plains of Thessaly?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly.

STRANGER: I asked you, because here is a new division of the management of herds, into the management of land and of water herds.

YOUNG SOCRATES: There is.

STRANGER: And do you agree that we ought to divide the collective rearing of herds into two corresponding parts, the one the rearing of water, and the other the rearing of land herds?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Yes.

STRANGER: There is surely no need to ask which of these two contains the royal art, for it is evident to everybody.
D: Narrative:
The Stranger begins the corrected division. Returning to their earlier point -- that the commanding branch of knowledge concerned the rearing of living creatures in herds -- the Stranger notes that a prior division was already implicit: animals divide into tame and wild. Those whose nature can be tamed are called tame; those that cannot be tamed are called wild. Political science concerns itself with tame animals, and specifically with tame gregarious animals.

But this time they must not rush. They should not divide by taking the whole class at once. Haste brought the misfortune mentioned in the proverb: too much haste is too little speed. Young Socrates accepts the rebuke cheerfully.

The Stranger starts fresh with the collective rearing of tame gregarious animals and introduces a new division: water herds versus land herds. The examples are vivid -- fish preserves in the Nile, ponds of the Great King of Persia, goose and crane nurseries in the plains of Thessaly. These all represent the management of water-dwelling herds. But the royal art obviously belongs to the management of land herds, not water herds. No one needs to be told on which side the Statesman falls.
D: Refashioned:
The Stranger begins the corrected division, this time following the method properly.

First, animals divide into tame and wild. Political science concerns only tame animals -- and specifically tame animals that live in groups. Now the task is to subdivide this category without rushing to isolate humans.

The first cut: water herds versus land herds. Fish farms in the Nile, the royal ponds of the Persian King, goose and crane nurseries on the Thessalian plains -- all of these represent the management of water-dwelling herds. The Statesman obviously belongs to the management of land herds. That much requires no argument.
D: Elegant: Tame, gregarious animals divide into water herds and land herds. Fish farms, goose nurseries, and royal ponds fall on one side. The Statesman's domain falls on the other. This much is obvious -- the real work of division lies ahead.
D: Summary: The Stranger restarts the division properly: tame gregarious animals divide first into water herds and land herds. The Statesman clearly belongs to the management of land herds. The Stranger emphasizes proceeding carefully rather than rushing to the answer.
D: Distilled: Haste in classification is too much speed and too little progress -- the only way forward is patient, systematic division.
Notes: The Persian King's fish preserves and animal parks (paradeisoi, from which we get the word 'paradise') were well known to the Greeks through Xenophon's accounts in the Anabasis and Cyropaedia. The Thessalian plains in northern Greece were famous for horse-breeding and agriculture. The proverb 'more haste, less speed' (speude bradeos) was proverbial in Greek -- it was later attributed to Augustus Caesar as 'festina lente.' The division into tame and wild (hemera/agria) maps onto the Greek cultural distinction between civilization and nature, between the polis and the wild.

D: Original:
YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly.

STRANGER: Any one can divide the herds which feed on dry land?

YOUNG SOCRATES: How would you divide them?

STRANGER: I should distinguish between those which fly and those which walk.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Most true.

STRANGER: And where shall we look for the political animal? Might not an idiot, so to speak, know that he is a pedestrian?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly.

STRANGER: The art of managing the walking animal has to be further divided, just as you might halve an even number.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Clearly.

STRANGER: Let me note that here appear in view two ways to that part or class which the argument aims at reaching,—the one a speedier way, which cuts off a small portion and leaves a large; the other agrees better with the principle which we were laying down, that as far as we can we should divide in the middle; but it is longer. We can take either of them, whichever we please.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Cannot we have both ways?

STRANGER: Together? What a thing to ask! but, if you take them in turn, you clearly may.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Then I should like to have them in turn.

STRANGER: There will be no difficulty, as we are near the end; if we had been at the beginning, or in the middle, I should have demurred to your request; but now, in accordance with your desire, let us begin with the longer way; while we are fresh, we shall get on better. And now attend to the division.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Let me hear.

STRANGER: The tame walking herding animals are distributed by nature into two classes.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Upon what principle?

STRANGER: The one grows horns; and the other is without horns.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Clearly.

STRANGER: Suppose that you divide the science which manages pedestrian animals into two corresponding parts, and define them; for if you try to invent names for them, you will find the intricacy too great.

YOUNG SOCRATES: How must I speak of them, then?

STRANGER: In this way: let the science of managing pedestrian animals be divided into two parts, and one part assigned to the horned herd, and the other to the herd that has no horns.

YOUNG SOCRATES: All that you say has been abundantly proved, and may therefore be assumed.

STRANGER: The king is clearly the shepherd of a polled herd, who have no horns.
D: Narrative:
The division of land herds continues. The Stranger divides them into those that fly and those that walk. The political animal is clearly a pedestrian -- a walker, not a flyer. Even a fool would know that.

Now the art of managing walking animals must be divided further, as one might halve an even number. The Stranger notes that two paths appear: one shorter, cutting off a small piece and leaving a large remainder, and one longer, which better follows the principle of dividing through the middle. Young Socrates wants to try both, and the Stranger agrees to take them in turn, starting with the longer, more methodical route.

The tame walking herding animals divide into those that grow horns and those without horns. Rather than inventing complex names, they simply assign one branch of management to horned herds and the other to hornless herds. The king is clearly the shepherd of the hornless, polled herd.
D: Refashioned:
Land herds divide into flyers and walkers. The Statesman governs walkers -- no argument needed.

Walking animals divide into horned and hornless. Two analytical paths present themselves: a short cut that would isolate humans quickly, or the longer route that divides through the middle as proper method requires. The Stranger takes the longer route.

Horned herds include cattle, goats, and deer. The Statesman governs the hornless herd. The classification narrows steadily: from living things to tame animals, to gregarious tame animals, to land herds, to walkers, to the hornless among them.
D: Elegant: From all living things down to tame gregarious land-walking hornless animals -- each cut narrows the field by half and follows the natural joints. Two paths were available: the quick shortcut and the methodical route. The Stranger chose method over speed, because proper classification is its own reward.
D: Summary: The Stranger continues dividing: land animals split into flyers and walkers, then walkers into horned and hornless. Following proper method, the longer and more systematic path is chosen over the shortcut. The Statesman is identified as the shepherd of hornless herds.
D: Distilled: Each proper division halves the field and follows a natural joint, steadily narrowing the classification from all living things toward the Statesman's specific domain.
Notes: The two paths -- the shorter (cutting off a small piece) and the longer (dividing through the middle) -- illustrate the Stranger's methodological principle in action. He deliberately chooses the longer route to model proper technique, even when a shortcut would reach the same result. The distinction between horned (kerata echon) and hornless (akeraton) animals was a standard Greek zoological classification. The term 'polled' (kolobon) refers specifically to animals naturally lacking horns. This taxonomic approach anticipates Aristotle's biological classifications in the Parts of Animals, where horns are among the distinguishing features discussed.

D: Original:
YOUNG SOCRATES: That is evident.

STRANGER: Shall we break up this hornless herd into sections, and endeavour to assign to him what is his?

YOUNG SOCRATES: By all means.

STRANGER: Shall we distinguish them by their having or not having cloven feet, or by their mixing or not mixing the breed? You know what I mean.

YOUNG SOCRATES: What?

STRANGER: I mean that horses and asses naturally breed from one another.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Yes.

STRANGER: But the remainder of the hornless herd of tame animals will not mix the breed.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Very true.

STRANGER: And of which has the Statesman charge,—of the mixed or of the unmixed race?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Clearly of the unmixed.

STRANGER: I suppose that we must divide this again as before.

YOUNG SOCRATES: We must.

STRANGER: Every tame and herding animal has now been split up, with the exception of two species; for I hardly think that dogs should be reckoned among gregarious animals.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly not; but how shall we divide the two remaining species?

STRANGER: There is a measure of difference which may be appropriately employed by you and Theaetetus, who are students of geometry.

YOUNG SOCRATES: What is that?

STRANGER: The diameter; and, again, the diameter of a diameter. (Compare Meno.)

YOUNG SOCRATES: What do you mean?

STRANGER: How does man walk, but as a diameter whose power is two feet?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Just so.

STRANGER: And the power of the remaining kind, being the power of twice two feet, may be said to be the diameter of our diameter.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly; and now I think that I pretty nearly understand you.

STRANGER: In these divisions, Socrates, I descry what would make another famous jest.

YOUNG SOCRATES: What is it?

STRANGER: Human beings have come out in the same class with the freest and airiest of creation, and have been running a race with them.

YOUNG SOCRATES: I remark that very singular coincidence.

STRANGER: And would you not expect the slowest to arrive last?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Indeed I should.
D: Narrative:
The hornless herd must be further divided. The Stranger considers several possible bases for division: cloven-footed versus non-cloven, or mixing breeds versus keeping breeds pure. He explains the second option: horses and asses naturally breed with one another, producing mules, while the rest of the hornless tame herd does not interbreed. The Statesman has charge of the unmixed, pure-bred race.

This group must be divided once more. Nearly every tame gregarious animal has now been accounted for, with only two species remaining -- dogs being excluded since they are not truly gregarious. To distinguish the final two, the Stranger reaches for a mathematical metaphor, fitting for Young Socrates the geometry student: man walks as a biped, whose power is two feet -- like a diagonal whose square is two. The remaining kind walks on four feet -- whose power is twice two, like the diagonal of the diagonal.

The Stranger observes with amusement that in this classification, human beings have ended up running in the same class as the freest and airiest of creatures. And one would expect the slowest runner to arrive last in any race.
D: Refashioned:
The hornless herd divides again. Some hornless animals interbreed across species -- horses and asses produce mules. The remainder keep to their own kind. The Statesman governs the pure-bred, non-interbreeding group.

Only two species remain, and the Stranger uses a geometrical joke to distinguish them, playing to Young Socrates' mathematical training. Humans are bipeds -- their 'power' is two feet, like a square root of two. The other remaining species are quadrupeds -- their power is the square root of four. Humans, it turns out, have been classified alongside birds: both are bipeds, both featherless, running in the same taxonomic lane. The Stranger finds this amusing -- the king has ended up sharing a classification with the most nimble creatures in nature.
D: Elegant: Through patient division, humans and birds land in the same class: both bipeds among the hornless, pure-bred, tame, gregarious, walking, land-dwelling animals. The king finds himself racing alongside the bird-catcher. This is what happens when you follow the logic of classification honestly -- it does not flatter human dignity.
D: Summary: Further division separates interbreeding animals from pure-bred ones, then uses a mathematical distinction between bipeds and quadrupeds. Humans end up classified alongside birds as bipeds, producing the comic result that the king shares a category with the bird-catcher.
D: Distilled: Honest classification does not flatter -- follow the logic far enough and humans end up in the same category as birds.
Notes: The mathematical metaphor uses the concept of 'dunamis' (power/square) from Greek geometry. The diagonal of a unit square has a length of the square root of 2 (an irrational number), which was a famous discovery of the Pythagorean school and reportedly caused a philosophical crisis. The reference to the Meno connects to that dialogue's geometric demonstration (82b-85b) where Socrates uses the diagonal of a square to illustrate the theory of recollection. The joke about humans and birds sharing a classification anticipates Diogenes the Cynic's famous response to Plato's definition of man as a 'featherless biped': Diogenes reportedly plucked a chicken and declared 'Here is Plato's man!' This anecdote, reported by Diogenes Laertius (VI.40), may be a direct response to this passage.

D: Original: STRANGER: And there is a still more ridiculous consequence, that the king is found running about with the herd and in close competition with the bird-catcher, who of all mankind is most of an adept at the airy life. (Plato is here introducing a new subdivision, i.e. that of bipeds into men and birds. Others however refer the passage to the division into quadrupeds and bipeds, making pigs compete with human beings and the pig-driver with the king. According to this explanation we must translate the words above, 'freest and airiest of creation,' 'worthiest and laziest of creation.')
D: Narrative: The Stranger continues the joke: the result is even more ridiculous than it appears. The king is found running alongside the herd, in close competition with the bird-catcher -- who of all people is most expert at the airy, birdlike life. The classification has produced an absurd pairing, placing the ruler of humanity in the same class as the trapper of birds.
D: Refashioned: The absurdity deepens. Through this chain of divisions, the king has ended up classified alongside the bird-catcher -- the person whose entire expertise is catching the very creatures humans were just grouped with. The method of division does not care about dignity or social rank; it follows the logical structure of categories wherever it leads, even to comically deflating results.
D: Elegant: The king ends up racing the bird-catcher. The method of division has no respect for human vanity -- it classifies by structure, not by status.
D: Summary: The chain of divisions produces the comic result of classifying the king alongside the bird-catcher, illustrating that the method of division follows logical structure rather than social importance.
D: Distilled: Rigorous classification follows structural logic, not human vanity -- which is why the king ends up next to the bird-catcher.
Notes: The parenthetical editorial note in the original text reflects an ancient scholarly debate about whether this passage refers to the biped/bird classification (placing the king alongside the bird-catcher) or to an alternative reading involving quadrupeds (placing the king alongside the pig-driver). The bird-catcher (ixeutes) interpretation is generally preferred by modern scholars. The humor serves a philosophical purpose: it demonstrates that the method of division is objective and impersonal, a point the Stranger will make explicit in the next passage.

D: Original:
YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly.

STRANGER: Then here, Socrates, is still clearer evidence of the truth of what was said in the enquiry about the Sophist? (Compare Sophist.)

YOUNG SOCRATES: What?

STRANGER: That the dialectical method is no respecter of persons, and does not set the great above the small, but always arrives in her own way at the truest result.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Clearly.

STRANGER: And now, I will not wait for you to ask, but will of my own accord take you by the shorter road to the definition of a king.

YOUNG SOCRATES: By all means.
D: Narrative:
Young Socrates asks for the lesson. The Stranger draws it out: this result demonstrates a principle already established in the inquiry about the Sophist -- the dialectical method is no respecter of persons. It does not elevate the great above the small but always arrives at the truest result in its own way, regardless of what is flattering or convenient.

With the principle affirmed, the Stranger offers to take Young Socrates by the shorter road to the definition of the king, without waiting to be asked. They have learned the methodological lesson; now they can use the shortcut without falling into the error it previously would have caused.
D: Refashioned:
The lesson the Stranger draws from this comic result: the dialectical method does not care about prestige. It treats the king and the bird-catcher with the same logical rigor, arriving at truth regardless of what flatters human self-importance. This principle was already demonstrated in the Sophist, and it holds here too.

Having made the methodological point, the Stranger now offers to take the shorter path to defining the king. The detour through the longer route was never about reaching the destination faster -- it was about learning how to divide properly. Now that the lesson is internalized, they can use the shortcut without committing the error it would have produced before.
D: Elegant: The dialectical method is no respecter of persons. It places the king beside the bird-catcher with the same indifference it would show to any other logical result. Having learned this lesson through the long route, they can now take the short one -- not because the detour was wasted, but because it taught them how to cut properly.
D: Summary: The Stranger draws the methodological lesson: dialectical division follows logical structure impartially, without regard to social status. Having demonstrated this through the longer route, he now takes the shorter path to the definition, since the lesson about proper division has been learned.
D: Distilled: The dialectical method is no respecter of persons -- it follows the logic of classification wherever it leads, indifferent to what flatters human importance.
Notes: The reference to the Sophist confirms the dramatic continuity between the dialogues. In the Sophist (227a-b), the Stranger made a similar point about the method of division treating noble and ignoble arts with equal seriousness. The phrase 'no respecter of persons' anticipates a concept that became important in Jewish and Christian theology (prosopolepsia in the New Testament), though Plato's point is purely methodological. The decision to now take the 'shorter road' shows that the methodological detour served a pedagogical purpose: the long route taught Young Socrates how to divide properly, making the shortcut safe to use.

D: Original: STRANGER: I say that we should have begun at first by dividing land animals into biped and quadruped; and since the winged herd, and that alone, comes out in the same class with man, we should divide bipeds into those which have feathers and those which have not, and when they have been divided, and the art of the management of mankind is brought to light, the time will have come to produce our Statesman and ruler, and set him like a charioteer in his place, and hand over to him the reins of state, for that too is a vocation which belongs to him.
D: Narrative: The Stranger now takes the shorter route. They should have begun by dividing land animals into bipeds and quadrupeds. Since only the winged herd (birds) shares the biped class with humans, they then divide bipeds into feathered and featherless. This immediately isolates the management of humankind. Once this art is brought to light, the time has come to install the Statesman in his place, like a charioteer receiving the reins of state -- for this vocation belongs to him.
