D: Refashioned: The shorter route makes the final definition efficient. Land animals divide into bipeds and quadrupeds. Among bipeds, the feathered (birds) separate from the featherless (humans). This isolates the management of humankind as a distinct art. The Statesman can now be installed in his position -- like a charioteer taking the reins -- as the person whose knowledge entitles him to direct human affairs.
D: Elegant: Bipeds divide into feathered and featherless. Once birds are separated from humans, the art of managing humankind stands alone. The Statesman takes the reins like a charioteer -- his knowledge is his claim to authority.
D: Summary: Taking the shorter route, the Stranger divides bipeds into feathered and featherless, cleanly isolating the management of humankind. The Statesman is compared to a charioteer receiving the reins of state.
D: Distilled: Statesmanship is the art of managing featherless bipeds -- which is to say, the science of directing human collective life.
Notes: The charioteer metaphor connects to the famous chariot allegory in the Phaedrus (246a-254e), where the soul is compared to a charioteer driving two horses. The 'featherless biped' definition became one of the most discussed (and mocked) definitions in the history of philosophy. Diogenes Laertius (VI.40) reports that Diogenes the Cynic responded to this definition by plucking a chicken and presenting it as 'Plato's man,' leading to the amended definition 'featherless biped with flat nails.' The quick division demonstrates what the longer route taught: how to cut at natural joints efficiently.

D: Original:
YOUNG SOCRATES: Very good; you have paid me the debt,—I mean, that you have completed the argument, and I suppose that you added the digression by way of interest. (Compare Republic.)

STRANGER: Then now, let us go back to the beginning, and join the links, which together make the definition of the name of the Statesman's art.

YOUNG SOCRATES: By all means.
D: Narrative: Young Socrates is satisfied: the Stranger has paid his debt and completed the argument. He jokes that the earlier digression on method was like interest added to the principal. The Stranger accepts the compliment but redirects: now they should go back to the beginning and assemble the full chain of divisions into a single connected definition of the Statesman's art.
D: Refashioned: The argument appears complete, and Young Socrates is pleased -- he jokes that the methodological detour was like interest on a debt. But the Stranger wants to consolidate. Before moving forward, they should retrace the entire chain of divisions from beginning to end, linking every step into a single continuous definition.
D: Elegant: The definition has been reached through a long chain of cuts. Now the links must be joined together into one continuous statement -- the full definition of the Statesman's art, from first principle to final distinction.
D: Summary: With the division complete, the Stranger proposes going back to the beginning and assembling all the divisions into a single connected definition of the Statesman's art.
D: Distilled: A definition is not complete until every step in the chain of divisions has been joined into a single continuous statement.
Notes: Young Socrates' joke about 'interest' (tokos) on a 'debt' reflects the commercial metaphors common in Greek philosophical discourse. The term tokos literally means 'offspring' and was the standard Greek word for interest on a loan. The reference to the Republic in the editorial note connects to Republic 506e-507a, where Socrates similarly discusses paying intellectual debts. The practice of summarizing a chain of divisions into a synthetic definition is standard in Platonic dialectic -- the Sophist (268c-d) concludes with a similar summary.

D: Original: STRANGER: The science of pure knowledge had, as we said originally, a part which was the science of rule or command, and from this was derived another part, which was called command-for-self, on the analogy of selling-for-self; an important section of this was the management of living animals, and this again was further limited to the management of them in herds; and again in herds of pedestrian animals. The chief division of the latter was the art of managing pedestrian animals which are without horns; this again has a part which can only be comprehended under one term by joining together three names—shepherding pure-bred animals. The only further subdivision is the art of man-herding,—this has to do with bipeds, and is what we were seeking after, and have now found, being at once the royal and political.
D: Narrative: The Stranger recites the complete chain. The science of pure knowledge had a part concerned with command. From this derived the art of command-for-self, as distinct from the relay of others' commands. An important section of self-command was the management of living animals, which was further limited to the management of herds. Within herds, the focus narrowed to pedestrian animals, then to those without horns, then to pure-bred animals that do not cross-breed. The only further subdivision is the art of man-herding -- the management of bipeds. This is what they have been seeking, and it turns out to be at once the royal and political art.
D: Refashioned: The full definition, assembled link by link: Begin with all knowledge. Divide it into theoretical and practical. Within theoretical knowledge, divide into judging and commanding. Within commanding, divide into delegated and sovereign command. Sovereign command over living beings divides from command over lifeless objects. Living beings divide into individual care and herd management. Herds divide into water and land. Land herds divide into flyers and walkers. Walkers divide into horned and hornless. Hornless divide into cross-breeding and pure-bred. Pure-bred divide into quadrupeds and bipeds. The management of bipeds -- humans -- is the art of the Statesman. This is the royal and political science.
D: Elegant: Knowledge divides into theory and practice, theory into judgment and command, command into delegated and sovereign, sovereign into dominion over the living and the lifeless, the living into individuals and herds, herds into water and land, land into flying and walking, walking into horned and hornless, hornless into cross-bred and pure, pure into four-footed and two-footed. At the end of this chain stands the Statesman: the sovereign commander of the human herd.
D: Summary: The complete chain of divisions: from all knowledge through theoretical, commanding, sovereign, living, herding, land, walking, hornless, pure-bred, to biped -- arriving at the art of managing humankind, which is the royal and political science.
D: Distilled: The Statesman is defined as the sovereign intellectual commander of the collective life of featherless, pure-bred, hornless, walking, land-dwelling, tame, gregarious, living beings.
Notes: This summary recapitulates every division made since 2.1, compressing approximately twelve binary divisions into a single synthetic definition. The technique of recapitulation is characteristic of Platonic method -- the Sophist (268c-d) ends with a similar chain. The compound definition 'shepherding pure-bred animals' (eilikrinous zootrophia) uses a term (eilikrines) meaning 'pure, unmixed, genuine' that has philosophical resonance in Plato -- it appears in the Phaedo (66a) describing the soul's desire for pure knowledge unmixed with bodily influence. The identification of the royal art with the political art reaffirms the premise from 2.1.

D: Original:
YOUNG SOCRATES: To be sure.

STRANGER: And do you think, Socrates, that we really have done as you say?

YOUNG SOCRATES: What?

STRANGER: Do you think, I mean, that we have really fulfilled our intention?—There has been a sort of discussion, and yet the investigation seems to me not to be perfectly worked out: this is where the enquiry fails.

YOUNG SOCRATES: I do not understand.

STRANGER: I will try to make the thought, which is at this moment present in my mind, clearer to us both.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Let me hear.

STRANGER: There were many arts of shepherding, and one of them was the political, which had the charge of one particular herd?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Yes.

STRANGER: And this the argument defined to be the art of rearing, not horses or other brutes, but the art of rearing man collectively?

YOUNG SOCRATES: True.

STRANGER: Note, however, a difference which distinguishes the king from all other shepherds.

YOUNG SOCRATES: To what do you refer?

STRANGER: I want to ask, whether any one of the other herdsmen has a rival who professes and claims to share with him in the management of the herd?

YOUNG SOCRATES: What do you mean?

STRANGER: I mean to say that merchants, husbandmen, providers of food, and also training-masters and physicians, will all contend with the herdsmen of humanity, whom we call Statesmen, declaring that they themselves have the care of rearing or managing mankind, and that they rear not only the common herd, but also the rulers themselves.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Are they not right in saying so?
D: Narrative:
Young Socrates thinks the investigation is complete. But the Stranger is not satisfied. He suspects they have described a rough outline of the royal form but have not yet worked out the true image of the Statesman in his own nature. The definition seems adequate on the surface, but something essential is missing.

The Stranger identifies the problem. The argument defined the Statesman as one type of shepherd among many -- the one who rears humans collectively rather than horses or cattle. But here is what distinguishes the king from all other shepherds: no rival challenges the cattle-herder's claim to be the sole manager of his herd. Yet the Statesman faces countless rivals. Merchants, farmers, food-providers, trainers, and physicians all claim to rear and manage humankind. These claimants insist they care not only for the common populace but for the rulers themselves. Are they not right to make this claim?
D: Refashioned:
Young Socrates thinks they have finished, but the Stranger sees a fundamental problem. The definition identifies the Statesman as the shepherd of the human herd -- but unlike every other herdsman, the Statesman has rivals.

No one challenges the cattle-herder's exclusive claim over his herd. But the moment you call the Statesman a 'shepherd of humans,' a crowd of competitors appears: merchants who supply the people's goods, farmers who grow their food, doctors who tend their health, trainers who develop their bodies, teachers who shape their minds. Every one of these professions claims to manage human welfare. Some even claim to care for the rulers themselves. If the Statesman is merely the herdsman of humanity, how does he differ from all these other providers and caretakers?
D: Elegant: No one disputes the cattle-herder's authority over his herd. But call the Statesman a shepherd of humans, and every merchant, farmer, doctor, and teacher steps forward to claim the same title. The definition has rivals, which means it is not yet precise enough.
D: Summary: The Stranger identifies a fundamental flaw in the definition: unlike other herdsmen, the Statesman faces rivals. Merchants, farmers, doctors, and teachers all claim to manage human welfare, making the 'shepherd of humanity' definition too broad to isolate the Statesman's unique role.
D: Distilled: A definition that cannot exclude its rivals has not yet found what it is looking for.
Notes: This passage marks the critical turning point of the dialogue's first movement. The herdsman analogy, which has structured the entire inquiry so far, is now revealed to be inadequate. The list of rivals -- merchants (emporoi), farmers (georgoi), food-providers (sitopoinoi), trainers (gymnastai), and physicians (iatroi) -- represents the major functional roles in a Greek city-state. The objection anticipates Aristotle's analysis in Politics III.4-5 of the relationship between the statesman and the various experts who contribute to civic life. It also connects to the Republic's discussion of the division of labor in the just city (369b-374d).

D: Original: STRANGER: Very likely they may be, and we will consider their claim. But we are certain of this,—that no one will raise a similar claim as against the herdsman, who is allowed on all hands to be the sole and only feeder and physician of his herd; he is also their match-maker and accoucheur; no one else knows that department of science. And he is their merry-maker and musician, as far as their nature is susceptible of such influences, and no one can console and soothe his own herd better than he can, either with the natural tones of his voice or with instruments. And the same may be said of tenders of animals in general.
D: Narrative:
The Stranger sharpens the contrast. Consider any ordinary herdsman -- a shepherd of cattle, for instance. No one disputes his comprehensive authority over his herd. He is the sole feeder, physician, and matchmaker of his animals. He is their midwife, their musician, their comforter. He consoles them with his voice and his instruments as far as their nature permits. No one else claims any of these functions.

But the Statesman enjoys no such undisputed monopoly. Every one of these functions has its own specialist claiming authority over human beings. The shepherd analogy, which seemed to define the Statesman perfectly, actually obscures the crucial question: what exactly does the Statesman do that no one else can claim to do?
D: Refashioned:
The ordinary herdsman is the undisputed master of every aspect of his flock's existence. He feeds them, heals them, breeds them, delivers their young, entertains them, and soothes them. No one challenges his comprehensive authority.

The Statesman has no such monopoly. For every function the herdsman performs alone, there is a human specialist claiming jurisdiction: the doctor heals, the farmer feeds, the trainer conditions, the merchant supplies, the priest consoles. The shepherd analogy breaks down precisely because human societies have division of labor. The question the definition has failed to answer is: what does the Statesman do that all these specialists cannot?
D: Elegant: The shepherd feeds, heals, breeds, comforts, and entertains his flock with undisputed authority. The Statesman does none of these things exclusively -- each function has its own specialist. The herdsman analogy fails because human life is too complex for any one person to manage comprehensively.
D: Summary: The ordinary herdsman has undisputed authority over every aspect of his flock's care. The Statesman, by contrast, faces specialists who claim each of those functions -- feeding, healing, training, and comforting. The shepherd analogy breaks down because human societies involve division of labor.
D: Distilled: The herdsman analogy fails because no single person can monopolize the care of human beings the way a shepherd monopolizes the care of sheep.
Notes: The Stranger's description of the herdsman as feeder, physician, matchmaker, midwife (maiotikon), and musician draws on the realities of ancient pastoral life. Shepherds did in fact serve all these functions for their flocks. The recognition that the herdsman analogy fails for human governance represents a significant philosophical advance over the Homeric tradition of the 'shepherd of the people.' The argument from division of labor parallels the Republic's foundational argument for the city (369b-c): the city arises because no individual is self-sufficient, and each person does best at one specialized task. Here the same principle is turned against the analogy that was supposed to define the ruler.

D: Original:
YOUNG SOCRATES: Very true.

STRANGER: But if this is as you say, can our argument about the king be true and unimpeachable? Were we right in selecting him out of ten thousand other claimants to be the shepherd and rearer of the human flock?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Surely not.

STRANGER: Had we not reason just to now to apprehend, that although we may have described a sort of royal form, we have not as yet accurately worked out the true image of the Statesman? and that we cannot reveal him as he truly is in his own nature, until we have disengaged and separated him from those who hang about him and claim to share in his prerogatives?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Very true.

STRANGER: And that, Socrates, is what we must do, if we do not mean to bring disgrace upon the argument at its close.

YOUNG SOCRATES: We must certainly avoid that.

STRANGER: Then let us make a new beginning, and travel by a different road.

YOUNG SOCRATES: What road?

STRANGER: I think that we may have a little amusement; there is a famous tale, of which a good portion may with advantage be interwoven, and then we may resume our series of divisions, and proceed in the old path until we arrive at the desired summit. Shall we do as I say?

YOUNG SOCRATES: By all means.
D: Narrative:
Young Socrates concedes the point. The Stranger asks directly: can their argument about the king be true and unimpeachable? Were they right to select him from ten thousand rivals as the shepherd and rearer of the human flock? Surely not. They may have described a rough outline of the royal form, but they have not accurately worked out the true image of the Statesman. To reveal him as he truly is, they must disengage him from all those who crowd around him claiming to share his prerogatives. They cannot let the argument end in disgrace by leaving this problem unresolved.

Young Socrates agrees -- the work must continue. The Stranger proposes a new beginning: they will travel by a different road. He suggests introducing a famous tale, weaving a good portion of it into the argument, and then resuming their series of divisions to arrive at the definition they seek. The inquiry is far from over.
D: Refashioned:
The definition has failed its own test. Calling the Statesman a 'shepherd of humanity' does not distinguish him from the crowd of merchants, doctors, farmers, and teachers who all claim to manage human welfare. The argument cannot end here without disgracing itself.

The Stranger proposes starting over on a different road. Rather than continuing the chain of divisions directly, he will introduce a myth -- a famous tale that will illuminate what the shepherd analogy got wrong and point toward a better definition. The inquiry is far from finished; the real work of defining the Statesman has only just begun.
D: Elegant: The shepherd analogy has been tried and found insufficient. The Statesman cannot be defined by what he shares with every other caretaker of human beings -- he must be defined by what separates him from them all. A myth will illuminate where the divisions went wrong, and then the inquiry will begin again on firmer ground.
D: Summary: The Stranger acknowledges that the definition of the Statesman as a shepherd is insufficient because it cannot distinguish him from rival claimants. He proposes a new approach: a mythological tale that will illuminate the error, followed by a fresh series of divisions.
D: Distilled: When a definition cannot exclude its rivals, the inquiry must start over -- not by repeating the same steps, but by finding an entirely different road.
Notes: The 'famous tale' the Stranger is about to introduce is the Myth of Cronus (or the Cosmic Myth), which will occupy the next section of the dialogue. The decision to use myth at this point is methodologically significant: when the method of division reaches an impasse, Plato introduces narrative as a different kind of philosophical tool. This parallels the use of myths in the Republic (the allegory of the cave, the Allegory of Er), the Phaedrus (the chariot myth), and the Timaeus (the cosmological myth). The phrase 'ten thousand rivals' (myrioi amphisbetountes) uses the Greek word for an indefinitely large number, emphasizing the severity of the problem. The transition from division to myth marks the end of the dialogue's first major movement.

D: Original:
STRANGER: Listen, then, to a tale which a child would love to hear; and you are not too old for childish amusement.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Let me hear.

STRANGER: There did really happen, and will again happen, like many other events of which ancient tradition has preserved the record, the portent which is traditionally said to have occurred in the quarrel of Atreus and Thyestes. You have heard, no doubt, and remember what they say happened at that time?

YOUNG SOCRATES: I suppose you to mean the token of the birth of the golden lamb.

STRANGER: No, not that; but another part of the story, which tells how the sun and the stars once rose in the west, and set in the east, and that the god reversed their motion, and gave them that which they now have as a testimony to the right of Atreus.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Yes; there is that legend also.

STRANGER: Again, we have been often told of the reign of Cronos.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Yes, very often.

STRANGER: Did you ever hear that the men of former times were earth-born, and not begotten of one another?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Yes, that is another old tradition.

STRANGER: All these stories, and ten thousand others which are still more wonderful, have a common origin; many of them have been lost in the lapse of ages, or are repeated only in a disconnected form; but the origin of them is what no one has told, and may as well be told now; for the tale is suited to throw light on the nature of the king.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Very good; and I hope that you will give the whole story, and leave out nothing.

STRANGER: Listen, then. There is a time when God himself guides and helps to roll the world in its course; and there is a time, on the completion of a certain cycle, when he lets go, and the world being a living creature, and having originally received intelligence from its author and creator, turns about and by an inherent necessity revolves in the opposite direction.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Why is that?
D: Narrative:
The Stranger invited Young Socrates to listen to a tale that even a child would enjoy, assuring him he was not too old for such amusement. He began by recalling ancient traditions -- portents and legends that most people had heard in fragments but never connected. There was the story of Atreus and Thyestes, not the golden lamb, but the part where the sun and stars once rose in the west and set in the east, and the god reversed their motion. There was the reign of Cronus. There was the tradition that the earliest humans were earth-born rather than begotten.

All these stories, the Stranger explained, along with countless others now lost or garbled by time, share a single origin that no one has yet revealed. And this origin bears directly on the nature of the king they are trying to define. The cosmos, he said, alternates between two great phases. There is a time when God himself guides the world in its course, and a time when, having completed a certain cycle, he releases it. The world, being a living creature endowed with intelligence by its creator, then turns about by inherent necessity and revolves in the opposite direction.
D: Refashioned:
The Stranger shifts strategy. Rather than continuing to divide categories, he offers a myth -- one that connects several ancient legends most people know only in fragments.

The reversal of the sun's course in the story of Atreus and Thyestes, the golden age under Cronus, the tradition of humans born from the earth -- these are not separate myths. They share a single cosmological source that bears directly on the nature of political rule.

Here is the core claim: the universe alternates between two phases. In one, God actively guides the world's rotation. In the other, he lets go. The cosmos, being a living thing endowed with intelligence by its maker, then reverses direction on its own. The question of what kind of ruler humans need depends entirely on which phase they inhabit.
D: Elegant: Every golden age myth, every story of cosmic reversal, traces to one idea: the universe alternates between divine guidance and self-direction. The kind of ruler a people needs depends on which era they live in.
D: Summary: The Stranger introduces a myth connecting several ancient legends to a single cosmological principle. The universe alternates between periods of divine guidance and periods of autonomous self-direction, and this distinction is essential to understanding political rule.
D: Distilled: The cosmos cycles between divine governance and self-governance, and the nature of political authority depends on which era we inhabit.
Notes: Atreus and Thyestes -- Mythological brothers whose rivalry over the throne of Mycenae involved divine signs, including the reversal of the sun's course. The golden lamb and solar reversal are separate episodes in the myth cycle. -- The reign of Cronus (Kronos) corresponds to Hesiod's Golden Age in Works and Days (lines 109-120), where humans lived without toil or suffering under divine care. -- Earth-born (autochthonous) humans are a widespread Greek tradition, especially prominent in Athenian civic mythology (Athenians claimed autochthony as a mark of legitimacy). -- The cosmic reversal myth has parallels in Empedocles' cosmology, where the universe alternates between periods dominated by Love (unity) and Strife (separation). -- Plato's 'living cosmos' concept draws on the Timaeus, where the demiurge creates the world as a living creature with a soul.

D: Original: STRANGER: Why, because only the most divine things of all remain ever unchanged and the same, and body is not included in this class. Heaven and the universe, as we have termed them, although they have been endowed by the Creator with many glories, partake of a bodily nature, and therefore cannot be entirely free from perturbation. But their motion is, as far as possible, single and in the same place, and of the same kind; and is therefore only subject to a reversal, which is the least alteration possible. For the lord of all moving things is alone able to move of himself; and to think that he moves them at one time in one direction and at another time in another is blasphemy. Hence we must not say that the world is either self-moved always, or all made to go round by God in two opposite courses; or that two Gods, having opposite purposes, make it move round. But as I have already said (and this is the only remaining alternative) the world is guided at one time by an external power which is divine and receives fresh life and immortality from the renewing hand of the Creator, and again, when let go, moves spontaneously, being set free at such a time as to have, during infinite cycles of years, a reverse movement: this is due to its perfect balance, to its vast size, and to the fact that it turns on the smallest pivot.
D: Narrative: The Stranger explained why the cosmos reverses. Only the most divine things remain eternally unchanged, and the universe, for all its glories, partakes of a bodily nature and therefore cannot be entirely free from change. Its motion is as uniform and stable as possible, subject only to reversal -- the smallest alteration available to it. To say that God moves the world in opposite directions at different times would be blasphemy, and to posit two gods with opposing purposes is equally unacceptable. The only coherent account is this: the world is guided for a time by divine power, receiving fresh life and immortality, and then, when released, it moves spontaneously in the reverse direction. This is possible because of its perfect balance, its vast size, and the fact that it turns on the smallest possible pivot.
D: Refashioned:
Why does the cosmos reverse? Because only purely divine things are truly changeless, and the universe has a body. Anything with a physical nature cannot hold perfectly still forever. But the universe comes as close as possible -- its motion is the most uniform kind there is, and reversal is the smallest deviation available to it.

Three explanations are off the table. God does not push the world in opposite directions at different times -- that would make him inconsistent. Two gods with opposing agendas do not take turns -- that is mythological nonsense. And the world is not purely self-moving.

What actually happens: God guides the world for a time, renewing its life. Then he lets go. The cosmos, perfectly balanced and turning on the smallest possible axis, continues under its own momentum -- but in reverse.
D: Elegant: Only the purely divine is changeless. The cosmos has a body, so it cannot hold still forever. When God releases the helm, the world -- balanced on the finest pivot imaginable -- reverses course under its own weight.
D: Summary: The cosmos reverses because it has a physical nature and cannot remain eternally unchanged. God periodically guides it, then releases it, at which point its perfect balance allows it to rotate in the opposite direction on its own.
D: Distilled: The universe is too physical to be permanent and too well-made to be chaotic, so it oscillates between divine guidance and autonomous reversal.
Notes: The argument that only the purely incorporeal can be truly changeless reflects core Platonic metaphysics -- the Forms are eternal precisely because they are non-physical. -- The 'smallest pivot' image anticipates later Greek and medieval cosmological thinking about the axis mundi. -- The rejection of two opposing gods may target Empedocles' Love and Strife as cosmic principles, or possibly Zoroastrian dualism. -- The 'blasphemy' of attributing inconsistency to God reflects Plato's theological principle from Republic II (379a-383c) that God is perfectly good and unchanging.

D: Original:
YOUNG SOCRATES: Your account of the world seems to be very reasonable indeed.

STRANGER: Let us now reflect and try to gather from what has been said the nature of the phenomenon which we affirmed to be the cause of all these wonders. It is this.

YOUNG SOCRATES: What?

STRANGER: The reversal which takes place from time to time of the motion of the universe.

YOUNG SOCRATES: How is that the cause?

STRANGER: Of all changes of the heavenly motions, we may consider this to be the greatest and most complete.

YOUNG SOCRATES: I should imagine so.

STRANGER: And it may be supposed to result in the greatest changes to the human beings who are the inhabitants of the world at the time.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Such changes would naturally occur.

STRANGER: And animals, as we know, survive with difficulty great and serious changes of many different kinds when they come upon them at once.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Very true.

STRANGER: Hence there necessarily occurs a great destruction of them, which extends also to the life of man; few survivors of the race are left, and those who remain become the subjects of several novel and remarkable phenomena, and of one in particular, which takes place at the time when the transition is made to the cycle opposite to that in which we are now living.

YOUNG SOCRATES: What is it?
D: Narrative: Young Socrates found this account reasonable, and the Stranger pressed forward to identify the key phenomenon behind all these ancient wonders: the periodic reversal of the universe's motion. This reversal, being the greatest and most complete change in the heavens, naturally produces the most dramatic consequences for the world's inhabitants. Animals, as they both knew, survive great and sudden changes only with difficulty. When the cosmic reversal occurs, there is necessarily a vast destruction. Few survivors remain from the human race, and those who do become subject to extraordinary phenomena -- especially one that occurs at the moment of transition into the cycle opposite to the present one.
D: Refashioned:
The philosophical point behind the myth now comes into focus. The periodic reversal of cosmic motion is the single cause behind all the ancient legends. And because it is the most total change possible in the heavens, it produces the most catastrophic effects on earth.

Most living things cannot survive sudden, massive upheaval. When the universe reverses, the vast majority of humans and animals perish. The few who survive experience something unprecedented -- a phenomenon that occurs specifically at the moment of transition from one cosmic cycle to the other.
D: Elegant: The greatest change in heaven produces the greatest catastrophe on earth. When the cosmos reverses, nearly everything dies. What survives enters a world running backward.
D: Summary: The cosmic reversal is the single cause behind all the ancient legends of catastrophe and transformation. When the universe changes direction, most life is destroyed, and the survivors experience radical, unprecedented changes.
D: Distilled: Cosmic upheaval destroys most of what exists; what survives must adapt to a world whose fundamental direction has changed.
Notes: The idea of periodic cosmic catastrophe appears in Plato's Timaeus (22c-23d), where the Egyptian priest tells Solon that civilizations are periodically destroyed by flood and fire. -- The 'great destruction' motif connects to Greek flood narratives (Deucalion) and Near Eastern parallels (Gilgamesh, Genesis). -- The philosophical function of the catastrophe is to establish discontinuity between cosmic eras, explaining why humans in the current age have no direct memory of the previous one.

D: Original:
STRANGER: The life of all animals first came to a standstill, and the mortal nature ceased to be or look older, and was then reversed and grew young and delicate; the white locks of the aged darkened again, and the cheeks the bearded man became smooth, and recovered their former bloom; the bodies of youths in their prime grew softer and smaller, continually by day and night returning and becoming assimilated to the nature of a newly-born child in mind as well as body; in the succeeding stage they wasted away and wholly disappeared. And the bodies of those who died by violence at that time quickly passed through the like changes, and in a few days were no more seen.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Then how, Stranger, were the animals created in those days; and in what way were they begotten of one another?
D: Narrative:
The Stranger described the phenomenon. When the reversal occurs, the life of all animals first comes to a standstill, and then the process of aging reverses. The mortal nature ceases growing older and begins growing younger. White hair darkens again. The bearded man's cheeks become smooth and recover their former bloom. The bodies of those in their prime grow softer and smaller, day by day returning to the condition of a newborn child -- in mind as well as body. In the stages that follow, they waste away entirely and disappear. Even the bodies of those who had died by violence pass through the same reverse changes and quickly dissolve.

Young Socrates, struck by this image, asked how animals were created in that reversed era, and how they came into being if not through normal reproduction.
D: Refashioned:
Here is what happens when the cosmos reverses: aging runs backward. The old grow young again -- white hair darkens, wrinkled skin smooths, the body softens and shrinks. Day by day, people regress toward infancy, in mind as well as body, until they eventually dissolve and vanish entirely. Even corpses undergo the same reverse process and disappear.

This is not just a picturesque myth. It establishes a key premise: in the reversed cycle, normal biological reproduction does not exist. If everyone is aging backward toward nonexistence, how do new creatures come into being at all?
D: Elegant: When the cosmos reverses, so does life itself. The old grow young, the young dissolve into nothing. Death runs backward. Birth, as we know it, ceases to exist.
D: Summary: During the cosmic reversal, aging reverses -- the old become young, then infantile, then vanish entirely. Normal reproduction ceases, raising the question of how life originates in the reversed cycle.
D: Distilled: In a world running backward, aging reverses and biological reproduction becomes impossible -- life must originate from somewhere else entirely.
Notes: The reversal of aging is Plato's mythological device for establishing the earth-born tradition on cosmological grounds. -- The detail that mental regression accompanies physical regression ('in mind as well as body') is philosophically significant: it implies that rational capacity is tied to biological development, not independent of it. -- This passage has been compared to the myth of Benjamin Button and to Hindu cyclical cosmology (pralaya), though Plato's version serves a specific argumentative function about political authority.

D: Original:
STRANGER: It is evident, Socrates, that there was no such thing in the then order of nature as the procreation of animals from one another; the earth-born race, of which we hear in story, was the one which existed in those days—they rose again from the ground; and of this tradition, which is now-a-days often unduly discredited, our ancestors, who were nearest in point of time to the end of the last period and came into being at the beginning of this, are to us the heralds. And mark how consistent the sequel of the tale is; after the return of age to youth, follows the return of the dead, who are lying in the earth, to life; simultaneously with the reversal of the world the wheel of their generation has been turned back, and they are put together and rise and live in the opposite order, unless God has carried any of them away to some other lot. According to this tradition they of necessity sprang from the earth and have the name of earth-born, and so the above legend clings to them.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly that is quite consistent with what has preceded; but tell me, was the life which you said existed in the reign of Cronos in that cycle of the world, or in this? For the change in the course of the stars and the sun must have occurred in both.
D: Narrative:
The Stranger answered that in the reversed order of nature, there was no procreation of animals from one another. Instead, the earth-born race of ancient tradition was the one that existed -- people rose again from the ground. This tradition, which modern people too easily dismiss, was handed down by ancestors who lived nearest to the end of the last reversed period and came into being at the start of the current one. The logic was consistent: after the return of age to youth comes the return of the dead to life. As the world's rotation reverses, so does the wheel of generation. The dead are reconstituted and rise to live in the opposite order, unless God has carried some away to another fate. This is why they are called earth-born, and why the legend persists.

Young Socrates agreed this was consistent, then asked a crucial question: did the blessed life attributed to the reign of Cronus belong to the reversed cycle or to the present one? After all, the change in the course of the stars must have occurred in both.
D: Refashioned:
If reproduction runs backward, new life must come from the earth itself. The earth-born race of ancient tradition is not just a quaint story -- it is the logical consequence of the reversal. When the wheel of generation turns backward, the dead reconstitute and rise from the ground. Our ancestors, who lived closest to the transition between cycles, passed this account down. It has been 'unduly discredited' ever since.

The myth is internally consistent. Reversed aging leads to reversed birth leads to earth-born humans. But Young Socrates presses the crucial question: which cycle contained the golden age of Cronus? The blessed, effortless life -- did it belong to the era of divine guidance, or to our own era of self-direction?
D: Elegant: If aging runs backward, birth must too. The dead rise from the earth, not from mothers. The earth-born tradition is not myth -- it is the logical consequence of a reversed cosmos. The real question is: which cycle was the golden age?
D: Summary: In the reversed cycle, the dead rise from the earth rather than being born through reproduction, explaining the tradition of earth-born humans. The key question becomes whether the golden age under Cronus belonged to the divinely guided cycle or the present one.
D: Distilled: The earth-born tradition is the logical consequence of cosmic reversal: if generation runs backward, life must originate from the ground itself.
Notes: Autochthony (being earth-born) was a central Athenian civic myth -- Athenians claimed to have sprung directly from the soil of Attica, unlike other Greeks who migrated. Plato here gives this political myth a cosmological foundation. -- The 'wheel of generation' (kyklos geneseos) appears in Orphic and Pythagorean thought as the cycle of reincarnation. Plato repurposes it as a cosmic mechanism. -- Young Socrates' question about which cycle contains the golden age is philosophically sharp: it forces the Stranger to locate divine care in one era and human self-sufficiency in the other, which is the whole point of the myth for the definition of the statesman.

D: Original:
STRANGER: I see that you enter into my meaning;—no, that blessed and spontaneous life does not belong to the present cycle of the world, but to the previous one, in which God superintended the whole revolution of the universe; and the several parts the universe were distributed under the rule of certain inferior deities, as is the way in some places still. There were demigods, who were the shepherds of the various species and herds of animals, and each one was in all respects sufficient for those of whom he was the shepherd; neither was there any violence, or devouring of one another, or war or quarrel among them; and I might tell of ten thousand other blessings, which belonged to that dispensation. The reason why the life of man was, as tradition says, spontaneous, is as follows: In those days God himself was their shepherd, and ruled over them, just as man, who is by comparison a divine being, still rules over the lower animals. Under him there were no forms of government or separate possession of women and children; for all men rose again from the earth, having no memory of the past. And although they had nothing of this sort, the earth gave them fruits in abundance, which grew on trees and shrubs unbidden, and were not planted by the hand of man. And they dwelt naked, and mostly in the open air, for the temperature of their seasons was mild; and they had no beds, but lay on soft couches of grass, which grew plentifully out of the earth. Such was the life of man in the days of Cronos, Socrates; the character of our present life, which is said to be under Zeus, you know from your own experience. Can you, and will you, determine which of them you deem the happier?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Impossible.

STRANGER: Then shall I determine for you as well as I can?

YOUNG SOCRATES: By all means.
D: Narrative:
The Stranger confirmed that the blessed, spontaneous life belonged to the previous cycle, when God superintended the universe. In that era, the various parts of the world were distributed under the rule of lesser deities, each serving as shepherd to a particular species. Each divine shepherd was entirely sufficient for those in his care. There was no violence, no predation, no war or conflict.

The reason human life was spontaneous in that age is that God himself was their shepherd, ruling over humans just as humans -- who are by comparison divine beings -- now rule over lower animals. Under divine rule, there were no governments, no separate possession of women and children, for all humans rose from the earth with no memory of the past. The earth gave them fruit in abundance without cultivation. They lived naked in the open air, for the seasons were mild. They slept on soft grass that grew plentifully from the ground.

Such was life under Cronus. The present life, said to be under Zeus, Young Socrates knew from his own experience. The Stranger asked which era seemed happier, and when Young Socrates could not decide, offered to determine it for him.
D: Refashioned:
The golden age belonged to the previous cycle -- the one under direct divine management. God himself was the shepherd of humanity. Lesser deities governed each animal species, and each was perfectly sufficient for those in its care. There was no violence, no predation, no war.

Under divine rule, humans needed no government because they needed nothing at all. There was no private property, no family structure, no memory of the past -- everyone rose fresh from the earth. Food grew without cultivation. The climate was mild enough to live outdoors year-round. They slept on grass that sprang up on its own.

This was life under Cronus. Life under Zeus -- the current era -- requires no description. We live it. The question is: which is actually better? Young Socrates cannot say. The answer, the Stranger suggests, is not as obvious as it seems.
D: Elegant: Under Cronus, God was the shepherd. There was no government because there was no need -- no violence, no scarcity, no memory, no families. Food grew unbidden, the weather was always mild, and humans lived like favored animals in a divine enclosure. Whether that constitutes happiness is the real question.
D: Summary: The golden age under Cronus featured direct divine governance, with God as humanity's shepherd. There was no need for government, property, or agriculture because divine care provided everything. The Stranger raises the question of whether this effortless existence was truly happier than the present age of human self-direction.
D: Distilled: A life of perfect provision without freedom, effort, or self-determination may not be the paradise it appears.
Notes: Hesiod's Works and Days (109-120) describes the Golden Age under Cronus in strikingly similar terms: no toil, abundant food, mild climate, freedom from suffering. Plato both invokes and critically reexamines this tradition. -- The detail 'God himself was their shepherd, ruling over them, just as man rules over the lower animals' is the crucial analogy. It implies that humans under divine care were to God as livestock are to humans -- provided for, but not free or self-directing. -- The absence of government, family, and memory creates a pre-political condition that anticipates Rousseau's state of nature by two millennia. -- The comparison to life 'under Zeus' (the current era) sets up the political argument: if divine shepherding is no longer available, what kind of human governance can substitute?

D: Original: STRANGER: Suppose that the nurslings of Cronos, having this boundless leisure, and the power of holding intercourse, not only with men, but with the brute creation, had used all these advantages with a view to philosophy, conversing with the brutes as well as with one another, and learning of every nature which was gifted with any special power, and was able to contribute some special experience to the store of wisdom, there would be no difficulty in deciding that they would be a thousand times happier than the men of our own day. Or, again, if they had merely eaten and drunk until they were full, and told stories to one another and to the animals—such stories as are now attributed to them—in this case also, as I should imagine, the answer would be easy. But until some satisfactory witness can be found of the love of that age for knowledge and discussion, we had better let the matter drop, and give the reason why we have unearthed this tale, and then we shall be able to get on. In the fulness of time, when the change was to take place, and the earth-born race had all perished, and every soul had completed its proper cycle of births and been sown in the earth her appointed number of times, the pilot of the universe let the helm go, and retired to his place of view; and then Fate and innate desire reversed the motion of the world.
D: Narrative:
The Stranger posed the decisive question. If the nurslings of Cronus, with their boundless leisure and their ability to communicate not only with each other but with animals, had used these advantages to pursue philosophy -- conversing with every creature that possessed some special gift, accumulating wisdom from all sources -- then they would have been a thousand times happier than people today. That answer would be easy.

But if they had merely eaten and drunk their fill and told stories to one another and to the animals -- the kind of stories now attributed to that age -- then the answer would be equally easy, though in the opposite direction.

Since no reliable witness could testify to whether that age loved knowledge, the Stranger set the question aside and returned to the purpose of the myth. In the fullness of time, when the change was due, and the earth-born race had all perished, and every soul had completed its appointed cycle of births, the pilot of the universe let go of the helm and retired to his place of observation. Then Fate and innate desire reversed the motion of the world.
D: Refashioned:
The Stranger poses the test that decides whether the golden age was truly golden: What did they do with their freedom?

If the people of Cronus's age used their boundless leisure and universal communication -- they could even converse with animals -- to pursue philosophy, to learn from every creature with a special gift, to accumulate genuine wisdom, then they were incomparably happier than we are. That verdict is obvious.

But if they merely ate, drank, and told each other pleasant stories -- the kind of idle tales tradition attributes to them -- then the verdict goes the other way just as decisively.

Since we have no witness to settle which it was, the Stranger drops the question and returns to the myth's conclusion. When every soul had completed its appointed cycle, the pilot of the universe released the helm and withdrew. Fate and the world's own innate momentum reversed its course.
D: Elegant: The golden age is only golden if its people used their freedom for philosophy. Boundless leisure spent on idle pleasure is not paradise -- it is a comfortable cage. Without a witness to tell us which they chose, the question remains open and the cosmic wheel turns on.
D: Summary: Whether the age of Cronus was truly happy depends on whether its people pursued wisdom or merely indulged in ease. Since no witness can settle this, the Stranger concludes the myth: the divine pilot released the helm, and the cosmos reversed its course.
D: Distilled: Leisure without philosophy is not paradise; the value of a golden age depends entirely on what its inhabitants did with their freedom.
Notes: This passage contains one of Plato's most characteristic moves: redefining happiness in terms of philosophical activity rather than material comfort. Compare Aristotle's later argument in Nicomachean Ethics X that the contemplative life is the highest happiness. -- The ability to converse with animals recalls Orphic and Pythagorean traditions about the kinship of all living things. -- 'The pilot of the universe let go of the helm' -- the helmsman (kubernetes) metaphor for divine governance recurs throughout Greek political thought and gives us the English word 'governor' (via Latin gubernator). -- 'Fate and innate desire' (heimarmene and symphytos epithymia) as the agents of reversal suggest that the cosmos has its own natural tendencies that reassert themselves when divine control is withdrawn -- a key premise for the political argument about what happens when ideal governance ends.

D: Original: Then also all the inferior deities who share the rule of the supreme power, being informed of what was happening, let go the parts of the world which were under their control. And the world turning round with a sudden shock, being impelled in an opposite direction from beginning to end, was shaken by a mighty earthquake, which wrought a new destruction of all manner of animals. Afterwards, when sufficient time had elapsed, the tumult and confusion and earthquake ceased, and the universal creature, once more at peace, attained to a calm, and settled down into his own orderly and accustomed course, having the charge and rule of himself and of all the creatures which are contained in him, and executing, as far as he remembered them, the instructions of his Father and Creator, more precisely at first, but afterwords with less exactness. The reason of the falling off was the admixture of matter in him; this was inherent in the primal nature, which was full of disorder, until attaining to the present order. From God, the constructor, the world received all that is good in him, but from a previous state came elements of evil and unrighteousness, which, thence derived, first of all passed into the world, and were then transmitted to the animals. While the world was aided by the pilot in nurturing the animals, the evil was small, and great the good which he produced, but after the separation, when the world was let go, at first all proceeded well enough; but, as time went on, there was more and more forgetting, and the old discord again held sway and burst forth in full glory; and at last small was the good, and great was the admixture of evil, and there was a danger of universal ruin to the world, and to the things contained in him.
D: Narrative:
When the supreme power released the world, all the lesser deities who had shared in governing it also let go of their respective domains. The world, suddenly impelled in the opposite direction, was shaken by a tremendous earthquake that destroyed all manner of animals. But after sufficient time, the tumult ceased. The universal creature -- the cosmos itself -- settled into a calm and took charge of its own orderly course, executing the instructions of its Creator as well as it could remember them. At first it did so precisely, but over time with diminishing accuracy.

The reason for this decline was the admixture of matter in the cosmos -- matter inherited from a prior state of disorder. From God, the world received everything good. From its previous chaotic state, it inherited elements of evil and disorder, which passed into the world and then into its creatures. While the divine pilot was at the helm, the evil was small and the good was great. But after the separation, as time went on, the cosmos forgot more and more of its divine instructions. The old disorder reasserted itself until eventually there was a danger of universal ruin.
D: Refashioned:
When God let go, so did every lesser deity governing a portion of the world. The cosmos lurched into reverse, shaken by a catastrophic earthquake. Most life was destroyed.

Once the upheaval subsided, the cosmos had to govern itself. At first it managed well -- it still remembered its Creator's instructions and executed them faithfully. But the world is not purely divine. It contains matter, and matter carries disorder from before creation. Over time, the cosmos forgot. The divine instructions faded. The inherited chaos reasserted itself.

This is the trajectory of any system left to run on its own: initial competence degrading steadily toward dysfunction. While the pilot held the helm, good vastly outweighed evil. After the separation, the ratio gradually inverted until universal ruin became a real possibility.
D: Elegant: When God released the helm, the cosmos governed itself -- well at first, then worse, then catastrophically. Matter carries disorder the way memory carries loss. Every system left to its own devices eventually forgets what made it work.
D: Summary: After the divine withdrawal, the cosmos initially governed itself well by following remembered divine instructions. But the inherent disorder in matter caused a gradual decline, with evil and chaos increasing over time until universal destruction threatened.
D: Distilled: Every self-governing system inherits both its design and its disorder, and without renewal, the disorder eventually wins.
Notes: The 'admixture of matter' (to tes hyles synkerannumenon) as a source of evil is a key Platonic metaphysical principle, developed further in the Timaeus (47e-48a) where the demiurge works with pre-existing chaotic matter ('Necessity') that resists perfect ordering. -- This is not a Fall narrative in the Judeo-Christian sense -- there is no moral transgression. The decline is structural, built into the nature of physical existence itself. -- The gradual forgetting of divine instructions parallels the epistemological decline described in the Republic's Allegory of the Cave and the Allegory of the Allegory of the Sun. -- The political analogy is central: just as the cosmos degrades without divine maintenance, a state degrades without proper governance. This anticipates the Stranger's argument that human statesmanship must substitute for absent divine care.

D: Original: Wherefore God, the orderer of all, in his tender care, seeing that the world was in great straits, and fearing that all might be dissolved in the storm and disappear in infinite chaos, again seated himself at the helm; and bringing back the elements which had fallen into dissolution and disorder to the motion which had prevailed under his dispensation, he set them in order and restored them, and made the world imperishable and immortal. And this is the whole tale, of which the first part will suffice to illustrate the nature of the king. For when the world turned towards the present cycle of generation, the age of man again stood still, and a change opposite to the previous one was the result. The small creatures which had almost disappeared grew in and stature, and the newly-born children of the earth became grey and died and sank into the earth again. All things changed, imitating and following the condition of the universe, and of necessity agreeing with that in their mode of conception and generation and nurture; for no animal was any longer allowed to come into being in the earth through the agency of other creative beings, but as the world was ordained to be the lord of his own progress, in like manner the parts were ordained to grow and generate and give nourishment, as far as they could, of themselves, impelled by a similar movement. And so we have arrived at the real end of this discourse; for although there might be much to tell of the lower animals, and of the condition out of which they changed and of the causes of the change, about men there is not much, and that little is more to the purpose. Deprived of the care of God, who had possessed and tended them, they were left helpless and defenceless, and were torn in pieces by the beasts, who were naturally fierce and had now grown wild. And in the first ages they were still without skill or resource; the food which once grew spontaneously had failed, and as yet they knew not how to procure it, because they had never felt the pressure of necessity.
D: Narrative:
When the world was on the verge of dissolving into infinite chaos, God intervened once more. In his tender care, seeing the world's dire condition, he returned to the helm. He restored the elements that had fallen into dissolution, set them back in order, and made the world imperishable and immortal once again.

This, the Stranger said, was the whole tale, and the first part sufficed to illuminate the nature of the king. For when the world turned toward the present cycle, the process reversed again. The age of man resumed. The creatures that had nearly vanished grew in size and stature. Children born from the earth grew old, died, and returned to the ground. Everything imitated the condition of the universe. No animal could any longer come into being through divine agency -- the world was now ordained to be its own master, and its parts were impelled to grow, generate, and nourish themselves.

This brought the Stranger to the real conclusion. Deprived of divine care, humans were left helpless and defenseless, torn apart by beasts that had grown wild. In the earliest ages of the current cycle, humans had no skills and no resources. The food that once grew spontaneously had failed, and they did not yet know how to procure it, because they had never before felt the pressure of necessity.
D: Refashioned:
When chaos threatened to consume the world entirely, God returned to the helm. He restored order, repaired the dissolution, and made the cosmos stable once more. Then the current cycle began.

Now everything ran in the present direction. Creatures grew from small to large. Children were born, aged, died, and returned to the earth in the normal way. The cosmos was its own master, and every living thing within it had to generate and sustain itself without divine intervention.

This is where the myth becomes political argument. Stripped of divine care, the first humans of our era were helpless. They had no skills, no tools, no knowledge of agriculture. Wild animals, no longer restrained by divine shepherds, became predators. Food that once grew on its own had ceased. And humans had never needed to provide for themselves before -- they had no experience of necessity.

The statesman, in other words, exists because God left.
D: Elegant: God returned to save the cosmos from chaos, then let the current era begin. Humans entered a world without divine care -- no skills, no tools, no food that grew on its own, and predators everywhere. The statesman exists because God left and necessity arrived.
D: Summary: God periodically intervenes to prevent cosmic dissolution, but in the current cycle, humans must fend for themselves. Without divine shepherding, early humans were helpless -- lacking skills, food, and protection from predators. This is why human governance became necessary.
D: Distilled: The statesman exists because divine care ended and human beings had to learn to govern themselves under the pressure of necessity.
Notes: The return of God to the helm establishes that cosmic history is cyclical, not linear -- a structure shared with Hindu yugas and Stoic ekpyrosis, though Plato's version is uniquely tied to political theory. -- The transition from divine provision to human self-sufficiency parallels the Prometheus myth in Protagoras (320c-322d), where the gods equip humans with fire and technical skill to compensate for their natural defenselessness. -- 'They had never felt the pressure of necessity' -- this is the origin point for the entire argument about statesmanship. Necessity (ananke) is the mother of political art, just as it is the mother of invention generally. -- The image of humans as helpless without divine care inverts the earlier definition of the statesman as divine shepherd: in our era, no human ruler can provide the total care that God once did.

D: Original:
For all these reasons they were in a great strait; wherefore also the gifts spoken of in the old tradition were imparted to man by the gods, together with so much teaching and education as was indispensable; fire was given to them by Prometheus, the arts by Hephaestus and his fellow-worker, Athene, seeds and plants by others. From these is derived all that has helped to frame human life; since the care of the Gods, as I was saying, had now failed men, and they had to order their course of life for themselves, and were their own masters, just like the universal creature whom they imitate and follow, ever changing, as he changes, and ever living and growing, at one time in one manner, and at another time in another. Enough of the story, which may be of use in showing us how greatly we erred in the delineation of the king and the statesman in our previous discourse.

YOUNG SOCRATES: What was this great error of which you speak?

STRANGER: There were two; the first a lesser one, the other was an error on a much larger and grander scale.

YOUNG SOCRATES: What do you mean?
D: Narrative:
Because of their helplessness, the Stranger continued, the gods gave humans the gifts preserved in ancient tradition: fire from Prometheus, the arts from Hephaestus and Athena, seeds and plants from others. From these gifts, human life as we know it was built. For the care of the gods had failed, and humans had to order their own existence and become their own masters -- just like the universal creature they inhabit and imitate, ever changing as it changes, living and growing now in one manner, now in another.

The Stranger announced that this myth was sufficient to show how greatly they had erred in their previous attempt to define the king and statesman. Young Socrates asked what the great error was, and the Stranger replied that there were two -- the first a lesser one, the second much larger and grander in scale.
D: Refashioned:
The gods compensated for their withdrawal by giving humans the means of self-sufficiency: fire from Prometheus, technical arts from Hephaestus and Athena, agriculture from others. Everything that constitutes human civilization was a parting gift to replace direct divine management.

This is the condition we actually live in: no divine shepherd, only inherited tools and the obligation to govern ourselves. Humans imitate the cosmos -- self-directing, imperfect, gradually forgetting, periodically needing correction.

With the myth complete, the Stranger announces its payoff: it reveals two errors in their earlier definition of the statesman. The first error was smaller. The second was far more consequential.
D: Elegant: Fire, craft, agriculture -- parting gifts from gods who were leaving. Human civilization is what you build when divine care ends. The statesman is not a shepherd-god. He is a human solving human problems with inherited tools.
D: Summary: The gods compensated for their withdrawal by giving humans fire, technical arts, and agriculture. The myth reveals two errors in the previous definition of the statesman, setting up a corrected approach.
D: Distilled: Human civilization is built from the tools the gods left behind when they stopped governing directly.
Notes: The Promethean gift of fire and Hephaestus/Athena's gift of technical arts (technai) closely parallel the account in Protagoras 320c-322d, where Prometheus steals fire and Athena's craft-knowledge to compensate for Epimetheus's failure to equip humans with natural defenses. -- The phrase 'ever changing, as he changes' establishes humans as microcosmic mirrors of the macrocosm -- a concept that becomes central in Neoplatonic philosophy. -- The transition from myth back to dialectic is characteristic of Plato's method: the myth is not an end in itself but a tool for illuminating conceptual errors in the logical division.

D: Original:
STRANGER: I mean to say that when we were asked about a king and statesman of the present cycle and generation, we told of a shepherd of a human flock who belonged to the other cycle, and of one who was a god when he ought to have been a man; and this a great error. Again, we declared him to be the ruler of the entire State, without explaining how: this was not the whole truth, nor very intelligible; but still it was true, and therefore the second error was not so great as the first.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Very good.
D: Narrative:
The Stranger explained the two errors. First, when asked about a king and statesman of the present cycle, they had described a shepherd belonging to the other cycle -- a divine figure, when the statesman should be a human being. This was the greater error. Second, they had declared the statesman to be ruler of the entire state without explaining how he rules -- what his specific mode of governance is. This was less false but still incomplete, and therefore the lesser error.

Young Socrates accepted this assessment.
D: Refashioned:
The two errors are now clear.

The first and greater error: they described the statesman as a divine shepherd -- a figure from the age of Cronus, when God himself managed humanity. But the statesman they are looking for belongs to the current age, where the ruler is a human being, not a god. Confusing the two eras means confusing the nature of the office entirely.

The second and lesser error: they said the statesman rules the entire state but never specified how. The claim was true but incomplete -- like identifying someone's job title without describing what the job actually involves.

With both errors diagnosed, the inquiry can resume on corrected foundations.
D: Elegant: Two errors. The greater: they described a divine shepherd when they needed a human governor. The lesser: they named the office without defining the work. Both must be corrected before the inquiry can proceed.
D: Summary: The myth reveals two errors in the earlier definition. The greater error was describing the statesman as a divine shepherd rather than a human ruler. The lesser error was declaring him ruler of the state without specifying how he rules.
D: Distilled: The statesman is not a god caring for livestock but a human being whose specific mode of governance must be defined.
Notes: The distinction between divine shepherd and human statesman is the central payoff of the myth. It separates the Statesman from the Republic's philosopher-king, who has quasi-divine knowledge of the Forms. Here, the emphasis falls on human limitation and practical governance. -- The 'lesser error' of not specifying the mode of governance anticipates the dialogue's later distinction between rule by law, rule by knowledge, and rule by force. -- This passage marks the transition from mythological illustration back to the method of division (diairesis) that structures the dialogue.

D: Original:
STRANGER: Before we can expect to have a perfect description of the statesman we must define the nature of his office.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly.

STRANGER: And the myth was introduced in order to show, not only that all others are rivals of the true shepherd who is the object of our search, but in order that we might have a clearer view of him who is alone worthy to receive this appellation, because he alone of shepherds and herdsmen, according to the image which we have employed, has the care of human beings.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Very true.

STRANGER: And I cannot help thinking, Socrates, that the form of the divine shepherd is even higher than that of a king; whereas the statesmen who are now on earth seem to be much more like their subjects in character, and much more nearly to partake of their breeding and education.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly.

STRANGER: Still they must be investigated all the same, to see whether, like the divine shepherd, they are above their subjects or on a level with them.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Of course.

STRANGER: To resume:—Do you remember that we spoke of a command-for-self exercised over animals, not singly but collectively, which we called the art of rearing a herd?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Yes, I remember.

STRANGER: There, somewhere, lay our error; for we never included or mentioned the Statesman; and we did not observe that he had no place in our nomenclature.

YOUNG SOCRATES: How was that?

STRANGER: All other herdsmen 'rear' their herds, but this is not a suitable term to apply to the Statesman; we should use a name which is common to them all.

YOUNG SOCRATES: True, if there be such a name.

STRANGER: Why, is not 'care' of herds applicable to all? For this implies no feeding, or any special duty; if we say either 'tending' the herds, or 'managing' the herds, or 'having the care' of them, the same word will include all, and then we may wrap up the Statesman with the rest, as the argument seems to require.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Quite right; but how shall we take the next step in the division?
D: Narrative:
The Stranger declared that before they could achieve a perfect description of the statesman, they must define the nature of his office. The myth had served a dual purpose: to show that all other claimants are merely rivals of the true shepherd, and to clarify that only one figure deserves the title -- the one who has genuine care of human beings.

The Stranger observed that the divine shepherd's form is far above that of any earthly king, while present-day statesmen resemble their subjects in character, breeding, and education. Nevertheless, they must still be investigated.

Returning to the method of division, the Stranger recalled their earlier category of command-for-self exercised over animals collectively -- the art of rearing a herd. Their error had been to use this category without noticing it had no proper place for the statesman. The problem was the word 'rearing.' All other herdsmen rear their herds, but this term does not suit the statesman. A more general term was needed -- 'care,' or 'tending,' or 'managing' -- broad enough to include the statesman alongside other practitioners. Young Socrates agreed, and asked how they should proceed with the next division.
D: Refashioned:
With the myth's lessons absorbed, the Stranger returns to systematic definition. The myth accomplished two things: it exposed the rival claimants who compete with the true statesman, and it showed that divine shepherding is the wrong model for human governance.

Present-day statesmen are not gods among mortals. They resemble their subjects in character, education, and breeding. But they still need to be distinguished from everyone else who claims to care for people.

The earlier division broke down at a specific point: the word 'rearing.' Herdsmen rear their herds, but the statesman does not rear citizens. A broader term is needed -- 'care' or 'management' -- one general enough to include the statesman without importing the shepherd metaphor's baggage. With this terminological correction, the division can resume.
D: Elegant: The divine shepherd model is retired. The statesman is not above his people but among them -- distinguished not by nature but by function. The word is not 'rearing' but 'care,' and the division resumes on corrected terms.
D: Summary: The Stranger abandons the shepherd model and replaces 'rearing' with the broader term 'care' to describe the statesman's relationship to citizens. This terminological correction allows the systematic division to continue without importing misleading divine connotations.
D: Distilled: The statesman does not rear citizens like livestock; he provides care -- a distinction that separates human governance from divine shepherding.
Notes: The shift from 'rearing' (trophe) to 'care' (epimeleia) is not merely verbal -- it changes the entire conceptual framework. Rearing implies the provider is of a higher nature than those provided for; care implies a functional relationship between beings of similar nature. -- 'Statesmen who are now on earth seem to be much more like their subjects' -- this is a significant departure from the Republic's philosopher-king, who possesses knowledge the ruled lack. The Statesman is more egalitarian in its political anthropology. -- The method of division (diairesis) resumes here after the mythological interlude. Plato uses myth and dialectic as complementary methods throughout his later dialogues.

D: Original:
STRANGER: As before we divided the art of 'rearing' herds accordingly as they were land or water herds, winged and wingless, mixing or not mixing the breed, horned and hornless, so we may divide by these same differences the 'tending' of herds, comprehending in our definition the kingship of to-day and the rule of Cronos.

YOUNG SOCRATES: That is clear; but I still ask, what is to follow.

STRANGER: If the word had been 'managing' herds, instead of feeding or rearing them, no one would have argued that there was no care of men in the case of the politician, although it was justly contended, that there was no human art of feeding them which was worthy of the name, or at least, if there were, many a man had a prior and greater right to share in such an art than any king.

YOUNG SOCRATES: True.
D: Narrative:
The Stranger proposed applying to the art of 'tending' herds the same divisions they had previously applied to 'rearing' -- by land or water, winged or wingless, mixing breed or not, horned or hornless. These divisions would encompass both the present-day kingship and the rule of Cronus under a single framework.

Young Socrates found this clear but pressed for what would follow. The Stranger pointed out that if they had used the word 'managing' from the start rather than 'feeding' or 'rearing,' no one could have objected that the politician has no care of human beings. The original objection -- that many people have a better claim to feeding humans than any king -- would never have arisen. The problem was always in the framing, not the substance.
D: Refashioned:
The same divisions applied earlier -- land or water, winged or wingless, horned or hornless -- can be reapplied under the corrected term 'tending.' The framework holds; only the misleading label needed to change.

The Stranger drives the point home: if they had said 'managing' instead of 'feeding' from the beginning, the whole confusion would have been avoided. Nobody would have argued that merchants and doctors have a better claim to managing humanity than the statesman does. They only had a better claim to feeding humanity. The error was terminological, but terminological errors in definition are substantive errors in understanding.
D: Elegant: The framework was sound; the label was wrong. Call it 'feeding' and the farmer outranks the king. Call it 'managing' and only the statesman qualifies. Definitions are not word games -- they determine what you can see.
D: Summary: The earlier divisions remain valid under the corrected term 'tending' instead of 'rearing.' The Stranger shows that the original confusion arose from calling the statesman's art 'feeding' rather than 'managing' -- a terminological error with substantive consequences.
D: Distilled: A wrong name for the right concept produces wrong conclusions; the precision of definitions determines the clarity of thought.
Notes: The argument that terminological precision determines conceptual clarity is a hallmark of Plato's later method. The Cratylus explores whether names are natural or conventional; here, the practical consequence of naming is demonstrated. -- The distinction between feeding (trophe) and managing (epimeleia/therapeia) parallels modern debates about whether government's role is to provide for citizens (welfare state) or to organize and coordinate them (regulatory/administrative state). -- The list of rival claimants (merchants, farmers, doctors) who can claim to 'feed' humanity better than a king anticipates the later systematic separation of causal from cooperative arts.

D: Original:
STRANGER: But no other art or science will have a prior or better right than the royal science to care for human society and to rule over men in general.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Quite true.

STRANGER: In the next place, Socrates, we must surely notice that a great error was committed at the end of our analysis.

YOUNG SOCRATES: What was it?

STRANGER: Why, supposing we were ever so sure that there is such an art as the art of rearing or feeding bipeds, there was no reason why we should call this the royal or political art, as though there were no more to be said.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly not.

STRANGER: Our first duty, as we were saying, was to remodel the name, so as to have the notion of care rather than of feeding, and then to divide, for there may be still considerable divisions.

YOUNG SOCRATES: How can they be made?

STRANGER: First, by separating the divine shepherd from the human guardian or manager.

YOUNG SOCRATES: True.

STRANGER: And the art of management which is assigned to man would again have to be subdivided.

YOUNG SOCRATES: On what principle?
