STRANGER: On the principle of voluntary and compulsory.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Why?

STRANGER: Because, if I am not mistaken, there has been an error here; for our simplicity led us to rank king and tyrant together, whereas they are utterly distinct, like their modes of government.

YOUNG SOCRATES: True.

STRANGER: Then, now, as I said, let us make the correction and divide human care into two parts, on the principle of voluntary and compulsory.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly.

STRANGER: And if we call the management of violent rulers tyranny, and the voluntary management of herds of voluntary bipeds politics, may we not further assert that he who has this latter art of management is the true king and statesman?

YOUNG SOCRATES: I think, Stranger, that we have now completed the account of the Statesman.
D: Narrative:
The Stranger asserted that no other art or science has a prior or better claim than the royal science to care for human society and rule over people in general. Young Socrates agreed.

But the Stranger immediately identified another significant error. Even if they could confirm that an art of rearing bipeds exists, they had no reason to simply call it the royal or political art as though the definition were complete. Their first duty was to remodel the name to emphasize care rather than feeding, and then to continue dividing.

The Stranger proposed the first division: separating the divine shepherd from the human guardian or manager. Then the art of human management must be further subdivided -- on the principle of voluntary and compulsory. This distinction was critical because their earlier simplicity had led them to rank king and tyrant together, when in fact they are utterly distinct. Violent, compulsory rule is tyranny. Voluntary management of willing citizens is politics. The one who possesses this latter art -- the voluntary care of voluntary beings -- is the true king and statesman.

Young Socrates believed the definition was now complete.
D: Refashioned:
The royal science has the strongest claim to caring for human society. No rival art can match it. But naming the claim is not the same as defining the practice.

The Stranger introduces two crucial divisions. First, divine shepherding must be separated from human management -- the myth's lesson formalized. Second, human management itself divides into voluntary and compulsory.

This second division is where the definition gains real force. The earlier analysis had collapsed king and tyrant into the same category -- a serious error, because they operate on opposite principles. The tyrant rules by force over unwilling subjects. The statesman manages willing citizens through voluntary cooperation.

Compulsory rule over unwilling people is tyranny. Voluntary care of voluntary people is statesmanship. The true king is the one whose subjects accept his authority freely.

Young Socrates thinks the definition is complete. He is wrong.
D: Elegant: Two divisions cut to the core. First: the statesman is human, not divine. Second: the statesman rules the willing, not the coerced. A tyrant forces compliance; a king earns consent. Young Socrates thinks the job is done. It is not.
D: Summary: The Stranger separates divine from human governance, then divides human governance into voluntary (statesmanship) and compulsory (tyranny). The true statesman rules willing citizens through consent, not force. But the definition is still incomplete.
D: Distilled: The fundamental distinction between a statesman and a tyrant is that one governs the willing and the other coerces the unwilling.
Notes: The voluntary/compulsory distinction is one of the Statesman's most important contributions to political theory. It anticipates the social contract tradition (Hobbes, Locke, Rousseau) by grounding legitimate authority in the consent of the governed. -- The separation of king and tyrant corrects a conflation that Thrasymachus exploits in Republic I (338c-339a), where he argues that justice is the advantage of the stronger. -- 'Young Socrates believed the definition was now complete' -- this premature satisfaction is a recurring Platonic device. The interlocutor thinks the work is done precisely when it is about to become more difficult.

D: Original: STRANGER: Would that we had, Socrates, but I have to satisfy myself as well as you; and in my judgment the figure of the king is not yet perfected; like statuaries who, in their too great haste, having overdone the several parts of their work, lose time in cutting them down, so too we, partly out of haste, partly out of a magnanimous desire to expose our former error, and also because we imagined that a king required grand illustrations, have taken up a marvellous lump of fable, and have been obliged to use more than was necessary. This made us discourse at large, and, nevertheless, the story never came to an end. And our discussion might be compared to a picture of some living being which had been fairly drawn in outline, but had not yet attained the life and clearness which is given by the blending of colours. Now to intelligent persons a living being had better be delineated by language and discourse than by any painting or work of art: to the duller sort by works of art.
D: Narrative:
The Stranger gently corrected Young Socrates. The figure of the king was not yet perfected. Like sculptors who, in their haste, overdo certain parts of a statue and then waste time cutting them down, the Stranger and his companion had proceeded too eagerly. Partly from haste, partly from a desire to expose their earlier error, and partly because they imagined that defining a king required grand illustrations, they had taken up a marvelous lump of myth and used more of it than necessary. The story never quite came to an end, and their discussion resembled a painting that had been fairly drawn in outline but lacked the life and clarity that the blending of colors would provide.

For intelligent persons, the Stranger observed, a living being is better described through language and reasoning than through any painting or artwork. For duller minds, visual works suffice.
D: Refashioned:
The Stranger pulls back. The definition is not complete -- it is an outline without color.

They made three mistakes of method. They rushed. They overcompensated for the earlier error by reaching for a grand myth. And they assumed that a king requires grand illustrations. The result: a story that took on a life of its own without ever quite finishing, and a definition that looks right at a distance but lacks precision up close.

The analogy is to a sculptor who, working too fast, builds up too much material and then wastes time cutting it down. Or to a painting sketched in outline but never filled with the colors that give it life.

For people who think carefully, language and reasoning are better tools than images. But images are where they have to start.
D: Elegant: A definition sketched in outline is not a definition completed. They drew the shape of the statesman but never filled in the color. The myth was too much marble; now the excess must be carved away.
D: Summary: The Stranger acknowledges that their definition is an outline, not a finished portrait. The myth was grander than necessary, and the figure of the statesman still lacks precision. They need a more focused analytical method.
D: Distilled: A definition that looks right at a distance but lacks precision up close is not yet a definition at all.
Notes: The sculptor analogy anticipates Michelangelo's famous claim that sculpture is the art of removing excess stone to reveal the form within -- but Plato's point is methodological, not aesthetic. -- The distinction between 'intelligent persons' who learn through logos (language/reason) and 'duller minds' who need visual aids reflects the divided line in Republic VI (509d-511e), where direct intellectual apprehension is ranked above perception and image. -- The admission that the myth was excessive is characteristic of the Statesman's self-reflective methodology: the dialogue constantly critiques its own procedures.

D: Original:
YOUNG SOCRATES: Very true; but what is the imperfection which still remains? I wish that you would tell me.

STRANGER: The higher ideas, my dear friend, can hardly be set forth except through the medium of examples; every man seems to know all things in a dreamy sort of way, and then again to wake up and to know nothing.

YOUNG SOCRATES: What do you mean?

STRANGER: I fear that I have been unfortunate in raising a question about our experience of knowledge.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Why so?

STRANGER: Why, because my 'example' requires the assistance of another example.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Proceed; you need not fear that I shall tire.

STRANGER: I will proceed, finding, as I do, such a ready listener in you: when children are beginning to know their letters—

YOUNG SOCRATES: What are you going to say?

STRANGER: That they distinguish the several letters well enough in very short and easy syllables, and are able to tell them correctly.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly.

STRANGER: Whereas in other syllables they do not recognize them, and think and speak falsely of them.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Very true.

STRANGER: Will not the best and easiest way of bringing them to a knowledge of what they do not as yet know be—

YOUNG SOCRATES: Be what?
D: Narrative:
Young Socrates asked what imperfection remained and urged the Stranger to explain. The Stranger replied that the highest ideas can hardly be conveyed except through examples. Everyone seems to know all things in a dreamy way, and then to wake up and know nothing.

Young Socrates was puzzled, and the Stranger admitted that he was raising an awkward question about the nature of knowledge itself. Worse, his 'example' needed another example to explain it.

Undaunted, Young Socrates encouraged him to proceed. The Stranger offered the analogy of children learning letters. When children are beginning to read, they can identify letters correctly in short, easy syllables. But in longer or unfamiliar syllables, they fail to recognize the very same letters and make mistakes. The question was: what is the best way to bring them to recognize what they do not yet know?
D: Refashioned:
What is still missing? The Stranger's answer goes to the heart of how understanding works.

Everyone, he says, knows everything in a kind of dream state. We recognize things vaguely, get the shape right, but cannot articulate the details. Then we try to be precise and discover we know nothing clearly.

To bridge this gap, you need examples -- but examples themselves need to be understood first, which requires further examples. It sounds circular, but it is not. The Stranger illustrates with children learning to read.

A child learning letters can identify them correctly in simple syllables. But in longer, unfamiliar combinations, the same child fails to recognize the very same letters. The letters have not changed. The child's ability to see them has. The question is: how do you teach recognition of the same element in an unfamiliar context?
D: Elegant: We all know everything in a dream and nothing when we wake. The challenge of philosophy is the same as the challenge of reading: recognizing familiar elements in unfamiliar combinations.
D: Summary: The Stranger argues that understanding requires examples because people know things vaguely but not precisely. Using the analogy of children learning letters, he shows that the difficulty lies in recognizing familiar elements in unfamiliar contexts.
D: Distilled: Philosophy, like literacy, is the art of recognizing the same principle in different contexts.
Notes: The 'dreamy knowledge' (onar vs. hypar) distinction connects to Republic V (476c-d), where Socrates distinguishes those who dream of beauty from those who are awake to the Form of Beauty itself. -- The letters analogy (stoicheia) is significant because the same Greek word means both 'letters' and 'elements' -- Plato is making a point about the structure of reality as well as the structure of knowledge. The elements of political science must be recognized across different contexts just as letters must be recognized across different syllables. -- The Stranger's admission that his example needs another example is a moment of philosophical honesty about the regress problem in epistemology.

D: Original: STRANGER: To refer them first of all to cases in which they judge correctly about the letters in question, and then to compare these with the cases in which they do not as yet know, and to show them that the letters are the same, and have the same character in both combinations, until all cases in which they are right have been placed side by side with all cases in which they are wrong. In this way they have examples, and are made to learn that each letter in every combination is always the same and not another, and is always called by the same name.
D: Narrative: The Stranger provided the answer. You refer children first to cases where they correctly identify the letter in question, then compare those cases with the ones where they fail to recognize it. By placing correct and incorrect instances side by side, the children learn that the letter is the same in both combinations -- always the same element, always called by the same name. In this way, examples are formed: you take a thing and compare it with another distinct instance of the same thing, and from the comparison a single true notion emerges that encompasses both.
D: Refashioned:
The method is comparison. You take a case where the child correctly identifies the letter and place it next to a case where the child gets it wrong. Side by side, the child sees that the element is the same in both -- same letter, same name, different context.

This is how examples work in general. You take something understood in one context and set it alongside the same thing in an unfamiliar context. The comparison produces a single unified concept that covers both instances. Recognition transfers from the known case to the unknown one.

This is exactly what the Stranger is about to do with statesmanship: find a simpler art that shares the same structure, analyze it thoroughly, and then transfer that understanding to the more complex case.
D: Elegant: Place what you recognize next to what you do not, and the pattern reveals itself. This is the method of examples: understanding transfers from the known to the unknown through structured comparison.
D: Summary: The method of examples works by placing a correctly understood instance next to an incorrectly understood one, revealing that the same element is present in both. Understanding transfers from the familiar context to the unfamiliar one.
D: Distilled: You learn to see what you have missed by placing it beside what you already recognize.
Notes: The method of paradeigma (example/paradigm) described here is distinct from both the method of division (diairesis) and the method of hypothesis used in the Phaedo and Republic. It is a comparative method, closer to analogical reasoning. -- The letters analogy has a deeper philosophical implication: just as letters are the elements (stoicheia) of language, the Forms are the elements of reality. Learning to recognize the same Form in different material instances is the core of Platonic education. -- This methodological passage prepares for the introduction of weaving as a model (paradeigma) for statesmanship.

D: Original:
YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly.

STRANGER: Are not examples formed in this manner? We take a thing and compare it with another distinct instance of the same thing, of which we have a right conception, and out of the comparison there arises one true notion, which includes both of them.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Exactly.

STRANGER: Can we wonder, then, that the soul has the same uncertainty about the alphabet of things, and sometimes and in some cases is firmly fixed by the truth in each particular, and then, again, in other cases is altogether at sea; having somehow or other a correct notion of combinations; but when the elements are transferred into the long and difficult language (syllables) of facts, is again ignorant of them?

YOUNG SOCRATES: There is nothing wonderful in that.

STRANGER: Could any one, my friend, who began with false opinion ever expect to arrive even at a small portion of truth and to attain wisdom?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Hardly.

STRANGER: Then you and I will not be far wrong in trying to see the nature of example in general in a small and particular instance; afterwards from lesser things we intend to pass to the royal class, which is the highest form of the same nature, and endeavour to discover by rules of art what the management of cities is; and then the dream will become a reality to us.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Very true.

STRANGER: Then, once more, let us resume the previous argument, and as there were innumerable rivals of the royal race who claim to have the care of states, let us part them all off, and leave him alone; and, as I was saying, a model or example of this process has first to be framed.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Exactly.

STRANGER: What model is there which is small, and yet has any analogy with the political occupation? Suppose, Socrates, that if we have no other example at hand, we choose weaving, or, more precisely, weaving of wool—this will be quite enough, without taking the whole of weaving, to illustrate our meaning?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly.

STRANGER: Why should we not apply to weaving the same processes of division and subdivision which we have already applied to other classes; going once more as rapidly as we can through all the steps until we come to that which is needed for our purpose?

YOUNG SOCRATES: How do you mean?
D: Narrative:
The Stranger extended the analogy. Just as the soul has uncertainty about the alphabet of things -- sometimes firmly grasping the truth in particular cases, sometimes completely at sea -- so too our understanding wavers. We may have correct notions about simple combinations but become ignorant when the same elements appear in the long and difficult language of facts.

No one who begins with false opinion, the Stranger warned, can hope to arrive at even a small portion of truth. Therefore they would not go wrong in first examining the nature of example itself through a small, particular instance. From lesser things they would pass to the royal class -- the highest form of the same nature -- and try to discover by methodical rules what the management of cities truly is.

The Stranger proposed a model: since innumerable rivals claim the care of states, they needed to separate the true statesman from all pretenders. To do this, they needed an example -- a small art with an analogy to the political occupation. He suggested weaving, specifically the weaving of wool, as sufficient to illustrate the point. They would apply to weaving the same processes of division and subdivision they had used before, working through the steps as rapidly as possible until they reached what was needed.
D: Refashioned:
Our minds work like children learning letters. We grasp simple cases clearly but lose the pattern in complex ones. The same element that is obvious in a simple context becomes invisible in a difficult one. And you cannot reason your way to truth from a false starting point.

The solution: start with a small, manageable example that shares the same structure as the complex problem. Master the small case. Then transfer that understanding to the larger one.

The complex problem is statesmanship -- surrounded by rival claimants, all insisting they have the right to care for the state. The small, manageable example the Stranger chooses is weaving. Specifically, wool-weaving.

Why weaving? Because it shares the structural features that matter: it combines different materials into a unified product, it requires distinguishing the essential art from auxiliary and cooperative arts, and it is surrounded by rival crafts that claim a share of its territory. The same method of division that failed with 'herdsman' will now be applied to weaving, and whatever they learn there will transfer directly to politics.
D: Elegant: Start small to see clearly. Weaving is the model for statesmanship -- not because looms resemble governments, but because both arts combine different elements into something unified while fending off rivals who claim the work as their own.
D: Summary: The Stranger introduces weaving as a small-scale example that shares the structural features of statesmanship. By analyzing how weaving is distinguished from its rival and cooperative arts, they can transfer that understanding to the more complex problem of defining the statesman.
D: Distilled: To understand a complex art, first master a simple one with the same structure, then transfer the pattern.
Notes: The choice of weaving (hyphantike) as paradeigma for statesmanship is not arbitrary. Athena was goddess of both weaving and civic wisdom. The metaphor of weaving the state appears in Republic (369d-374e) and becomes central to the Statesman's final definition. -- The 'rivals of the true shepherd' anticipates the systematic separation of cooperative from causal arts that follows. In political terms, these rivals are the merchants, doctors, teachers, and military commanders who each claim to provide for citizens. -- 'The long and difficult language of facts' (literally 'long and not-easy syllables of things') maintains the stoicheia metaphor: reality has a grammar, and political science is a form of literacy.

D: Original:
STRANGER: I shall reply by actually performing the process.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Very good.
D: Narrative: The Stranger announced he would demonstrate the method by actually performing the division of weaving, rather than merely describing it. Young Socrates encouraged him to proceed.
D: Refashioned: Rather than theorize further about method, the Stranger will demonstrate it. The division of weaving begins now.
D: Elegant: Theory yields to demonstration. The Stranger will show the method by performing it.
D: Summary: The Stranger transitions from discussing the method of examples to actually applying it, beginning the systematic division of the art of weaving.
D: Distilled: The best way to teach a method is to perform it.
Notes: This brief transitional passage embodies a Platonic principle: philosophical method is learned through practice, not just description. The Stranger models the very kind of teaching the dialogue is about.

D: Original: STRANGER: All things which we make or acquire are either creative or preventive; of the preventive class are antidotes, divine and human, and also defences; and defences are either military weapons or protections; and protections are veils, and also shields against heat and cold, and shields against heat and cold are shelters and coverings; and coverings are blankets and garments; and garments are some of them in one piece, and others of them are made in several parts; and of these latter some are stitched, others are fastened and not stitched; and of the not stitched, some are made of the sinews of plants, and some of hair; and of these, again, some are cemented with water and earth, and others are fastened together by themselves. And these last defences and coverings which are fastened together by themselves are called clothes, and the art which superintends them we may call, from the nature of the operation, the art of clothing, just as before the art of the Statesman was derived from the State; and may we not say that the art of weaving, at least that largest portion of it which was concerned with the making of clothes, differs only in name from this art of clothing, in the same way that, in the previous case, the royal science differed from the political?
D: Narrative:
The Stranger began the division at the highest level. Everything humans make or acquire is either creative or preventive. The preventive class includes antidotes (both divine and human) and defenses. Defenses are either military weapons or protections. Protections are either veils or shields against heat and cold. Shields against heat and cold are either shelters or coverings. Coverings are either blankets or garments. Garments are either one piece or made of several parts. Of those made in several parts, some are stitched and some are fastened without stitching. Of the unstitched, some are made of plant fibers and some of hair. Of the hair-based ones, some are cemented with water and earth, and some are fastened together by themselves.

These last -- defenses and coverings fastened together by themselves -- are called clothes. The art that superintends their production is the art of clothing. And the art of weaving, at least the largest part of it concerned with making clothes, differs from the art of clothing only in name -- just as the royal science differs from the political.
D: Refashioned:
The Stranger demonstrates the method of division by carving a path from the most general category down to weaving.

Everything we make or acquire is either creative or preventive. Within the preventive class: antidotes and defenses. Within defenses: weapons and protections. Within protections: veils and shields against weather. Within weather-shields: shelters and coverings. Within coverings: blankets and garments. Within garments: one-piece and multi-part. Within multi-part: stitched and unstitched. Within unstitched: plant fiber and hair. Within hair-based: cemented with earth-and-water, or self-fastening.

Self-fastening hair coverings are what we call clothes. The art that produces them is the art of clothing. And weaving -- the largest portion of clothing-making -- differs from 'the art of clothing' only in name, just as 'royal science' and 'political art' are two names for the same thing.

The division is complete. But the definition is not, because weaving has not yet been separated from its related and cooperative arts.
D: Elegant: From the broadest category -- everything humans make -- the Stranger cuts twelve divisions deep to reach weaving. The path is precise: creative vs. preventive, defenses vs. antidotes, protections vs. weapons, all the way down to self-fastening hair coverings. But reaching weaving is not the same as defining it.
D: Summary: The Stranger demonstrates the division method by tracing a path from the most general category (things made or acquired) through twelve subdivisions down to the art of weaving. Weaving is identified as the art of making self-fastening woolen clothes, but it has not yet been distinguished from related arts.
D: Distilled: Reaching a category through division is not the same as defining it -- you must still separate it from everything that resembles it.
Notes: The twelve-step division from 'all things made' to weaving demonstrates the method of diairesis (division) in its most systematic form. Each step is a binary cut. -- The final identification of weaving with 'the art of clothing' parallels the identification of the royal science with political art -- both are cases where two names obscure a single referent. -- The distinction between stitched and unstitched, plant fiber and hair, etc., reflects real ancient textile classification. Greek clothing (chiton, himation, peplos) was typically woven wool, pinned or draped rather than sewn. -- 'Cemented with water and earth' refers to felting, which is being explicitly separated from weaving.

D: Original:
YOUNG SOCRATES: Most true.

STRANGER: In the next place, let us make the reflection, that the art of weaving clothes, which an incompetent person might fancy to have been sufficiently described, has been separated off from several others which are of the same family, but not from the co-operative arts.

YOUNG SOCRATES: And which are the kindred arts?

STRANGER: I see that I have not taken you with me. So I think that we had better go backwards, starting from the end. We just now parted off from the weaving of clothes, the making of blankets, which differ from each other in that one is put under and the other is put around: and these are what I termed kindred arts.

YOUNG SOCRATES: I understand.

STRANGER: And we have subtracted the manufacture of all articles made of flax and cords, and all that we just now metaphorically termed the sinews of plants, and we have also separated off the process of felting and the putting together of materials by stitching and sewing, of which the most important part is the cobbler's art.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Precisely.
D: Narrative:
The Stranger observed that while weaving clothes had been separated from several kindred arts, it had not yet been distinguished from its cooperative arts. Young Socrates asked which arts were kindred.

The Stranger suggested working backward from what they had just established. They had separated the making of blankets from the weaving of clothes -- blankets being put under, clothes being put around. These were what he called kindred arts. They had also subtracted the manufacture of articles made from flax and plant cords, and they had separated felting and stitching-and-sewing, the most important part of which was cobbling.

Young Socrates confirmed each of these separations.
D: Refashioned:
Weaving has been isolated from many related arts, but the Stranger notes an important gap: the cooperative arts have not yet been separated out.

Working backward through the divisions, here is what has already been removed. Blanket-making was separated from garment-making -- one goes under, the other goes around. Flax and plant-cord manufacturing was set aside. Felting was excluded. Stitching and sewing -- especially cobbling -- were cut away.

These are all kindred arts. They share materials or methods with weaving but produce different things. The cooperative arts -- the ones that contribute to weaving without being weaving -- are a different problem entirely.
D: Elegant: Blankets, felt, cord, cobbling -- each has been carved away from weaving. These are the kindred arts, related by material and method. But the cooperative arts, the ones that serve weaving without being it, remain tangled in the definition.
D: Summary: The Stranger reviews which kindred arts have been separated from weaving -- blanket-making, flax-working, felting, cobbling -- and identifies the still-unresolved problem of distinguishing weaving from its cooperative arts.
D: Distilled: Separating an art from its relatives is only half the work; you must also separate it from its servants.
Notes: The distinction between kindred arts (syngeneis technai) and cooperative arts (synaitiai technai) maps directly onto the political problem: the statesman must be distinguished both from rival rulers (kindred) and from administrators, soldiers, and functionaries who serve the state without ruling it (cooperative). -- Ancient Greek textiles were primarily wool, linen (flax), and felt. The separation of these materials tracks real divisions in ancient craft specialization. -- Cobbling as 'the most important part of stitching and sewing' reflects the social status of shoemakers in Athens -- a common and visible trade, often referenced in philosophical examples.

D: Original: STRANGER: Then we separated off the currier's art, which prepared coverings in entire pieces, and the art of sheltering, and subtracted the various arts of making water-tight which are employed in building, and in general in carpentering, and in other crafts, and all such arts as furnish impediments to thieving and acts of violence, and are concerned with making the lids of boxes and the fixing of doors, being divisions of the art of joining; and we also cut off the manufacture of arms, which is a section of the great and manifold art of making defences; and we originally began by parting off the whole of the magic art which is concerned with antidotes, and have left, as would appear, the very art of which we were in search, the art of protection against winter cold, which fabricates woollen defences, and has the name of weaving.
D: Narrative:
The Stranger continued the review. They had separated the currier's art -- the preparation of coverings in entire pieces. They had subtracted the art of sheltering and the various waterproofing arts used in building and carpentry. They had excluded all arts concerned with making impediments to theft and violence -- the lids of boxes and the fixing of doors, which are divisions of the art of joining. They had cut off the manufacture of arms, a section of the great art of defense. And at the very beginning, they had separated the whole of the magical art concerned with antidotes.

What remained, it appeared, was the very art they had been searching for: the art of protection against cold that fabricates woolen defenses -- the art called weaving.
D: Refashioned:
The Stranger continues clearing the field. The leather-worker's art: removed. Shelter-building: removed. Waterproofing, carpentry, locksmithing, door-making: all removed. Arms manufacturing: removed. The magical art of antidotes: removed at the very first step.

What remains is precisely what they set out to define: the art that fabricates woolen protection against cold. This is weaving.

But as with the statesman, arriving at the correct name is not the end. Every art that has been removed is a potential rival claiming territory that overlaps with weaving. The definition must be precise enough to exclude all of them definitively.
D: Elegant: Strip away leather-work, shelter, waterproofing, carpentry, locksmithing, arms-making, and magic. What remains is weaving: the art of woolen protection against cold. But naming it is not enough -- it must be distinguished from everything that serves it.
D: Summary: After systematically removing all related arts -- leather-working, building, carpentry, arms-making, and more -- only weaving remains: the art of fabricating woolen protection against cold. But the definition still requires further refinement.
D: Distilled: Definition by elimination reveals what something is by exhaustively establishing what it is not.
Notes: The comprehensive list of excluded arts demonstrates the thoroughness required by the method of division. Each excluded art corresponds to a real Athenian craft guild or trade. -- 'The magical art concerned with antidotes' (pharmakeia) includes both medicinal and supernatural remedies. Its placement at the first division (creative vs. preventive) shows that Plato considers magical arts a legitimate category requiring formal exclusion rather than dismissal. -- The parallel to statesmanship is becoming explicit: just as weaving must be distinguished from all the arts that contribute to or resemble it, the statesman must be distinguished from all who claim to govern or care for citizens.

D: Original:
YOUNG SOCRATES: Very true.

STRANGER: Yes, my boy, but that is not all; for the first process to which the material is subjected is the opposite of weaving.

YOUNG SOCRATES: How so?

STRANGER: Weaving is a sort of uniting?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Yes.

STRANGER: But the first process is a separation of the clotted and matted fibres?

YOUNG SOCRATES: What do you mean?

STRANGER: I mean the work of the carder's art; for we cannot say that carding is weaving, or that the carder is a weaver.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly not.

STRANGER: Again, if a person were to say that the art of making the warp and the woof was the art of weaving, he would say what was paradoxical and false.

YOUNG SOCRATES: To be sure.

STRANGER: Shall we say that the whole art of the fuller or of the mender has nothing to do with the care and treatment of clothes, or are we to regard all these as arts of weaving?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly not.

STRANGER: And yet surely all these arts will maintain that they are concerned with the treatment and production of clothes; they will dispute the exclusive prerogative of weaving, and though assigning a larger sphere to that, will still reserve a considerable field for themselves.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Very true.

STRANGER: Besides these, there are the arts which make tools and instruments of weaving, and which will claim at least to be co-operative causes in every work of the weaver.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Most true.

STRANGER: Well, then, suppose that we define weaving, or rather that part of it which has been selected by us, to be the greatest and noblest of arts which are concerned with woollen garments—shall we be right? Is not the definition, although true, wanting in clearness and completeness; for do not all those other arts require to be first cleared away?

YOUNG SOCRATES: True.

STRANGER: Then the next thing will be to separate them, in order that the argument may proceed in a regular manner?

YOUNG SOCRATES: By all means.

STRANGER: Let us consider, in the first place, that there are two kinds of arts entering into everything which we do.

YOUNG SOCRATES: What are they?

STRANGER: The one kind is the conditional or co-operative, the other the principal cause.

YOUNG SOCRATES: What do you mean?
D: Narrative:
Young Socrates agreed they had arrived at weaving, but the Stranger was not finished. The first process applied to the raw material is the opposite of weaving: weaving unites, but the first step -- carding -- separates matted fibers. The carder is not a weaver. Similarly, spinning the warp and woof is not weaving, though someone might mistakenly call it that.

What about the fuller and the mender? Surely they have something to do with the care of clothes? Yet they are not weavers. All these arts -- carding, spinning, fulling, mending -- will insist they are concerned with the production and treatment of clothes, disputing weaving's exclusive claim while reserving considerable territory for themselves.

Beyond these, there are the arts that make the tools and instruments of weaving -- the spindles and combs and shuttles -- which claim to be cooperative causes of every woven product.

The Stranger posed the central question: if they simply declare weaving to be the greatest and noblest of arts concerned with woolen garments, is the definition complete? It is not. All these other arts must first be systematically cleared away.

To do this, the Stranger introduced a fundamental distinction: in everything we do, there are two kinds of arts. One is the conditional or cooperative cause. The other is the principal cause.
D: Refashioned:
Arriving at weaving does not mean understanding it. The art is surrounded by processes that resemble it, contribute to it, or claim to be part of it.

Consider what is not weaving. Carding separates fibers -- the opposite of weaving's uniting action. Spinning produces the warp and woof, but spinning is not weaving. Fulling and mending treat finished cloth, but neither is weaving. All of these arts will insist they are 'concerned with clothes,' and they are right -- but being concerned with clothes is not the same as being the art of weaving.

Then there are the tool-makers: the craftsmen who produce spindles, combs, and shuttles. Without their work, weaving cannot happen. But making a loom is not weaving.

Simply calling weaving 'the greatest art concerned with woolen garments' is not a definition. It is a compliment. A real definition must separate the essential art from everything that assists it.

The Stranger introduces the key distinction: every productive activity involves two kinds of contributing arts. There are cooperative causes -- arts that provide necessary conditions but do not produce the thing itself. And there are principal causes -- arts that actually make the product.
D: Elegant: Carding separates. Spinning prepares. Fulling finishes. Tool-making equips. None of them is weaving. The essential art must be distinguished from everything that merely assists it. Two categories: cooperative causes that enable, and principal causes that create.
D: Summary: The Stranger shows that carding, spinning, fulling, mending, and tool-making all contribute to cloth production but are not themselves weaving. He introduces the fundamental distinction between cooperative causes (enabling arts) and principal causes (productive arts) to isolate weaving proper.
D: Distilled: The art that makes the thing is not the same as the arts that make the thing possible.
Notes: The distinction between principal cause (aitia) and cooperative/conditional cause (synaitia) is one of the Statesman's most influential conceptual contributions. It reappears in Aristotle's four causes (material, formal, efficient, final) and in modern philosophy of action. -- Applied to politics: the statesman is the principal cause of civic order, while soldiers, judges, administrators, and educators are cooperative causes. This distinction will become the basis for the final definition of the statesman as weaver of the state. -- The specific crafts mentioned -- carding (xantike), spinning (nesis), fulling (gnapheion) -- correspond to real stages of ancient wool processing that any Athenian audience would recognize. -- The challenge of rival claimants parallels Socrates' challenge in Republic I, where Thrasymachus, Polemarchus, and Cephalus each offer competing definitions of justice.

D: Original:
STRANGER: The arts which do not manufacture the actual thing, but which furnish the necessary tools for the manufacture, without which the several arts could not fulfil their appointed work, are co-operative; but those which make the things themselves are causal.

YOUNG SOCRATES: A very reasonable distinction.

STRANGER: Thus the arts which make spindles, combs, and other instruments of the production of clothes, may be called co-operative, and those which treat and fabricate the things themselves, causal.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Very true.

STRANGER: The arts of washing and mending, and the other preparatory arts which belong to the causal class, and form a division of the great art of adornment, may be all comprehended under what we call the fuller's art.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Very good.

STRANGER: Carding and spinning threads and all the parts of the process which are concerned with the actual manufacture of a woollen garment form a single art, which is one of those universally acknowledged,—the art of working in wool.

YOUNG SOCRATES: To be sure.

STRANGER: Of working in wool, again, there are two divisions, and both these are parts of two arts at once.

YOUNG SOCRATES: How is that?

STRANGER: Carding and one half of the use of the comb, and the other processes of wool-working which separate the composite, may be classed together as belonging both to the art of wool-working, and also to one of the two great arts which are of universal application—the art of composition and the art of division.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Yes.

STRANGER: To the latter belong carding and the other processes of which I was just now speaking; the art of discernment or division in wool and yarn, which is effected in one manner with the comb and in another with the hands, is variously described under all the names which I just now mentioned.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Very true.

STRANGER: Again, let us take some process of wool-working which is also a portion of the art of composition, and, dismissing the elements of division which we found there, make two halves, one on the principle of composition, and the other on the principle of division.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Let that be done.

STRANGER: And once more, Socrates, we must divide the part which belongs at once both to wool-working and composition, if we are ever to discover satisfactorily the aforesaid art of weaving.

YOUNG SOCRATES: We must.
D: Narrative:
The Stranger defined the distinction. Cooperative arts are those that do not manufacture the actual product but furnish the necessary tools without which the principal art could not do its work. Principal or causal arts are those that make the things themselves. Thus, the arts that make spindles, combs, and other instruments are cooperative, while those that treat and fabricate the cloth itself are causal.

Within the causal arts, the Stranger identified a further subdivision. The arts of washing, mending, and other preparatory work belong to the causal class but form a division he called the fuller's art -- the art of adornment or finishing. Carding and spinning, along with the other processes of actual manufacture, constitute a single universally recognized art: the art of working in wool.

But the art of working in wool itself has two divisions, and each belongs to two broader arts simultaneously. Carding and the separating processes belong both to wool-working and to the universal art of division. The combining processes belong both to wool-working and to the universal art of composition.

The Stranger then proposed to further divide the compositional side of wool-working, since within it lay the specific art of weaving that they sought. Young Socrates agreed this division must be made.
D: Refashioned:
The distinction is now formalized. Cooperative arts make the tools. Causal arts make the product. Spindle-makers and comb-makers are cooperative. Cloth-fabricators are causal.

Within the causal arts, the Stranger identifies further structure. Washing, mending, and finishing belong to the fuller's art -- they treat cloth after it is made. Carding and spinning, along with the actual manufacturing processes, constitute the art of working in wool.

But wool-working contains an internal tension. Some of its processes separate (carding pulls fibers apart) while others combine (spinning twists them together). These two operations belong to two universal arts that run through all of human activity: the art of division and the art of composition.

Weaving lies on the composition side of wool-working. To isolate it, they must divide the compositional processes further -- separating twisting from the final interlacing that is weaving itself.
D: Elegant: Tool-making enables; cloth-making creates. Within creation, some processes separate and some combine. Division and composition are universal arts running through all human activity. Weaving is the specific form of composition that interlaces warp and woof.
D: Summary: The Stranger distinguishes cooperative arts (tool-making) from causal arts (cloth-making), then subdivides the causal arts. Wool-working contains both dividing processes (carding) and combining processes (spinning, weaving). Weaving must be isolated as a specific form of composition.
D: Distilled: Every art of making contains two universal operations -- division and composition -- and the essential art is defined by which operation constitutes its core act.
Notes: The universal arts of division (diairesis) and composition (synthesis) are not just craft categories -- they are the fundamental operations of Platonic dialectic itself. The Sophist (253d-e) identifies the philosopher as one who can divide by kinds and compose them correctly. Here, these logical operations are shown to have material analogues in craft production. -- The nested classification (cooperative/causal, then division/composition within causal) demonstrates the method of division applied to itself -- a characteristic move of Plato's later dialectical works. -- Fulling (gnapheion) involved cleaning, thickening, and finishing woven cloth through processes like washing, beating, and brushing. It was a distinct trade in Athens, often conducted in separate workshops.

D: Original:
STRANGER: Yes, certainly, and let us call one part of the art the art of twisting threads, the other the art of combining them.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Do I understand you, in speaking of twisting, to be referring to manufacture of the warp?

STRANGER: Yes, and of the woof too; how, if not by twisting, is the woof made?

YOUNG SOCRATES: There is no other way.

STRANGER: Then suppose that you define the warp and the woof, for I think that the definition will be of use to you.

YOUNG SOCRATES: How shall I define them?

STRANGER: As thus: A piece of carded wool which is drawn out lengthwise and breadthwise is said to be pulled out.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Yes.

STRANGER: And the wool thus prepared, when twisted by the spindle, and made into a firm thread, is called the warp, and the art which regulates these operations the art of spinning the warp.

YOUNG SOCRATES: True.

STRANGER: And the threads which are more loosely spun, having a softness proportioned to the intertexture of the warp and to the degree of force used in dressing the cloth,—the threads which are thus spun are called the woof, and the art which is set over them may be called the art of spinning the woof.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Very true.

STRANGER: And, now, there can be no mistake about the nature of the part of weaving which we have undertaken to define. For when that part of the art of composition which is employed in the working of wool forms a web by the regular intertexture of warp and woof, the entire woven substance is called by us a woollen garment, and the art which presides over this is the art of weaving.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Very true.

STRANGER: But why did we not say at once that weaving is the art of entwining warp and woof, instead of making a long and useless circuit?

YOUNG SOCRATES: I thought, Stranger, that there was nothing useless in what was said.
D: Narrative:
The Stranger proposed calling one part of the compositional art the art of twisting threads, and the other the art of combining them. Young Socrates asked whether twisting referred specifically to making the warp, and the Stranger confirmed it applied to both warp and woof -- both are made by twisting.

The Stranger then asked Young Socrates to define the warp and the woof. Carded wool drawn out lengthwise and breadthwise is said to be pulled out. When this prepared wool is twisted by the spindle into a firm thread, it becomes the warp, and the art governing this is the art of spinning the warp. The threads spun more loosely, with softness proportioned to the intertexture required and the force used in dressing the cloth, are called the woof, and the art governing them is the art of spinning the woof.

With these definitions in place, the Stranger arrived at the definition of weaving proper. When the art of composition employed in wool-working forms a web by the regular interlacing of warp and woof, the entire woven substance is a woolen garment, and the art presiding over this is the art of weaving.

Young Socrates asked why they had not simply said this from the start -- that weaving is the art of interlacing warp and woof -- rather than making such a long circuit. The Stranger's implicit answer was that the circuit was the point: only by systematically separating weaving from every cooperative and kindred art could the definition achieve the precision required to serve as a model for statesmanship.
D: Refashioned:
The final division separates twisting from combining. Twisting produces the raw threads -- both warp and woof. The warp is carded wool twisted tightly on a spindle into a firm thread. The woof is spun more loosely, with softness calibrated to the interlacing it must undergo.

Weaving proper is neither the twisting nor the carding nor the fulling. It is the specific act of interlacing warp and woof into a unified fabric. That is the definition.

Young Socrates asks the obvious question: why not just say that from the beginning? Why the elaborate circuit through every related and cooperative art?

Because the circuit is the point. Saying 'weaving is interlacing warp and woof' is true but uninformative. It does not tell you what weaving is not. It does not separate weaving from the dozens of arts that contribute to it, resemble it, or claim its territory. Only the full division achieves that.

This is exactly the method they will now apply to the statesman. The political art is not just 'caring for citizens.' It is a specific act of combination -- weaving together different kinds of people, different virtues, different functions -- that must be isolated from every cooperative and rival art that touches political life.
D: Elegant: Weaving is the interlacing of warp and woof. Simple to say, laborious to define -- because a true definition must exclude everything that is not the thing. This is the method that will now be applied to statesmanship: not what it is called, but what it alone does that nothing else can.
D: Summary: Weaving is defined as the specific act of interlacing firm warp threads with soft woof threads into a unified fabric. The elaborate process of reaching this definition by excluding all cooperative and kindred arts was necessary because it provides the model for how statesmanship must be similarly isolated and defined.
D: Distilled: A true definition does not just name what something is -- it systematically excludes everything it is not, and this method of exclusion is the model for defining statesmanship itself.
Notes: The warp (stemon) and woof (kroke) distinction carries political significance that becomes explicit later in the dialogue (305e-311c): the warp corresponds to the courageous, firm character traits in citizens, while the woof corresponds to the temperate, gentle traits. The statesman's art is to interlace these into a stable civic fabric. -- Young Socrates' question ('Why not just say that from the beginning?') voices the reader's impatience and gives Plato the opportunity to justify the method of division. The answer is that quick definitions are vulnerable to the rival-claimant problem: anyone can say they 'care for citizens,' but only the true statesman performs the specific integrative act. -- The warp/woof technical description reflects actual Greek wool-working: the warp was tightly spun for strength (it bears the tension of the loom), while the woof was loosely spun for flexibility (it must be threaded through the warp). -- This passage completes the weaving paradigm. The remainder of the dialogue will apply this model to politics, defining the statesman as the one who weaves together the different character types and social functions into a unified state.

D: Original:
STRANGER: Very likely, but you may not always think so, my sweet friend; and in case any feeling of dissatisfaction should hereafter arise in your mind, as it very well may, let me lay down a principle which will apply to arguments in general.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Proceed.

STRANGER: Let us begin by considering the whole nature of excess and defect, and then we shall have a rational ground on which we may praise or blame too much length or too much shortness in discussions of this kind.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Let us do so.

STRANGER: The points on which I think that we ought to dwell are the following:—

YOUNG SOCRATES: What?

STRANGER: Length and shortness, excess and defect; with all of these the art of measurement is conversant.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Yes.

STRANGER: And the art of measurement has to be divided into two parts, with a view to our present purpose.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Where would you make the division?

STRANGER: As thus: I would make two parts, one having regard to the relativity of greatness and smallness to each other; and there is another, without which the existence of production would be impossible.

YOUNG SOCRATES: What do you mean?

STRANGER: Do you not think that it is only natural for the greater to be called greater with reference to the less alone, and the less less with reference to the greater alone?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Yes.

STRANGER: Well, but is there not also something exceeding and exceeded by the principle of the mean, both in speech and action, and is not this a reality, and the chief mark of difference between good and bad men?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Plainly.

STRANGER: Then we must suppose that the great and small exist and are discerned in both these ways, and not, as we were saying before, only relatively to one another, but there must also be another comparison of them with the mean or ideal standard; would you like to hear the reason why?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly.

STRANGER: If we assume the greater to exist only in relation to the less, there will never be any comparison of either with the mean.

YOUNG SOCRATES: True.
D: Narrative: The Stranger warns Young Socrates that a feeling of dissatisfaction about the length of their discussions may arise, and proposes laying down a principle to address it. He suggests they begin by considering the whole nature of excess and deficiency, so they will have a rational basis for judging whether any discussion is too long or too short. The art of measurement, he observes, is concerned with length, shortness, excess, and deficiency in all their forms. But this art must be divided into two parts. One part deals with the relative comparison of greatness and smallness to each other — the greater is called greater only in reference to the less, and the less only in reference to the greater. But there is another kind of measurement: things can exceed or fall short of an objective standard, a principle of the mean, both in speech and action. This, the Stranger insists, is a reality, and the chief mark of difference between good and bad people. The great and small therefore exist in two ways — not merely relative to one another, but also in comparison with a mean or ideal standard. If we assume the greater exists only in relation to the less, there will never be any comparison of either with the mean.
D: Refashioned:
Before going further, the Stranger introduces a principle that applies to all arguments, not just this one. We need to understand what it actually means for something to be "too much" or "too little" — whether we're talking about the length of a discussion, the scope of a policy, or the ambition of a project.

There are two fundamentally different kinds of measurement. The first is relative: something is bigger or smaller only compared to something else. A speech is long compared to a short one; a building is tall compared to its neighbor. This kind of measurement never tells you whether anything is the right size — only that it differs from something else.

The second kind of measurement compares things to an objective standard: the fitting, the appropriate, the mean. This is how we actually distinguish good work from bad. Something is excessive not merely because it's larger than something else, but because it overshoots what the situation requires. It falls short not because it's smaller than an alternative, but because it fails to meet the standard the task demands.

If we only had relative measurement — more and less compared to each other — we could never identify what is genuinely appropriate. And that distinction is what separates competent people from incompetent ones.
D: Elegant: There are two ways to measure anything. You can compare it to something else — longer, shorter, more, less. Or you can measure it against what the situation actually requires. The first tells you about difference. The second tells you about excellence. Without the second kind, you can rank things but never know if any of them are right.
D: Summary: Measurement has two forms: relative comparison (greater vs. lesser) and comparison against an objective standard of appropriateness (the mean). Only the second kind lets us judge whether something is genuinely good or bad, rather than merely different from something else.
D: Distilled: Knowing that something is bigger or smaller than something else tells you nothing about whether it is the right size.
Notes: The distinction between relative measurement and measurement against 'the mean' (to metrion) is one of the most philosophically significant passages in the Statesman. It anticipates Aristotle's doctrine of the mean in the Nicomachean Ethics (Book II), where virtue is defined as hitting the mean between excess and deficiency. Plato's 'mean' here is connected to the Form of the Good — an objective standard independent of comparative relations. The Stranger references the discussion of not-being in the Sophist (241d-242a), where they similarly had to establish an ontological point to save the inquiry.

D: Original:
STRANGER: And would not this doctrine be the ruin of all the arts and their creations; would not the art of the Statesman and the aforesaid art of weaving disappear? For all these arts are on the watch against excess and defect, not as unrealities, but as real evils, which occasion a difficulty in action; and the excellence or beauty of every work of art is due to this observance of measure.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly.

STRANGER: But if the science of the Statesman disappears, the search for the royal science will be impossible.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Very true.
D: Narrative: The Stranger argues that without measurement against a standard, all arts and their creations would be ruined. The art of the statesman and the art of weaving alike depend on guarding against excess and deficiency — not as mere abstractions, but as real evils that create difficulty in action. The excellence and beauty of every work of art is due to this observance of measure. And if the science of the statesman disappears for lack of such a standard, the search for the royal science becomes impossible.
D: Refashioned:
Without this second kind of measurement, every skill and craft collapses. Statesmanship, weaving, medicine — all of them work by identifying what is excessive and what is deficient relative to what the task requires. The quality of any product, any policy, any decision comes from hitting the right measure. Excess and deficiency are not theoretical problems; they are practical obstacles that produce real failure.

If we cannot establish that measurement against a standard exists, then political science itself becomes impossible to define or practice.
D: Elegant: Every art is a war against too much and too little. The excellence of any creation — a garment, a law, a decision — lies in finding the measure the situation demands. Lose that standard, and you lose the art itself.
D: Summary: All arts, including statesmanship, depend on measuring against an objective standard of appropriateness. Without this kind of measurement, the excellence of any craft or creation cannot exist, and political science becomes impossible.
D: Distilled: Every art exists to find the right measure — lose the standard and you lose the craft.
Notes: The claim that 'the excellence or beauty of every work of art is due to this observance of measure' connects to Plato's broader metaphysics of the Good as a principle of order and proportion. See also Philebus 64d-65a, where the Good is analyzed into measure (metron), proportion (symmetria), and beauty. The interdependence of the arts and measurement creates a mutual entailment: if arts exist, measurement against a standard must exist; if such measurement exists, arts are possible.

D: Original:
STRANGER: Well, then, as in the case of the Sophist we extorted the inference that not-being had an existence, because here was the point at which the argument eluded our grasp, so in this we must endeavour to show that the greater and less are not only to be measured with one another, but also have to do with the production of the mean; for if this is not admitted, neither a statesman nor any other man of action can be an undisputed master of his science.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Yes, we must certainly do again what we did then.

STRANGER: But this, Socrates, is a greater work than the other, of which we only too well remember the length. I think, however, that we may fairly assume something of this sort—

YOUNG SOCRATES: What?
D: Narrative: The Stranger draws an analogy to their earlier investigation in the Sophist. Just as they had to establish that not-being has some form of existence in order to save their argument about the Sophist, they must now establish that the greater and less are not only measured against each other but also have to do with the production of the mean. Without this, no statesman or person of action can be an undisputed master of their science. Young Socrates agrees this work must be done. The Stranger acknowledges it would be even more demanding than the Sophist inquiry, but proposes they may fairly assume something for now rather than undertaking the full demonstration.
D: Refashioned:
The Stranger recognizes they face the same kind of foundational problem they confronted in the Sophist. There, the argument stalled until they could show that not-being has a kind of existence. Here, the inquiry into statesmanship stalls unless they can establish that measurement against a standard — the production of the mean — is real, not just measurement of things against each other.

Without this, no practitioner of any art can claim genuine mastery. But the full proof would be enormous — larger even than the investigation of not-being. So the Stranger proposes a working assumption instead of a complete demonstration.
D: Elegant: Every serious inquiry eventually hits a wall that requires establishing something fundamental. In the Sophist, it was the reality of not-being. Here, it is the reality of an objective standard. Until you prove the measure exists, you cannot prove the measurer knows anything.
D: Summary: Just as the Sophist dialogue required establishing the existence of not-being, the Statesman requires establishing that measurement against an objective standard (the mean) is real. Without this, no practical art can claim genuine expertise. The full proof is deferred.
D: Distilled: You cannot define expertise until you establish that there is an objective standard to be expert about.
Notes: The reference is to Sophist 237a-241d, where the Eleatic Stranger (the same character) had to argue against Parmenides' dictum that 'not-being cannot be' in order to define the Sophist as someone who produces false appearances. The parallel structure — both dialogues requiring an ontological detour to save a definitional inquiry — is a deliberate architectural feature of Plato's late dialogues. The 'greater work' the Stranger defers may refer to the unwritten doctrines or the projected but never-completed dialogue Philosopher.

D: Original: STRANGER: That we shall some day require this notion of a mean with a view to the demonstration of absolute truth; meanwhile, the argument that the very existence of the arts must be held to depend on the possibility of measuring more or less, not only with one another, but also with a view to the attainment of the mean, seems to afford a grand support and satisfactory proof of the doctrine which we are maintaining; for if there are arts, there is a standard of measure, and if there is a standard of measure, there are arts; but if either is wanting, there is neither.
D: Narrative: The Stranger proposes a provisional argument. While they will someday need the notion of a mean for a demonstration of absolute truth, for now they can rely on a more accessible proof: the very existence of the arts depends on the possibility of measuring not only relative to one another, but also with a view to attaining the mean. This creates a reciprocal relationship — if there are arts, there must be a standard of measure; if there is a standard of measure, there must be arts. If either is absent, neither exists.
D: Refashioned:
The full metaphysical proof can wait. For now, a simpler argument suffices: arts exist. We can see them, practice them, evaluate their products. But arts can only exist if there is a standard of measure — something beyond mere relative comparison — that practitioners aim at. And a standard of measure can only be meaningful if there are arts that use it.

This mutual dependence is the key insight. The existence of skilled practice proves the existence of objective standards. The existence of objective standards is what makes skilled practice possible. Deny one and you lose both.
D: Elegant: The proof is circular in the best sense: arts exist because standards exist, and standards exist because arts exist. You cannot have craft without measure, or measure without craft. Each proves the other.
D: Summary: The existence of the arts proves the existence of objective measurement standards, and vice versa. If either is absent, neither can exist. This reciprocal relationship serves as a practical proof that measurement against the mean is real.
D: Distilled: The existence of any genuine skill is proof that an objective standard of excellence exists.
Notes: This passage presents what philosophers call a 'transcendental argument' — reasoning from the existence of a phenomenon (the arts) to the necessary conditions of its possibility (objective standards of measure). The deferred 'demonstration of absolute truth' may refer to the dialectical method described in Republic VI-VII or to the unwritten doctrines on the One and the Indefinite Dyad that Aristotle attributes to Plato (Metaphysics I.6). The reciprocal entailment between arts and measure anticipates modern arguments about the objectivity of value.

D: Original:
YOUNG SOCRATES: True; and what is the next step?

STRANGER: The next step clearly is to divide the art of measurement into two parts, as we have said already, and to place in the one part all the arts which measure number, length, depth, breadth, swiftness with their opposites; and to have another part in which they are measured with the mean, and the fit, and the opportune, and the due, and with all those words, in short, which denote a mean or standard removed from the extremes.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Here are two vast divisions, embracing two very different spheres.
D: Narrative: The Stranger now formally divides the art of measurement into two parts. The first includes all arts that measure number, length, depth, breadth, and swiftness against their opposites — purely relative measurement. The second part contains those arts that measure against the mean, the fit, the opportune, and the due — all words denoting a standard removed from the extremes. Young Socrates recognizes these as two vast and very different spheres.
D: Refashioned:
The division is now explicit. One branch of measurement compares quantities against each other: longer versus shorter, faster versus slower, more versus less. This is the measurement of mathematics and physics.

The other branch measures against qualitative standards: the fitting, the timely, the appropriate, the due. This is the measurement of craft, ethics, and governance. Every art that produces something good — whether a garment, a speech, or a political arrangement — operates in this second sphere, aiming not at a quantity but at what the situation requires.
D: Elegant: Mathematics measures things against each other. Craft measures things against what they ought to be. The first gives you numbers. The second gives you excellence.
D: Summary: The art of measurement divides into two branches: quantitative comparison (greater vs. lesser) and qualitative judgment (against the fitting, the timely, the due). All productive and practical arts operate in the second sphere.
D: Distilled: Quantitative measurement tells you how much; qualitative measurement tells you how good.
Notes: The terms used for the second kind of measurement — to metrion (the measure), to prepon (the fitting), to kairion (the opportune/timely), to deon (the due/necessary) — form a cluster of evaluative concepts central to Greek ethics. Kairos (the opportune moment) was especially important in rhetoric and medicine. The distinction between these two kinds of measurement maps roughly onto the modern distinction between descriptive and normative inquiry, or between positive and normative science.

D: Original: STRANGER: There are many accomplished men, Socrates, who say, believing themselves to speak wisely, that the art of measurement is universal, and has to do with all things. And this means what we are now saying; for all things which come within the province of art do certainly in some sense partake of measure. But these persons, because they are not accustomed to distinguish classes according to real forms, jumble together two widely different things, relation to one another, and to a standard, under the idea that they are the same, and also fall into the converse error of dividing other things not according to their real parts. Whereas the right way is, if a man has first seen the unity of things, to go on with the enquiry and not desist until he has found all the differences contained in it which form distinct classes; nor again should he be able to rest contented with the manifold diversities which are seen in a multitude of things until he has comprehended all of them that have any affinity within the bounds of one similarity and embraced them within the reality of a single kind. But we have said enough on this head, and also of excess and defect; we have only to bear in mind that two divisions of the art of measurement have been discovered which are concerned with them, and not forget what they are.
D: Narrative: The Stranger observes that many accomplished people claim the art of measurement is universal and applies to all things. They are partly right — all things within the province of art do partake of measure. But these thinkers make a critical error: unaccustomed to distinguishing classes according to their real forms, they conflate two fundamentally different things — relative measurement and measurement against a standard — under the assumption that they are the same. They also commit the opposite error, dividing things that should not be divided. The right method, the Stranger insists, is to start from the unity of things and pursue all the real differences within that unity until every distinct class is found; and conversely, when confronted with diversity, to seek the underlying similarity that unites disparate things within a single kind. With this principle established, they can move forward, keeping both divisions of measurement in mind.
D: Refashioned:
Many intelligent people say measurement is universal — and they are right that everything involving skill partakes of measure. But they make a fundamental error. Because they are not trained in proper classification, they lump together two completely different operations: comparing things to each other and comparing things to a standard. They treat these as the same activity. The reverse mistake is equally common — splitting apart things that actually belong together.

The correct method is dialectical. When you see unity, keep dividing until you find every genuine distinction it contains. When you see multiplicity, keep looking for the real similarity that binds apparently different things into a single kind. Classification is not about imposing neat categories; it is about discovering the joints where reality actually divides.

With both kinds of measurement now distinguished, they can proceed.
D: Elegant: The intellectual error of the age: lumping different things together because they share a surface feature, or splitting similar things apart because they look different. Real thinking means carving at the joints — finding every genuine distinction within unity, and every genuine unity within diversity.
D: Summary: Many thinkers wrongly conflate relative measurement with measurement against a standard because they fail to classify by real forms. The correct method requires finding all genuine distinctions within apparent unity and all genuine unity within apparent diversity.
D: Distilled: Bad thinking conflates what is merely similar; good thinking discovers where reality actually divides.
Notes: The method described here — finding unity in diversity and diversity in unity — is Plato's mature dialectic, also described in Philebus 16c-17a and Sophist 253d-e. The metaphor of 'carving at the joints' comes from Phaedrus 265e. The criticism of thinkers who 'jumble together' different things under one category targets both empiricists who classify by surface resemblance and sophists who exploit ambiguity. This passage is one of Plato's clearest statements of his method of collection and division (synagoge and diairesis).

D: Original:
YOUNG SOCRATES: We will not forget.

STRANGER: And now that this discussion is completed, let us go on to consider another question, which concerns not this argument only but the conduct of such arguments in general.

YOUNG SOCRATES: What is this new question?

STRANGER: Take the case of a child who is engaged in learning his letters: when he is asked what letters make up a word, should we say that the question is intended to improve his grammatical knowledge of that particular word, or of all words?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Clearly, in order that he may have a better knowledge of all words.

STRANGER: And is our enquiry about the Statesman intended only to improve our knowledge of politics, or our power of reasoning generally?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Clearly, as in the former example, the purpose is general.
D: Narrative: The Stranger shifts to a broader methodological point. When a child learns letters, the purpose of asking which letters compose a particular word is not to learn about that word alone, but to become better at recognizing letters in all words. Similarly, the inquiry into the statesman is not merely about political knowledge — it is about improving the capacity for reasoning in general. Young Socrates agrees that, as with the letters example, the purpose of their investigation is general intellectual development, not just the specific definition they seek.
D: Refashioned:
The Stranger steps back to explain why the inquiry has taken so many apparent detours. When children learn to read, the teacher does not ask them to spell a particular word for the sake of that word alone. The exercise builds a general capacity — the ability to recognize letter patterns in any word they encounter.

This investigation works the same way. The immediate goal is to define the statesman, but the real purpose is to develop the method of thinking itself. Every distinction they draw, every division they practice, sharpens a skill that transfers to every other inquiry. The statesman is the word they are spelling; dialectic is the literacy they are building.
D: Elegant: A child spells a word not to learn the word, but to learn to read. We define the statesman not to master politics, but to master thinking. The subject is the exercise; the method is the prize.
D: Summary: The inquiry into the statesman serves a broader purpose than just defining political knowledge. Like a child learning letters by spelling specific words, the investigation develops the general capacity for dialectical reasoning that applies to all subjects.
D: Distilled: The subject of an inquiry is the exercise; the capacity for reasoning it builds is the real achievement.
Notes: The letters analogy appears also in Sophist 253a and Theaetetus 206a-b. It illustrates Plato's view that dialectic — the method of collection and division — is a general intellectual capacity, not domain-specific knowledge. This is related to the philosopher's training described in Republic VII (531d-534e), where individual mathematical sciences serve as preparation for dialectic. The passage also anticipates modern theories of 'transfer learning' — the idea that practicing reasoning in one domain improves reasoning ability generally.

D: Original:
STRANGER: Still less would any rational man seek to analyse the notion of weaving for its own sake.
