But people seem to forget that some things have sensible images, which are readily known, and can be easily pointed out when any one desires to answer an enquirer without any trouble or argument; whereas the greatest and highest truths have no outward image of themselves visible to man, which he who wishes to satisfy the soul of the enquirer can adapt to the eye of sense (compare Phaedr.), and therefore we ought to train ourselves to give and accept a rational account of them; for immaterial things, which are the noblest and greatest, are shown only in thought and idea, and in no other way, and all that we are now saying is said for the sake of them. Moreover, there is always less difficulty in fixing the mind on small matters than on great.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Very good.

STRANGER: Let us call to mind the bearing of all this.

YOUNG SOCRATES: What is it?
D: Narrative: The Stranger explains why they used the example of weaving rather than proceeding directly. Some things have sensible images that can be easily pointed out when someone wants an explanation. But the greatest and highest truths have no visible form that can satisfy the inquiring mind through sense perception alone. These immaterial things — the noblest and greatest realities — can only be grasped through thought and rational account. Everything in their discussion, including the weaving analogy, has been undertaken for the sake of these higher realities. It is always easier to fix the mind on small, concrete matters than on great abstract ones, so examples drawn from humble crafts serve as stepping stones to understanding the immaterial truths they seek.
D: Refashioned:
Why spend so much time on weaving? Because the most important realities have no physical form you can point to. You cannot hold up statesmanship and show it to someone the way you can hold up a shuttle or a loom.

Concrete, tangible things are easy to explain — you can just show them. But the concepts that matter most — justice, governance, the fitting measure — exist only in thought. They have no image you can display to satisfy someone who wants a quick answer. The only way to grasp them is through careful reasoning.

This is why examples from humble crafts are essential. The mind fixes more easily on small, concrete things than on vast abstractions. Weaving is the sensible model they can see and touch, used to train the capacity they will need when they turn to the statesman — something that can only be shown through thought.
D: Elegant: The most important truths are invisible. You cannot hold up justice or point to governance. The only path to understanding them is through careful reasoning, and the only way to begin reasoning carefully is to practice on things you can see and touch. That is what the weaving example was for.
D: Summary: The greatest truths have no physical form and can only be grasped through rational thought. Examples from tangible crafts like weaving serve as training ground for the mind, preparing it to understand immaterial realities like statesmanship and justice that cannot be shown to the senses.
D: Distilled: The most important things can only be shown through thought, so we practice thinking on things we can see.
Notes: The contrast between sensible images and immaterial truths accessible only through reason is a core Platonic doctrine, expressed most famously in the Allegory of the Cave (Republic 514a-520a) and the Divided Line (Republic 509d-511e). The parenthetical reference to the Phaedrus connects to Phaedrus 250b-d, where Plato argues that Beauty is the only Form that has a visible reflection in the sensible world. The method of using concrete analogies to approach abstract truths is what modern philosophers call analogical reasoning or model-based reasoning.

D: Original: STRANGER: I wanted to get rid of any impression of tediousness which we may have experienced in the discussion about weaving, and the reversal of the universe, and in the discussion concerning the Sophist and the being of not-being. I know that they were felt to be too long, and I reproached myself with this, fearing that they might be not only tedious but irrelevant; and all that I have now said is only designed to prevent the recurrence of any such disagreeables for the future.
D: Narrative: The Stranger explains his motivation for the methodological digression. He wanted to address any impression of tediousness from their earlier discussions — the weaving example, the cosmic myth about the reversal of the universe, and the investigation in the Sophist concerning the being of not-being. He acknowledges these were felt to be too long, and he reproached himself for making them potentially tedious and irrelevant. Everything he has now said about measurement and method is designed to prevent such dissatisfaction from recurring by establishing the principle that length should be judged against what is fitting, not merely against other discussions.
D: Refashioned:
The Stranger admits this digression on measurement has a practical motive. He is aware that their earlier detours — the weaving analysis, the cosmic myth, the investigation of not-being in the Sophist — were experienced as overly long. He worried they might seem not just tedious but pointless.

The entire discussion of measurement was designed to prevent that reaction from recurring. Now they have a principle: judge the length of an argument not by comparing it to other arguments, but by whether it serves the purpose it was meant to serve. With that standard in place, apparent digressions can be evaluated honestly rather than simply resented.
D: Elegant: The digression on measurement was itself a defense of digressions. If you have learned to judge length not by impatience but by purpose, then no necessary argument is too long, and no unnecessary one is short enough.
D: Summary: The Stranger's discussion of measurement was motivated by awareness that earlier digressions seemed tedious. By establishing that length should be judged against purpose rather than mere comparison, he provides a principle for evaluating whether any discussion is genuinely too long or appropriately thorough.
D: Distilled: A discussion is too long only when it exceeds its purpose, not when it exceeds your patience.
Notes: The 'reversal of the universe' refers to the Cosmic Myth in Statesman 268d-274e, where the Stranger describes alternating cosmic cycles. The 'being of not-being' refers to Sophist 237-259. The Stranger's self-awareness about length is a recurring feature of Plato's later dialogues, which are conspicuously longer and more methodologically self-conscious than the early Socratic dialogues. This passage is also a subtle defense of Plato's own literary method against critics who found the later dialogues tedious compared to the dramatic early works.

D: Original:
YOUNG SOCRATES: Very good. Will you proceed?

STRANGER: Then I would like to observe that you and I, remembering what has been said, should praise or blame the length or shortness of discussions, not by comparing them with one another, but with what is fitting, having regard to the part of measurement, which, as we said, was to be borne in mind.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Very true.
D: Narrative: The Stranger applies the principle they have just established. He and Young Socrates should praise or blame the length or shortness of discussions not by comparing them with one another, but by reference to what is fitting — using the second kind of measurement, the one concerned with the mean rather than mere relative comparison.
D: Refashioned:
The principle is now applied directly: when they judge whether any part of their investigation was the right length, they should not compare it to other discussions. They should ask whether it achieved what it needed to achieve. The standard is fitness for purpose, not relative brevity or length.

This is the second kind of measurement in action — evaluating against the appropriate, not against an arbitrary comparison.
D: Elegant: Do not ask whether a discussion was longer or shorter than another. Ask whether it accomplished what it needed to. That is the only measure that matters.
D: Summary: The principle of measurement against the mean is applied directly: discussions should be judged by whether they achieve their purpose, not by comparison with other discussions.
D: Distilled: Judge arguments by whether they hit their mark, not by how they compare to other arguments.
Notes: This passage closes the loop on the measurement digression by demonstrating its practical application. It models the very principle it teaches — the digression on measurement was itself measured against the standard of what the argument required, not against the length of other sections. This self-referential structure is characteristic of Plato's philosophical method.

D: Original:
STRANGER: And yet, not everything is to be judged even with a view to what is fitting; for we should only want such a length as is suited to give pleasure, if at all, as a secondary matter; and reason tells us, that we should be contented to make the ease or rapidity of an enquiry, not our first, but our second object; the first and highest of all being to assert the great method of division according to species—whether the discourse be shorter or longer is not to the point. No offence should be taken at length, but the longer and shorter are to be employed indifferently, according as either of them is better calculated to sharpen the wits of the auditors. Reason would also say to him who censures the length of discourses on such occasions and cannot away with their circumlocution, that he should not be in such a hurry to have done with them, when he can only complain that they are tedious, but he should prove that if they had been shorter they would have made those who took part in them better dialecticians, and more capable of expressing the truth of things; about any other praise and blame, he need not trouble himself—he should pretend not to hear them. But we have had enough of this, as you will probably agree with me in thinking. Let us return to our Statesman, and apply to his case the aforesaid example of weaving.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Very good;—let us do as you say.
D: Narrative: The Stranger adds a final qualification. Not everything should be judged solely by what is fitting. Pleasure in a discussion, if it arises, is a secondary consideration, not a primary one. The ease or speed of an inquiry should also be secondary. The first and highest priority is to assert the great method of division according to species. Whether a discourse is shorter or longer is beside the point; no offense should be taken at length. The longer and shorter should be employed as needed, depending on which better sharpens the minds of those involved. Anyone who complains about the length of such discussions should not merely object that they are tedious — they should prove that shorter discussions would have produced better thinkers and more capable dialecticians. Beyond that, other praise and blame can be ignored. With this principle firmly established, the Stranger proposes they return to the statesman and apply the weaving example to his case.
D: Refashioned:
One more qualification. Pleasure and speed are legitimate but secondary goals. The highest priority is always the method itself — the practice of dividing things according to their real kinds. If a discussion needs to be long to develop that capacity, it should be long. If it can be short, let it be short. Length is a tool, not a virtue or a vice.

Anyone who objects to the length of an argument must do more than complain. They must demonstrate that a shorter treatment would have produced sharper thinkers and more capable reasoners. Mere impatience is not a critique.

With the methodological ground now cleared, the Stranger is ready to return to the statesman and apply the weaving model directly.
D: Elegant: The only legitimate complaint about a long argument is proof that a shorter one would have taught more. Impatience is not a method of evaluation. Pleasure is a secondary benefit of inquiry, never its purpose. The purpose is to learn to think by dividing reality at its joints.
D: Summary: Pleasure and efficiency in discussion are secondary to the primary goal: mastering the method of division according to real kinds. Length should serve the development of dialectical capacity. Anyone who objects to length must prove that brevity would have produced better reasoning, not merely less tedium.
D: Distilled: The purpose of inquiry is to sharpen the capacity for thinking, and no shortcut that dulls that capacity is worth taking.
Notes: The 'great method of division according to species' (diairesis kata eide) is Plato's mature dialectical method, central to the Sophist, Statesman, and Philebus. The claim that pleasure is secondary connects to the Philebus (66a-c), where pleasure ranks below measure, beauty, reason, and knowledge in the hierarchy of goods. The challenge to critics — prove that shorter would be better, not just more pleasant — anticipates modern debates about rigor vs. accessibility in philosophical writing.

D: Original:
STRANGER: The art of the king has been separated from the similar arts of shepherds, and, indeed, from all those which have to do with herds at all. There still remain, however, of the causal and co-operative arts those which are immediately concerned with States, and which must first be distinguished from one another.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Very good.

STRANGER: You know that these arts cannot easily be divided into two halves; the reason will be very evident as we proceed.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Then we had better do so.

STRANGER: We must carve them like a victim into members or limbs, since we cannot bisect them. (Compare Phaedr.) For we certainly should divide everything into as few parts as possible.

YOUNG SOCRATES: What is to be done in this case?

STRANGER: What we did in the example of weaving—all those arts which furnish the tools were regarded by us as co-operative.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Yes.

STRANGER: So now, and with still more reason, all arts which make any implement in a State, whether great or small, may be regarded by us as co-operative, for without them neither State nor Statesmanship would be possible; and yet we are not inclined to say that any of them is a product of the kingly art.

YOUNG SOCRATES: No, indeed.

STRANGER: The task of separating this class from others is not an easy one; for there is plausibility in saying that anything in the world is the instrument of doing something. But there is another class of possessions in a city, of which I have a word to say.

YOUNG SOCRATES: What class do you mean?

STRANGER: A class which may be described as not having this power; that is to say, not like an instrument, framed for production, but designed for the preservation of that which is produced.

YOUNG SOCRATES: To what do you refer?
D: Narrative: The Stranger begins separating the co-operative arts from the statesman's own art. The kingly art has been distinguished from herding, but there remain causal and co-operative arts immediately concerned with states that must be distinguished from one another. These cannot be divided neatly into two halves, so they must be carved into members — divided into as few parts as possible while respecting natural divisions. Following the weaving example, all arts that furnish tools were classified as co-operative. Now, all arts that make any implement in a state, great or small, are similarly co-operative — necessary for the state's existence, but not products of the kingly art itself. However, separating this class is difficult because almost anything could plausibly be called an instrument for doing something. The Stranger introduces a further distinction: beyond instruments of production, there is a class of possessions designed not to produce but to preserve what has been produced.
D: Refashioned:
Now the weaving model is applied to the state. In weaving, they separated the art itself from the co-operative arts that supply it — tool-making, carding, spinning. The same separation must be made for statesmanship.

The state requires an enormous range of arts and products: tools, buildings, weapons, food, clothing. All of these are necessary for the state to exist. But none of them are the statesman's own art, just as loom-making is not weaving.

The challenge is classification. Almost anything can be called a tool for something. So the Stranger begins with a basic distinction: some possessions are instruments of production — they help make things. Others are instruments of preservation — they protect and store what has already been made. Both are co-operative, both are necessary, and neither is the statesman's art.
D: Elegant: A city needs a thousand things to exist — tools, buildings, weapons, food. All are necessary; none are statesmanship. The statesman's art is not in producing any of these things, but in knowing how they all fit together. The first step in finding that art is cataloguing everything it is not.
D: Summary: Applying the weaving model to the state, the Stranger begins separating co-operative arts from the statesman's own art. All arts that produce tools and implements are necessary for the state but are not the kingly art. A further distinction separates instruments of production from instruments of preservation.
D: Distilled: The statesman needs every art but practices none of them — statesmanship is the art that organizes all the others.
Notes: The method of 'carving like a victim into members' (kata mele) rather than bisecting comes from Phaedrus 265e, where Socrates describes good dialectic as carving at the natural joints 'like a good butcher.' The distinction between productive instruments and preservative possessions anticipates Aristotle's distinction between poiesis (making) and praxis (doing) in Nicomachean Ethics VI. The seven-class taxonomy that follows is one of the earliest systematic attempts at political economy — classifying everything a city needs.

D: Original: STRANGER: To the class of vessels, as they are comprehensively termed, which are constructed for the preservation of things moist and dry, of things prepared in the fire or out of the fire; this is a very large class, and has, if I am not mistaken, literally nothing to do with the royal art of which we are in search.
D: Narrative: The Stranger identifies the first class of co-operative possessions: vessels. These are containers constructed for the preservation of things moist and dry, things prepared in fire or out of fire. It is a very large class, and it has, the Stranger emphasizes, literally nothing to do with the royal art they seek.
D: Refashioned: The first class is containers — vessels for storing liquids and solids, cooked and raw goods. This is a vast category encompassing everything from water jars to grain silos. Essential to civilization, entirely irrelevant to the statesman's art.
D: Elegant: Containers: everything that holds, stores, and preserves the material of life. A city cannot function without them, and a statesman has nothing to do with making them.
D: Summary: The first class of civic possessions is vessels and containers for preserving goods. Though a large and essential category, it has no connection to the royal art of statesmanship.
D: Distilled: Storage is essential to any civilization but is no part of governing it.
Notes: This begins the Stranger's seven-fold classification of civic possessions (9.2-9.7), which anticipates Aristotle's enumeration of the parts necessary for a city in Politics VII.8 (1328b). Aristotle lists food, arts/crafts, arms, revenue, religion, and judgment of disputes — a classification clearly influenced by this passage.

D: Original:
YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly not.

STRANGER: There is also a third class of possessions to be noted, different from these and very extensive, moving or resting on land or water, honourable and also dishonourable. The whole of this class has one name, because it is intended to be sat upon, being always a seat for something.

YOUNG SOCRATES: What is it?

STRANGER: A vehicle, which is certainly not the work of the Statesman, but of the carpenter, potter, and coppersmith.

YOUNG SOCRATES: I understand.
D: Narrative: The Stranger identifies a third class of possessions — distinct from both tools and vessels — which is extensive and includes things that move or rest on land or water, both honored and humble. This entire class has a single defining feature: it is intended to be sat upon, to serve as a seat or platform for something. The Stranger calls this class vehicles. These are the work of carpenters, potters, and coppersmiths, certainly not of the statesman.
D: Refashioned: The third class is vehicles — everything designed to carry or support, whether on land or water. Carts, ships, chairs, platforms. Some are prestigious, some mundane, but all share the same function: they are seats or carriers for something else. Made by carpenters, potters, and metalworkers. Not the statesman's domain.
D: Elegant: Vehicles: everything that carries, supports, or conveys. From thrones to cargo ships, the function is the same — bearing something from one place or state to another. The work of craftsmen, not rulers.
D: Summary: The third class of civic possessions is vehicles — everything designed for carrying or supporting, whether on land or water. These are produced by various craftsmen and are not part of the statesman's art.
D: Distilled: The art of conveyance is necessary for the city but belongs to craftsmen, not the statesman.
Notes: The broad definition of 'vehicle' (ochema) as anything 'intended to be sat upon' is characteristically Platonic in its pursuit of essential function over surface appearance — grouping thrones with cargo carts because both serve as supports. This methodological principle of classifying by function rather than material or prestige connects to the discussion of proper division in 8.6.

D: Original: STRANGER: And is there not a fourth class which is again different, and in which most of the things formerly mentioned are contained,—every kind of dress, most sorts of arms, walls and enclosures, whether of earth or stone, and ten thousand other things? all of which being made for the sake of defence, may be truly called defences, and are for the most part to be regarded as the work of the builder or of the weaver, rather than of the Statesman.
D: Narrative: The Stranger identifies a fourth class, different again, which contains many of the things previously mentioned. This class includes every kind of dress, most sorts of arms, walls and enclosures of earth or stone, and countless other things. All are made for the sake of defense and may truly be called defenses. These are primarily the work of builders and weavers, not the statesman.
D: Refashioned:
The fourth class is defenses — everything made to protect. This encompasses clothing, armor, walls, fortifications, enclosures of earth or stone, and countless other protective constructions. It is a broad category that overlaps with others, since many things serve both a primary function and a defensive one.

Defenses are the province of builders and weavers, not statesmen. The ruler may decide where a wall is built, but the wall itself belongs to the builder's art.
D: Elegant: Defenses: clothing, armor, walls, fortifications — everything that stands between people and harm. The builder raises the wall; the weaver makes the cloak. The statesman decides neither thread count nor stone placement.
D: Summary: The fourth class is defenses — including clothing, armor, walls, and fortifications. All are made for protection and belong to the arts of building and weaving, not to statesmanship.
D: Distilled: Protection is the purpose of a vast class of civic possessions, but providing it is the work of craftsmen, not rulers.
Notes: The inclusion of clothing as 'defense' rather than ornament reflects the Greek understanding of clothing's primary function as protection from elements. The weaving analogy comes full circle here — the literal art of weaving produces defenses (garments), just as the metaphorical art of political weaving produces the civic fabric. Arms and fortifications being listed alongside clothing anticipates the military dimension that becomes central in chapter 10.

D: Original:
YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly.

STRANGER: Shall we add a fifth class, of ornamentation and drawing, and of the imitations produced by drawing and music, which are designed for amusement only, and may be fairly comprehended under one name?

YOUNG SOCRATES: What is it?

STRANGER: Plaything is the name.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly.

STRANGER: That one name may be fitly predicated of all of them, for none of these things have a serious purpose—amusement is their sole aim.

YOUNG SOCRATES: That again I understand.

STRANGER: Then there is a class which provides materials for all these, out of which and in which the arts already mentioned fabricate their works;—this manifold class, I say, which is the creation and offspring of many other arts, may I not rank sixth?

YOUNG SOCRATES: What do you mean?
D: Narrative: The Stranger proposes a fifth class: ornamentation, drawing, and the imitations produced by drawing and music, all designed purely for amusement. He gives this class a single name — playthings. None of these things have a serious productive purpose; their sole aim is entertainment. He then introduces a sixth class: the materials from which all the other arts fabricate their works.
D: Refashioned:
The fifth class is playthings — ornamentation, visual art, music, and all forms of imitation designed purely for amusement. None of these serve a productive or protective purpose; their only aim is pleasure. This is a significant class in any city, but it has nothing to do with governance.

The sixth class is raw materials — gold, silver, metals, timber, fibers, bark, leather, cork, papyrus, cordage. Everything that the other arts use as their starting material. These are the primitive, simple possessions from which all composite things are manufactured.
D: Elegant: Playthings: art, music, ornament — everything made for delight rather than use. Raw materials: metal, wood, fiber, leather — everything that exists to become something else. Both essential to a city's life. Neither the statesman's concern.
D: Summary: The fifth class is playthings (art, music, entertainment), designed purely for amusement. The sixth class is raw materials (metals, wood, fibers) from which other arts fabricate their products. Neither class belongs to the statesman's art.
D: Distilled: Entertainment and raw materials are both necessary to civilization but irrelevant to the art of governing.
Notes: The classification of all art and music as 'playthings' (paignia) designed solely for amusement contrasts sharply with the Republic's treatment of art as politically dangerous and requiring censorship (Republic II-III, X). In the Statesman, art is merely irrelevant to governance rather than threatening. The sixth class — raw materials — represents what economists would later call primary commodities or factors of production. This six-class taxonomy is moving toward a complete inventory of a city's material needs.

D: Original: STRANGER: I am referring to gold, silver, and other metals, and all that wood-cutting and shearing of every sort provides for the art of carpentry and plaiting; and there is the process of barking and stripping the cuticle of plants, and the currier's art, which strips off the skins of animals, and other similar arts which manufacture corks and papyri and cords, and provide for the manufacture of composite species out of simple kinds—the whole class may be termed the primitive and simple possession of man, and with this the kingly science has no concern at all.
D: Narrative: The Stranger elaborates on the sixth class: gold, silver, and other metals; all that wood-cutting and shearing provides for carpentry and plaiting; the processes of barking and stripping plant fibers; the currier's art of stripping animal skins; and similar arts that manufacture corks, papyri, and cords. All of these provide for the manufacture of composite things out of simple materials. The whole class may be termed the primitive and simple possession of humankind, and the kingly science has no concern with it at all.
D: Refashioned:
The sixth class encompasses all raw materials and the basic processing that makes them usable: mining and smelting metals, cutting and shaping wood, harvesting plant fibers, tanning leather, making rope and paper. These are the foundational materials of civilization — the simple inputs from which every composite product is manufactured.

This is humanity's most primitive class of possession, and the kingly science has nothing to do with it.
D: Elegant: Before anything can be built, sewn, or forged, something must be mined, felled, stripped, or harvested. Raw materials are the foundation beneath every craft — and beneath the statesman's notice.
D: Summary: The sixth class includes all raw materials and basic processing: metals, timber, fibers, leather, paper, and cordage. These primitive possessions are the inputs for all other arts but have no connection to the royal science.
D: Distilled: The raw materials of civilization are its most fundamental possessions and the furthest removed from the art of governing.
Notes: The detailed enumeration of material processing — mining, wood-cutting, bark-stripping, tanning, rope-making, papyrus production — provides a snapshot of the ancient Greek economy's material base. The term 'primitive and simple possession' (prote kai hapla ktesis) distinguishes raw materials from manufactured goods, anticipating the distinction between primary and secondary economic sectors.

D: Original:
YOUNG SOCRATES: True.

STRANGER: The provision of food and of all other things which mingle their particles with the particles of the human body, and minister to the body, will form a seventh class, which may be called by the general term of nourishment, unless you have any better name to offer. This, however, appertains rather to the husbandman, huntsman, trainer, doctor, cook, and is not to be assigned to the Statesman's art.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly not.
D: Narrative: The Stranger identifies the seventh and final class: nourishment — food and all things that mingle with the human body and minister to it. This class belongs to the husbandman, huntsman, trainer, doctor, and cook, not to the statesman's art.
D: Refashioned:
The seventh class is nourishment — food and everything else that enters the body to sustain it. This belongs to farmers, hunters, trainers, doctors, and cooks. A city cannot survive without being fed, but feeding it is not governing it.

With this seventh class, the inventory is complete. Every material thing a city needs has been catalogued and assigned to the relevant craft.
D: Elegant: Nourishment: everything the body takes in to sustain itself. The farmer grows it, the doctor prescribes it, the cook prepares it. The statesman does none of these things, yet without all of them, there is no city to govern.
D: Summary: The seventh class is nourishment — food and bodily sustenance, belonging to farmers, doctors, and cooks. This completes the inventory of a city's material possessions, none of which fall under the statesman's art.
D: Distilled: A city must be fed before it can be governed, but feeding and governing are entirely different arts.
Notes: The seven classes — (1) tools/instruments, (2) vessels/containers, (3) vehicles, (4) defenses, (5) playthings/entertainment, (6) raw materials, (7) nourishment — constitute one of the earliest systematic classifications of economic goods. Aristotle's Politics VII.8 adapts this classification, listing six necessities of the city: food, arts, arms, revenue, care of religion, and decision of disputes. The inclusion of doctors and trainers under 'nourishment' reflects the Greek concept of dietetics (diaita) as total bodily regimen, not just eating.

D: Original: STRANGER: These seven classes include nearly every description of property, with the exception of tame animals. Consider;—there was the original material, which ought to have been placed first; next come instruments, vessels, vehicles, defences, playthings, nourishment; small things, which may be included under one of these—as for example, coins, seals and stamps, are omitted, for they have not in them the character of any larger kind which includes them; but some of them may, with a little forcing, be placed among ornaments, and others may be made to harmonize with the class of implements. The art of herding, which has been already divided into parts, will include all property in tame animals, except slaves.
D: Narrative: The Stranger summarizes: these seven classes include nearly every description of property, with the exception of tame animals. In order, there was: original material (which should have been placed first), then instruments, vessels, vehicles, defenses, playthings, and nourishment. Small things like coins, seals, and stamps have been omitted because they do not fit neatly into any larger category, though some might be placed under ornaments or implements. The art of herding, already divided into parts in an earlier discussion, covers all property in tame animals — except slaves.
D: Refashioned:
The Stranger reviews the taxonomy. Seven classes cover nearly everything a city possesses: raw materials, tools, containers, vehicles, defenses, entertainment, and nourishment. Some minor items — coins, seals, stamps — resist neat classification but can be loosely placed under ornaments or tools. The herding arts, already analyzed, account for tame animals.

One category remains conspicuously unaddressed: slaves and servants. And it is here, the Stranger suspects, that the true rivals to the statesman's claim will finally be found.
D: Elegant: Seven classes account for everything a city owns: materials, tools, containers, vehicles, defenses, amusements, food. The inventory is complete — except for the people who serve. And among the servants, the real competitors for the throne are hiding.
D: Summary: The seven classes of civic possessions cover nearly all property. Small items like coins are loosely classified. Tame animals fall under herding. The one remaining category — slaves and servants — is where the true rivals to the statesman are expected to be found.
D: Distilled: Once you have catalogued everything a city owns, the question of who rules it lives among the people who serve it.
Notes: The mention of coins (nomismata), seals (sphragides), and stamps as items that resist classification reflects their status as signs or tokens rather than material goods — they represent value or authority rather than possessing inherent utility. The transition to slaves and servants sets up the crucial final separation: distinguishing the statesman from those who merely serve the state, which is the project of chapters 10-13.

D: Original:
YOUNG SOCRATES: Very true.

STRANGER: The class of slaves and ministers only remains, and I suspect that in this the real aspirants for the throne, who are the rivals of the king in the formation of the political web, will be discovered; just as spinners, carders, and the rest of them, were the rivals of the weaver. All the others, who were termed co-operators, have been got rid of among the occupations already mentioned, and separated from the royal and political science.

YOUNG SOCRATES: I agree.
D: Narrative: The Stranger reveals that the class of slaves and servants is where the real aspirants for the throne — the rivals of the king in forming the political web — will be discovered, just as spinners, carders, and other textile workers were the rivals of the weaver. All the other co-operative arts have been separated out and accounted for. Only the servants remain, and among them are those who compete with the statesman for the claim to rule.
D: Refashioned:
Now comes the critical move. Among the servants and attendants of the state is where the genuine rivals to the statesman will be found — the people who actually compete for the claim to rule.

The analogy to weaving holds precisely. In weaving, the true competitors were not the toolmakers or the loom-builders but the spinners and carders — the textile workers whose art most closely resembled and overlapped with weaving itself. In the state, the rivals are not the farmers, builders, or merchants, but the people whose roles most closely resemble and overlap with governance.

All the co-operative arts have been cleared away. The field is narrowed to those who serve the state in ways that could be mistaken for ruling it.
D: Elegant: The farmer does not compete with the king. The builder does not claim the throne. But among those who serve the state most intimately — interpreting its laws, commanding its armies, mediating between its people and the gods — are those who believe their service is rule itself.
D: Summary: The class of slaves and servants contains the real rivals to the statesman, just as spinners and carders were the true rivals to the weaver. With all co-operative arts separated out, only those whose service most closely resembles governance remain to be distinguished from the statesman.
D: Distilled: The statesman's real competitors are not the producers but the servants whose work most closely mimics governing.
Notes: The weaving parallel is maintained: spinners and carders are 'causal' arts (contributing directly to the fabric), not 'co-operative' arts (making tools). Similarly, generals, priests, judges, and sophists contribute directly to the political fabric — their work is closely enough related to governance that they can plausibly claim to be governors themselves. This sets up the crucial distinctions of chapters 10-13.

D: Original:
STRANGER: Let us go a little nearer, in order that we may be more certain of the complexion of this remaining class.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Let us do so.

STRANGER: We shall find from our present point of view that the greatest servants are in a case and condition which is the reverse of what we anticipated.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Who are they?

STRANGER: Those who have been purchased, and have so become possessions; these are unmistakably slaves, and certainly do not claim royal science.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly not.

STRANGER: Again, freemen who of their own accord become the servants of the other classes in a State, and who exchange and equalise the products of husbandry and the other arts, some sitting in the market-place, others going from city to city by land or sea, and giving money in exchange for money or for other productions—the money-changer, the merchant, the ship-owner, the retailer, will not put in any claim to statecraft or politics?

YOUNG SOCRATES: No; unless, indeed, to the politics of commerce.

STRANGER: But surely men whom we see acting as hirelings and serfs, and too happy to turn their hand to anything, will not profess to share in royal science?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly not.

STRANGER: But what would you say of some other serviceable officials?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Who are they, and what services do they perform?

STRANGER: There are heralds, and scribes perfected by practice, and divers others who have great skill in various sorts of business connected with the government of states—what shall we call them?

YOUNG SOCRATES: They are the officials, and servants of the rulers, as you just now called them, but not themselves rulers.

STRANGER: There may be something strange in any servant pretending to be a ruler, and yet I do not think that I could have been dreaming when I imagined that the principal claimants to political science would be found somewhere in this neighbourhood.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Very true.

STRANGER: Well, let us draw nearer, and try the claims of some who have not yet been tested: in the first place, there are diviners, who have a portion of servile or ministerial science, and are thought to be the interpreters of the gods to men.

YOUNG SOCRATES: True.
D: Narrative: The Stranger proposes they examine this remaining class more closely. The first group they encounter are purchased slaves — unmistakable possessions who certainly do not claim political expertise. Next are free merchants, traders, and retailers who voluntarily exchange goods in markets or across cities. Young Socrates jokes they might claim 'the politics of commerce,' but the Stranger dismisses this. Hired laborers willing to do any work also make no claim to royal science. Then there are heralds, practiced scribes, and various skilled officials connected with government. Young Socrates identifies these as servants of the rulers, not rulers themselves. The Stranger agrees but adds that he suspects the principal claimants to political science will be found somewhere nearby. He proposes they test those not yet examined, beginning with diviners — practitioners of a servile or ministerial art who are considered interpreters of the gods to humanity.
D: Refashioned:
The Stranger begins a systematic elimination. Who in the servant class can be quickly ruled out as rivals to the statesman?

Slaves — purchased property — obviously make no claim to rule. Merchants, traders, and retailers move goods and money between people and cities, but their expertise is commercial, not political. Day laborers sell their effort without pretending to political knowledge. Even skilled government officials — heralds, scribes, administrators — are servants of rulers, not rulers themselves.

But the Stranger is not satisfied. The real claimants are nearby. He turns to a more promising category: diviners, those who claim to interpret the will of the gods to humanity. Here, where service shades into authority, the competition for the statesman's title begins.
D: Elegant: Slaves do not claim to rule. Merchants do not claim to rule. Clerks do not claim to rule. But turn to those who claim to speak for the gods, and suddenly every servant looks like a king. The competition for political authority begins where ministerial service starts to feel like something higher.
D: Summary: The Stranger systematically eliminates groups from contention for the statesman's title: slaves, merchants, laborers, and government clerks are clearly servants, not rulers. The real rivals are found among those whose service carries authority — beginning with diviners who claim to interpret the gods' will.
D: Distilled: The rivals to the ruler are not those who serve the state's material needs but those whose service carries the appearance of authority.
Notes: The systematic elimination mirrors the method of division (diairesis) used throughout the dialogue. The distinction between merchants and political rulers anticipates Aristotle's separation of economics (oikonomia) and politics (politike) in Politics I. Diviners (manteis) held significant political influence in ancient Greece — the Oracle at Delphi was regularly consulted before major political and military decisions. Athens maintained official diviners (manteis) and oracle-interpreters (chresmologoi) as state functionaries.

D: Original:
STRANGER: There is also the priestly class, who, as the law declares, know how to give the gods gifts from men in the form of sacrifices which are acceptable to them, and to ask on our behalf blessings in return from them. Now both these are branches of the servile or ministerial art.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Yes, clearly.
D: Narrative: The Stranger identifies the priestly class. According to established custom, priests know how to give the gods acceptable gifts from humanity in the form of sacrifices, and to ask for blessings in return. Both the priestly art and divination are branches of the servile or ministerial art — they serve as intermediaries, not as rulers.
D: Refashioned:
Next are the priests — specialists in the formal relationship between the city and the gods. They know the proper sacrifices and the correct forms of petition. Their authority derives from religious tradition and ritual expertise.

But the Stranger classifies both priests and diviners as branches of the ministerial art. They are intermediaries, not authorities. They serve the relationship between gods and humans; they do not govern humans.
D: Elegant: Priests manage the transaction between mortals and gods — offering what is acceptable, requesting what is needed. Theirs is an art of mediation, not command. A go-between is not a governor, however sacred the parties involved.
D: Summary: Priests are specialists in sacrifice and petition, managing the formal relationship between the city and the gods. Like diviners, they are classified as ministerial — intermediaries serving a function, not rulers exercising authority.
D: Distilled: Religious intermediaries serve the relationship between humanity and the divine, but serving a relationship is not the same as governing a people.
Notes: Greek priests (hiereis) were typically not a separate caste but citizens who held priestly offices, often selected by lot or hereditary succession. Their role was primarily ritual — performing sacrifices (thusiai) correctly — rather than doctrinal or moral. This contrasts with the broader authority claimed by priesthoods in Egypt and the Near East, which the Stranger addresses in the next passage.

D: Original: STRANGER: And here I think that we seem to be getting on the right track; for the priest and the diviner are swollen with pride and prerogative, and they create an awful impression of themselves by the magnitude of their enterprises; in Egypt, the king himself is not allowed to reign, unless he have priestly powers, and if he should be of another class and has thrust himself in, he must get enrolled in the priesthood. In many parts of Hellas, the duty of offering the most solemn propitiatory sacrifices is assigned to the highest magistracies, and here, at Athens, the most solemn and national of the ancient sacrifices are supposed to be celebrated by him who has been chosen by lot to be the King Archon.
D: Narrative: The Stranger observes that they seem to be on the right track, because priests and diviners are swollen with pride and prerogative. They create an impressive aura through the magnitude of their undertakings. In Egypt, the king himself cannot reign without priestly powers — if he comes from another class and seizes power, he must be enrolled in the priesthood. In many parts of Greece, the duty of offering the most solemn propitiatory sacrifices is assigned to the highest magistrates. And in Athens specifically, the most solemn and ancient national sacrifices are performed by the King Archon, who is chosen by lot.
D: Refashioned:
Now they are getting close. Priests and diviners are formidable rivals because they command genuine awe. The scale and solemnity of their undertakings give them an imposing authority that bleeds into the political sphere.

The examples are striking. In Egypt, no one can be king without priestly authority — if a military leader seizes power, he must be formally enrolled in the priesthood before his rule is legitimate. Across Greece, the most sacred state sacrifices are reserved for the highest officials. In Athens, the most ancient and important religious ceremonies are conducted by the King Archon — a magistrate chosen by lot who carries a royal title precisely because the ceremonial functions of the old kings were too sacred to abandon.

This is why priests are serious competitors for the statesman's title: in practice, political and religious authority have been deeply intertwined.
D: Elegant: Priests make formidable rivals because their authority feels higher than political power. In Egypt, a general who seizes the throne must first be made a priest. In Athens, the most sacred ceremonies require a magistrate with a king's title. Wherever religion and governance fuse, the priest's claim to rule looks indistinguishable from the ruler's.
D: Summary: Priests and diviners are serious rivals to the statesman because religious and political authority are deeply intertwined in practice. In Egypt, kings must hold priestly status. In Athens, the King Archon performs the most sacred state ceremonies. This fusion makes the priestly claim to political power formidable.
D: Distilled: Wherever religious authority fuses with political power, priests become the most dangerous rivals to the statesman's claim.
Notes: The Egyptian requirement that pharaohs hold priestly status reflects the god-king tradition documented by Herodotus (Histories II.37). The King Archon (archon basileus) was one of Athens' nine archons, selected by lot, who retained the religious functions of the ancient Athenian kings after the monarchy was abolished. The office persisted into Plato's time precisely because the rituals were considered too sacred to transfer to new institutions. The most important ceremony was the Anthesteria festival. Plato's point — that political and priestly functions tend to merge — remains relevant to discussions of theocracy and the separation of church and state.

D: Original:
YOUNG SOCRATES: Precisely.

STRANGER: But who are these other kings and priests elected by lot who now come into view followed by their retainers and a vast throng, as the former class disappears and the scene changes?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Whom can you mean?

STRANGER: They are a strange crew.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Why strange?

STRANGER: A minute ago I thought that they were animals of every tribe; for many of them are like lions and centaurs, and many more like satyrs and such weak and shifty creatures;—Protean shapes quickly changing into one another's forms and natures; and now, Socrates, I begin to see who they are.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Who are they? You seem to be gazing on some strange vision.

STRANGER: Yes; every one looks strange when you do not know him; and just now I myself fell into this mistake—at first sight, coming suddenly upon him, I did not recognize the politician and his troop.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Who is he?

STRANGER: The chief of Sophists and most accomplished of wizards, who must at any cost be separated from the true king or Statesman, if we are ever to see daylight in the present enquiry.

YOUNG SOCRATES: That is a hope not lightly to be renounced.

STRANGER: Never, if I can help it; and, first, let me ask you a question.

YOUNG SOCRATES: What?
D: Narrative: The Stranger shifts attention. As the priests and diviners recede, a new and strange group comes into view — other kings and elected officials followed by retainers and a vast crowd. The Stranger describes them as creatures of every kind: some like lions and centaurs, others like satyrs and other weak, shifty beings — Protean shapes that quickly change form and nature. Young Socrates asks who they are. The Stranger says he now recognizes them: at the head of this strange troop is the chief of Sophists, the most accomplished of wizards, who must at all costs be separated from the true king or statesman if their inquiry is ever to reach its conclusion. Young Socrates agrees this is a hope not to be abandoned. The Stranger proposes to press on with the investigation.
D: Refashioned:
Now the most dangerous rival emerges. As the priests and diviners step aside, a bizarre procession appears — a crowd of figures following leaders who claim royal authority. The Stranger describes them in mythological terms: some resemble lions and centaurs, others satyrs and shape-shifters. They change form constantly, adapting their appearance to whatever the situation demands.

At first, the Stranger does not recognize them. Then he does. At the head of this procession is the Sophist — the supreme imitator, the most accomplished of political illusionists. This is the figure who must be separated from the true statesman at all costs. The Sophist is not merely a rival claimant; he is the claimant whose imitation of statesmanship is so convincing that without careful analysis, no one can tell the difference.

The inquiry has found its final and most formidable adversary.
D: Elegant: Lions, centaurs, satyrs — a menagerie of political pretenders parades past, shape-shifting with every glance. At their head stands the Sophist: the supreme illusionist of governance, whose imitation of the statesman is so perfect that only philosophy can tell them apart. Every other rival was easy to dismiss. This one requires the full force of dialectic.
D: Summary: The final and most dangerous rival to the statesman is the Sophist — a shape-shifting imitator whose imitation of political expertise is so convincing it is nearly indistinguishable from the real thing. Separating the Sophist from the true statesman is the decisive challenge of the inquiry.
D: Distilled: The most dangerous rival to genuine political expertise is the one whose imitation of it is indistinguishable from the real thing.
Notes: The mythological imagery — lions, centaurs, satyrs, Proteus — draws from Greek tradition where shape-shifting represents deception (Proteus in Odyssey IV.456-458; Empusa in Aristophanes' Frogs). The 'chief of Sophists' likely refers to the type defined in the companion dialogue Sophist, where the Sophist is identified as a maker of false appearances (Sophist 235a-236c). The description of sophists as political shape-shifters connects to Republic VI (492a-493d), where Socrates describes how sophists mirror popular opinion back to the crowd and call it wisdom. The separation of the sophist from the statesman parallels the separation of the sophist from the philosopher — the unfinished project of the never-written dialogue Philosopher.

D: Original:
STRANGER: Is not monarchy a recognized form of government?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Yes.

STRANGER: And, after monarchy, next in order comes the government of the few?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Of course.

STRANGER: Is not the third form of government the rule of the multitude, which is called by the name of democracy?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly.

STRANGER: And do not these three expand in a manner into five, producing out of themselves two other names?

YOUNG SOCRATES: What are they?

YOUNG SOCRATES: What are they?

STRANGER: There is a criterion of voluntary and involuntary, poverty and riches, law and the absence of law, which men now-a-days apply to them; the two first they subdivide accordingly, and ascribe to monarchy two forms and two corresponding names, royalty and tyranny.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Very true.

STRANGER: And the government of the few they distinguish by the names of aristocracy and oligarchy.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly.

STRANGER: Democracy alone, whether rigidly observing the laws or not, and whether the multitude rule over the men of property with their consent or against their consent, always in ordinary language has the same name.

YOUNG SOCRATES: True.

STRANGER: But do you suppose that any form of government which is defined by these characteristics of the one, the few, or the many, of poverty or wealth, of voluntary or compulsory submission, of written law or the absence of law, can be a right one?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Why not?

STRANGER: Reflect; and follow me.

YOUNG SOCRATES: In what direction?

STRANGER: Shall we abide by what we said at first, or shall we retract our words?

YOUNG SOCRATES: To what do you refer?

STRANGER: If I am not mistaken, we said that royal power was a science?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Yes.

STRANGER: And a science of a peculiar kind, which was selected out of the rest as having a character which is at once judicial and authoritative?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Yes.

STRANGER: And there was one kind of authority over lifeless things and another other living animals; and so we proceeded in the division step by step up to this point, not losing the idea of science, but unable as yet to determine the nature of the particular science?

YOUNG SOCRATES: True.
D: Narrative:
The discussion turns to the recognized forms of government. There are three basic types: monarchy, the rule of one; oligarchy or aristocracy, the rule of the few; and democracy, the rule of the many. But these three expand into five by applying further distinctions. Monarchy divides into royalty and tyranny based on whether rule is voluntary or compulsory, lawful or lawless. The rule of the few divides into aristocracy and oligarchy along similar lines. Democracy alone keeps a single name regardless of whether it observes laws or not, or whether the multitude rules with or against the consent of the propertied class.

But the critical question is whether any form of government defined merely by the number of rulers, the wealth or poverty of citizens, voluntary or involuntary submission, or the presence or absence of written law can truly be the right form. The answer requires returning to first principles. Statecraft was established as a science -- a particular kind of knowledge that is simultaneously judicial and authoritative. This science was distinguished through careful division from authority over lifeless things to authority over living beings, always preserving the idea of genuine knowledge. The defining principle of government, then, cannot rest on any of these surface features. Something deeper is required.
D: Refashioned:
There are three basic forms of government: rule by one, rule by the few, and rule by the many. Apply a few more distinctions -- consent versus coercion, wealth versus poverty, law versus lawlessness -- and three becomes five. Monarchy splits into legitimate kingship and tyranny. Rule by the few splits into aristocracy and oligarchy. Democracy, oddly, keeps one name whether it follows laws or not.

But here is the deeper question: can any of these categories actually identify good government? The number of rulers, their wealth, whether subjects consent, whether laws exist on paper -- none of these features touch what matters. If statecraft is a genuine science, as established earlier, then the defining criterion of legitimate government must be knowledge, not any of these surface characteristics.
D: Elegant: Monarchy, oligarchy, democracy -- subdivide them by law and consent and you get five names. But names built on who rules, how many, or how rich tell you nothing about whether they rule well. If governing is a science, the only criterion that matters is whether the rulers actually possess it.
D: Summary: The three basic forms of government -- monarchy, oligarchy, and democracy -- expand to five when divided by law, consent, and wealth. But none of these classifications address the fundamental question of whether rulers possess genuine political knowledge.
D: Distilled: The number of rulers and the presence of laws are surface features; the only meaningful criterion for legitimate government is whether rulers possess genuine knowledge of statecraft.
Notes: The sixfold classification of governments (monarchy/tyranny, aristocracy/oligarchy, lawful democracy/lawless democracy) originates here and in the Republic, later systematized by Aristotle in Politics III. The criteria of voluntary/involuntary, wealth/poverty, and law/lawlessness as classifying principles became foundational to Western political taxonomy. Plato's insistence that the 'science' of rule is what matters prefigures the modern debate over technocracy versus democracy.

D: Original:
STRANGER: Hence we are led to observe that the distinguishing principle of the State cannot be the few or many, the voluntary or involuntary, poverty or riches; but some notion of science must enter into it, if we are to be consistent with what has preceded.

YOUNG SOCRATES: And we must be consistent.

STRANGER: Well, then, in which of these various forms of States may the science of government, which is among the greatest of all sciences and most difficult to acquire, be supposed to reside? That we must discover, and then we shall see who are the false politicians who pretend to be politicians but are not, although they persuade many, and shall separate them from the wise king.

YOUNG SOCRATES: That, as the argument has already intimated, will be our duty.

STRANGER: Do you think that the multitude in a State can attain political science?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Impossible.

STRANGER: But, perhaps, in a city of a thousand men, there would be a hundred, or say fifty, who could?

YOUNG SOCRATES: In that case political science would certainly be the easiest of all sciences; there could not be found in a city of that number as many really first-rate draught-players, if judged by the standard of the rest of Hellas, and there would certainly not be as many kings. For kings we may truly call those who possess royal science, whether they rule or not, as was shown in the previous argument.

STRANGER: Thank you for reminding me; and the consequence is that any true form of government can only be supposed to be the government of one, two, or, at any rate, of a few.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly.
D: Narrative:
If consistency demands that the distinguishing principle of the state must involve knowledge rather than numbers, wealth, or consent, then the essential question becomes: in which of these governmental forms does genuine political science reside? Discovering this will expose the false politicians who merely pretend to expertise while persuading the many, and will separate them from the truly wise ruler.

Can the multitude attain political science? That seems impossible. Could even fifty or a hundred people in a city of a thousand possess it? That would make political science the easiest of all skills to acquire -- easier than becoming a first-rate board game player. In reality, there would not be that many true kings in any city. Those who possess royal science deserve the title of king whether they currently rule or not. The consequence is clear: any genuinely correct form of government can only be the government of one, or at most a very few.
D: Refashioned:
If the defining feature of legitimate government is knowledge, the question becomes: who actually has it? Can the general public master political science? Obviously not. Could even fifty experts exist in a city of a thousand? That would make statecraft the easiest skill in the world -- you would find more master politicians than chess grandmasters, which is absurd.

Genuine political expertise is extraordinarily rare. A person who truly possesses it deserves the title of ruler whether they currently hold office or not. The implication is stark: authentic government can only be the government of one, or at most a very few who actually know what they are doing.
D: Elegant: Can the masses master statecraft? No. Could even fifty people in a city of a thousand? That would make political expertise more common than mastery of chess -- absurd. True political knowledge is the rarest of skills. Legitimate government, therefore, can only be the government of one or a very few.
D: Summary: Genuine political science is extremely rare -- far too rare for the multitude or even a significant minority to possess. Therefore, truly legitimate government can only be the rule of one or a very few who actually have this knowledge.
D: Distilled: Political expertise is so rare that legitimate government can only be the rule of one or a very few who genuinely possess it.
Notes: The analogy to draught-players (pessoi, an ancient Greek board game) emphasizes that expertise in any domain is rare. The claim that a true king is defined by knowledge rather than office -- 'whether they rule or not' -- is a signature Platonic move that separates legitimate authority from actual power. This argument directly parallels Republic 473c-d, where Plato argues that cities will never be well-governed until philosophers rule or rulers philosophize.

D: Original: STRANGER: And these, whether they rule with the will, or against the will, of their subjects, with written laws or without written laws, and whether they are poor or rich, and whatever be the nature of their rule, must be supposed, according to our present view, to rule on some scientific principle; just as the physician, whether he cures us against our will or with our will, and whatever be his mode of treatment,—incision, burning, or the infliction of some other pain,—whether he practises out of a book or not out of a book, and whether he be rich or poor, whether he purges or reduces in some other way, or even fattens his patients, is a physician all the same, so long as he exercises authority over them according to rules of art, if he only does them good and heals and saves them. And this we lay down to be the only proper test of the art of medicine, or of any other art of command.
D: Narrative: These rare genuine rulers, whether they govern with or against the will of their subjects, with or without written laws, whether rich or poor, must be understood to rule on the basis of scientific principle. The analogy to medicine makes this clear. A physician remains a physician regardless of whether the patient consents to treatment, whether the methods involve cutting, burning, or other painful procedures, whether the doctor follows a textbook or improvises, and whether the doctor is wealthy or impoverished. The only proper test of medical art is whether the physician heals and saves the patient by exercising authority according to the rules of the art. The same criterion applies to any art of command.
D: Refashioned: A true ruler governs by knowledge, and nothing else matters -- consent, written laws, personal wealth, none of it. A physician is a physician whether the patient likes the treatment, whether it involves surgery or medication, whether the doctor follows a textbook or adapts on the spot. The only valid test is: does the doctor heal? The same standard applies to governance. The only measure of legitimacy is whether the ruler improves the condition of the governed through genuine expertise.
D: Elegant: A doctor is a doctor whether the patient consents, whether the treatment is painful, whether she follows a textbook or improvises, whether she is rich or poor. The only test is: does she heal? The same standard applies to rulers. The only question is whether they govern with genuine knowledge that actually benefits the governed.
D: Summary: Like a physician whose legitimacy rests solely on whether they heal the patient, a ruler's legitimacy rests solely on whether they govern with genuine knowledge. Consent, written laws, and personal wealth are irrelevant to this test.
D: Distilled: Just as the only valid test of a doctor is healing, the only valid test of a ruler is governing with genuine knowledge that benefits the governed.
Notes: The doctor/statesman analogy is one of Plato's most persistent arguments, appearing in the Gorgias (464b-465c), Republic (332c-d), and Laws (720a-e). It became the foundational metaphor for paternalistic governance and was later critiqued by Aristotle (Politics III.11) who argued that the patient may know better than the doctor what suits his own body. The argument that legitimate authority can override consent anticipates modern debates about expert governance, public health mandates, and the tension between technocracy and democracy.

D: Original:
YOUNG SOCRATES: Quite true.

STRANGER: Then that can be the only true form of government in which the governors are really found to possess science, and are not mere pretenders, whether they rule according to law or without law, over willing or unwilling subjects, and are rich or poor themselves—none of these things can with any propriety be included in the notion of the ruler.

YOUNG SOCRATES: True.
D: Narrative: The conclusion follows: the only true form of government is one in which the governors genuinely possess political science. Whether they rule by law or without it, over willing or unwilling subjects, and whether they are personally rich or poor -- none of these conditions properly enter into the definition of a true ruler. The ruler is defined by knowledge alone.
D: Refashioned: The conclusion is direct: the only legitimate form of government is one in which the rulers actually possess political knowledge. Law, consent, wealth -- none of these define the true ruler. Knowledge does. Everything else is incidental.
D: Elegant: Only one thing makes government legitimate: the rulers actually know what they are doing. Law, consent, wealth -- incidental. Knowledge is the whole of it.
D: Summary: True government is defined solely by whether rulers possess genuine political knowledge. All other criteria -- law, consent, wealth -- are irrelevant to the definition.
D: Distilled: The legitimacy of government rests entirely on the rulers' knowledge, not on law, consent, or wealth.
Notes: This distillation of the argument to its purest form -- legitimacy equals knowledge -- is the core thesis that Aristotle would later challenge by introducing the concept of collective wisdom (Politics III.11), arguing that many people together may possess more practical knowledge than any single expert. The modern democratic tradition largely sides with Aristotle on this point while acknowledging Plato's insight that expertise matters.

D: Original: STRANGER: And whether with a view to the public good they purge the State by killing some, or exiling some; whether they reduce the size of the body corporate by sending out from the hive swarms of citizens, or, by introducing persons from without, increase it; while they act according to the rules of wisdom and justice, and use their power with a view to the general security and improvement, the city over which they rule, and which has these characteristics, may be described as the only true State. All other governments are not genuine or real; but only imitations of this, and some of them are better and some of them are worse; the better are said to be well governed, but they are mere imitations like the others.
D: Narrative: Such true rulers may purge the state for the public good by killing or exiling some citizens, may reduce the population by sending out colonies, or may increase it by welcoming immigrants. So long as they act according to wisdom and justice with a view to the general security and improvement of the city, the state they rule is the only true state. All other governments are not genuine but merely imitations of this ideal -- some better, some worse, but imitations nonetheless.
D: Refashioned:
A ruler with genuine knowledge may take drastic action for the public good -- exile or execute the dangerous, reduce the population through resettlement, or expand it through immigration. As long as these decisions flow from wisdom and aim at the general welfare, the resulting state is the only true state.

Every other form of government is an imitation. Some imitations are better, some worse. The well-governed ones are praised, but they are imitations all the same.
D: Elegant: The true ruler may exile, execute, shrink, or expand the state -- so long as wisdom and the common good guide every decision. That state alone is real. All others are imitations: some better, some worse, but none genuine.
D: Summary: A state governed by genuine political knowledge -- even if the ruler takes drastic measures like exile or execution for the public good -- is the only true state. All other governments are imitations of varying quality.
D: Distilled: The state ruled by genuine knowledge is the only real state; every other form of government is merely a better or worse imitation.
Notes: The language of 'sending out swarms from the hive' refers to the Greek practice of founding colonies (apoikiai), a standard population-management tool of ancient city-states. The concept of the ideal state as the only 'real' one, with actual states as imitations, directly parallels Plato's Theory of Forms (Republic V-VII), where physical objects are imitations of ideal Forms. This framing makes actual political arrangements always deficient by definition -- a point Aristotle critiqued as impractical in Politics II.

D: Original:
YOUNG SOCRATES: I agree, Stranger, in the greater part of what you say; but as to their ruling without laws—the expression has a harsh sound.

STRANGER: You have been too quick for me, Socrates; I was just going to ask you whether you objected to any of my statements. And now I see that we shall have to consider this notion of there being good government without laws.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly.
D: Narrative: Young Socrates agrees with most of this but raises an objection: the idea of ruling without laws sounds harsh. The Stranger acknowledges this concern. Young Socrates has anticipated the next question -- whether there can be good government without laws. This is precisely what must now be examined.
D: Refashioned: The claim that a ruler could legitimately govern without laws is unsettling, and the objection comes immediately. It sounds dangerous. It sounds like a recipe for tyranny. But the argument demands it be taken seriously. If knowledge is the sole criterion of legitimacy, and if laws are merely generalized rules that cannot account for every situation, then a genuinely wise ruler operating above the law is at least theoretically possible. The question is whether this possibility can be defended.
D: Elegant: Rule without law sounds like tyranny. But if the true criterion is knowledge, and laws are blunt instruments that cannot fit every case, then the wise ruler who transcends law is not a tyrant but the only genuine statesman. The discomfort this provokes is exactly the point.
D: Summary: Young Socrates objects that ruling without laws sounds harsh and dangerous. The Stranger acknowledges the discomfort but insists the relationship between genuine knowledge and law must be examined.
D: Distilled: The idea of legitimate rule without law is disturbing precisely because it forces us to ask what actually makes government legitimate -- procedure or knowledge.
Notes: This moment of dramatic tension is characteristic of Plato's dialogues -- the interlocutor voices the commonsense democratic objection that Plato wants to address head-on. The Greek word for 'harsh' (skleros) carries connotations of both intellectual difficulty and moral discomfort. The question of whether a wise ruler can legitimately act above the law anticipates the entire tradition of constitutional theory from Aristotle through Locke and Montesquieu.

D: Original:
STRANGER: There can be no doubt that legislation is in a manner the business of a king, and yet the best thing of all is not that the law should rule, but that a man should rule supposing him to have wisdom and royal power. Do you see why this is?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Why?

STRANGER: Because the law does not perfectly comprehend what is noblest and most just for all and therefore cannot enforce what is best. The differences of men and actions, and the endless irregular movements of human things, do not admit of any universal and simple rule. And no art whatsoever can lay down a rule which will last for all time.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Of course not.

STRANGER: But the law is always striving to make one;—like an obstinate and ignorant tyrant, who will not allow anything to be done contrary to his appointment, or any question to be asked—not even in sudden changes of circumstances, when something happens to be better than what he commanded for some one.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly; the law treats us all precisely in the manner which you describe.

STRANGER: A perfectly simple principle can never be applied to a state of things which is the reverse of simple.

YOUNG SOCRATES: True.

STRANGER: Then if the law is not the perfection of right, why are we compelled to make laws at all? The reason of this has next to be investigated.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly.

STRANGER: Let me ask, whether you have not meetings for gymnastic contests in your city, such as there are in other cities, at which men compete in running, wrestling, and the like?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Yes; they are very common among us.

STRANGER: And what are the rules which are enforced on their pupils by professional trainers or by others having similar authority? Can you remember?

YOUNG SOCRATES: To what do you refer?

STRANGER: The training-masters do not issue minute rules for individuals, or give every individual what is exactly suited to his constitution; they think that they ought to go more roughly to work, and to prescribe generally the regimen which will benefit the majority.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Very true.

STRANGER: And therefore they assign equal amounts of exercise to them all; they send them forth together, and let them rest together from their running, wrestling, or whatever the form of bodily exercise may be.

YOUNG SOCRATES: True.
D: Narrative:
Legislation is indeed part of the king's business, yet the best thing is not that law should rule, but that a person with wisdom and royal power should rule. The reason is fundamental: law cannot perfectly comprehend what is noblest and most just for all people, and therefore cannot enforce what is truly best. The infinite differences among people and actions, and the endless irregular movements of human affairs, do not admit of any universal and simple rule. No art whatsoever can lay down a rule that will last for all time.

Yet law constantly strives to do exactly this -- like an obstinate and ignorant tyrant who will not allow anything to be done contrary to his appointment, or any question to be asked, not even when circumstances suddenly change and something better than what he originally commanded becomes apparent. A perfectly simple principle can never be applied to a state of things that is the reverse of simple.

If law is not the perfection of right, then why are we compelled to make laws at all? The answer can be illustrated by analogy. Athletic trainers do not issue minute rules tailored to each individual's constitution. They prescribe a general regimen designed to benefit the majority. They assign equal amounts of exercise to everyone, sending them all out together and resting them together, regardless of individual differences.
D: Refashioned:
Making laws is part of what a ruler does. But the ideal is not rule of law -- it is rule by a person who actually has the wisdom to govern. Here is why.

Law cannot perfectly grasp what is best for every person in every situation. Human beings are too different, human affairs too unpredictable, for any fixed rule to remain optimal in all cases. No body of rules can anticipate everything. Yet law behaves as though it can -- like a stubborn, ignorant authority that refuses to allow exceptions, refuses to hear questions, and insists on rigid compliance even when circumstances change and something better becomes obvious.

Simple rules cannot govern complex realities.

So if law is inherently imperfect, why do we make laws at all? For the same reason athletic trainers prescribe the same regimen to all their athletes rather than creating individual programs for each one. A trainer cannot be at every athlete's side every moment. General rules are a practical necessity -- they serve the majority reasonably well, even if they serve no individual perfectly.
D: Elegant: Law is a stubborn, ignorant tyrant. It cannot account for the infinite differences among people, the unpredictable turns of human affairs, or the cases where rigid compliance produces worse outcomes than flexibility would. Simple rules cannot govern complex realities. We make laws anyway, for the same reason trainers prescribe the same regimen to all their athletes: no one can be at every person's side making case-by-case judgments.
