Laws are a concession to our limitations, not an expression of justice perfected.
D: Summary: Law is inherently imperfect because it applies simple, fixed rules to infinitely complex and changing human situations. We make laws not because they achieve perfect justice, but because no ruler can be present to make individual judgments for every person at every moment.
D: Distilled: Law is a necessary compromise: too rigid to achieve perfect justice, but indispensable because no ruler can make case-by-case judgments for everyone.
Notes: The comparison of law to 'an obstinate and ignorant tyrant' (authades kai amathes) is one of the most famous passages in ancient political philosophy. It directly influenced Aristotle's discussion of equity (epieikeia) in Nicomachean Ethics V.10, where he argues that equity corrects law's inherent generality. The athletic training analogy anticipates modern arguments about the trade-off between personalized and standardized approaches in medicine, education, and public policy. Montesquieu (Spirit of the Laws, 1748) and Locke (Second Treatise, Ch. XIV on prerogative) both grappled with this tension between fixed law and executive discretion.

D: Original:
STRANGER: And now observe that the legislator who has to preside over the herd, and to enforce justice in their dealings with one another, will not be able, in enacting for the general good, to provide exactly what is suitable for each particular case.

YOUNG SOCRATES: He cannot be expected to do so.

STRANGER: He will lay down laws in a general form for the majority, roughly meeting the cases of individuals; and some of them he will deliver in writing, and others will be unwritten; and these last will be traditional customs of the country.

YOUNG SOCRATES: He will be right.

STRANGER: Yes, quite right; for how can he sit at every man's side all through his life, prescribing for him the exact particulars of his duty? Who, Socrates, would be equal to such a task? No one who really had the royal science, if he had been able to do this, would have imposed upon himself the restriction of a written law.

YOUNG SOCRATES: So I should infer from what has now been said.

STRANGER: Or rather, my good friend, from what is going to be said.

YOUNG SOCRATES: And what is that?

STRANGER: Let us put to ourselves the case of a physician, or trainer, who is about to go into a far country, and is expecting to be a long time away from his patients—thinking that his instructions will not be remembered unless they are written down, he will leave notes of them for the use of his pupils or patients.

YOUNG SOCRATES: True.
D: Narrative:
The legislator who must govern a whole population cannot provide exactly what is suitable for each particular case. Laws will be laid down in general form for the majority, addressing individual situations only roughly -- some written, some preserved as unwritten traditional customs.

This is right and inevitable. No lawmaker could sit at every citizen's side throughout their life, prescribing the exact particulars of their duty. Anyone who truly possessed royal science and could do this would never have imposed upon himself the restriction of written law in the first place.

Consider a physician or trainer about to travel far away for a long time. Expecting that his instructions will be forgotten, he leaves written notes for his pupils or patients. These written instructions are a practical substitute for his presence -- necessary, but inherently inferior to his direct, adaptive judgment.
D: Refashioned:
No legislature can tailor laws to every individual situation. Laws must be written for the general case, addressing most circumstances roughly. Some will be codified in statute; others will survive as unwritten customs and norms.

This is unavoidable. No lawmaker can stand beside every citizen for life, directing their specific choices. And anyone with genuine political wisdom would never voluntarily bind themselves to fixed rules when they could exercise adaptive judgment instead.

The analogy is a doctor leaving written instructions before a long trip. The notes are useful -- better than nothing -- but they are a substitute for the doctor's presence, not an improvement on it. Written law stands in the same relationship to wise governance: a necessary proxy, not the real thing.
D: Elegant: Laws are a doctor's notes left behind before a long journey -- useful instructions for the majority, better than nothing, but a poor substitute for the doctor's adaptive judgment in the room. No lawmaker could stand beside every citizen for life. Written law is a concession to absence, not an expression of perfected wisdom.
D: Summary: Laws must be general because no legislator can prescribe individually for every citizen. Like a doctor's written instructions left before a long trip, laws are a practical substitute for wise judgment, necessary but inherently inferior.
D: Distilled: Written law is to wise governance what a doctor's travel notes are to the doctor's direct care: a necessary but inferior substitute.
Notes: The absent-physician analogy extends the medical metaphor from 11.3 and adds the crucial element of absence -- laws exist because the wise ruler cannot be everywhere. This becomes the key to understanding the 'second-best' government argument that follows. Aristotle developed this insight into his theory of equity (epieikeia), arguing that good laws must include mechanisms for their own correction. The distinction between written law and unwritten custom (nomoi agraphoi) was important in Greek legal thought; Pericles' Funeral Oration (Thucydides II.37) praises both written laws and unwritten customs.

D: Original: STRANGER: But what would you say, if he came back sooner than he had intended, and, owing to an unexpected change of the winds or other celestial influences, something else happened to be better for them,—would he not venture to suggest this new remedy, although not contemplated in his former prescription? Would he persist in observing the original law, neither himself giving any new commandments, nor the patient daring to do otherwise than was prescribed, under the idea that this course only was healthy and medicinal, all others noxious and heterodox? Viewed in the light of science and true art, would not all such enactments be utterly ridiculous?
D: Narrative: But suppose the physician returns sooner than expected. Unexpected changes -- new winds, new circumstances -- mean that something different from the original prescription would now be better for the patient. Would the doctor stubbornly adhere to the old written instructions, refusing to prescribe anything new? Would the patient be forbidden from trying anything other than what was originally written down, on the grounds that only the original instructions were healthy and everything else was dangerous nonsense? Viewed in the light of genuine science and true art, such rigid adherence would be utterly ridiculous.
D: Refashioned:
Now suppose the doctor returns early and conditions have changed. A better treatment is obvious. Would anyone insist on following the outdated prescription? Would we forbid the patient from trying the better remedy simply because it was not in the original instructions?

That would be absurd. When the expert is present and reality has shifted, clinging to outdated rules is not prudence -- it is foolishness.
D: Elegant: The doctor returns early. Conditions have changed. A better treatment is obvious. Would anyone demand that the old prescription be followed anyway, simply because it was written down? When the expert is in the room and reality has shifted, rigid adherence to outdated rules is not prudence but absurdity.
D: Summary: If a physician returns early and sees that circumstances have changed, rigidly following the original written prescription instead of adapting would be absurd. The same logic applies to a wise ruler constrained by outdated laws.
D: Distilled: When the expert is present and circumstances have changed, clinging to outdated written rules over adaptive judgment is absurd.
Notes: This passage makes the case for what modern constitutional theory calls executive prerogative -- the authority to act outside or against existing law when circumstances demand it. Locke (Second Treatise, Ch. XIV) later formalized this as 'prerogative: the power to act according to discretion for the public good, without the prescription of the law, and sometimes even against it.' The word 'heterodox' in the original (literally 'other-thinking') is applied ironically to unapproved medical treatments, foreshadowing its later use in religious and political contexts.

D: Original:
YOUNG SOCRATES: Utterly.

STRANGER: And if he who gave laws, written or unwritten, determining what was good or bad, honourable or dishonourable, just or unjust, to the tribes of men who flock together in their several cities, and are governed in accordance with them; if, I say, the wise legislator were suddenly to come again, or another like to him, is he to be prohibited from changing them?—would not this prohibition be in reality quite as ridiculous as the other?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly.

STRANGER: Do you know a plausible saying of the common people which is in point?

YOUNG SOCRATES: I do not recall what you mean at the moment.

STRANGER: They say that if any one knows how the ancient laws may be improved, he must first persuade his own State of the improvement, and then he may legislate, but not otherwise.

YOUNG SOCRATES: And are they not right?

STRANGER: I dare say. But supposing that he does use some gentle violence for their good, what is this violence to be called? Or rather, before you answer, let me ask the same question in reference to our previous instances.

YOUNG SOCRATES: What do you mean?
D: Narrative:
The same logic applies to the lawgiver. If the wise legislator who originally created the laws -- or someone equally wise -- were to return, would it be reasonable to prohibit them from changing the laws? That would be just as ridiculous as forbidding the returning physician from updating a prescription.

There is a common saying that anyone who knows how to improve the laws must first persuade the state to accept the improvement before legislating. This sounds reasonable. But suppose the wise legislator uses some gentle force for the good of the citizens -- what should this be called? The question requires returning to the analogies.
D: Refashioned:
Apply the same logic to politics. If the wise lawgiver returns -- or someone equally qualified appears -- should we prohibit them from updating the laws? That would be as absurd as forbidding the doctor from changing an outdated prescription.

There is a popular democratic principle here: anyone who wants to change the laws must first persuade the public to agree. This sounds fair. But what if the expert knows the change is necessary and the public refuses to understand? What if the wise ruler uses gentle coercion for the citizens' genuine benefit? What do we call that?
D: Elegant: If the wise lawmaker returns and sees that circumstances demand new laws, forbidding the change is as absurd as forbidding the doctor from updating a prescription. The democratic principle says: persuade the public first. But what happens when the expert knows the change is necessary and the public will not listen?
D: Summary: Prohibiting a wise legislator from updating outdated laws is as absurd as forbidding a doctor from changing an outdated prescription. The democratic requirement of persuasion before legislation raises the question of what happens when the public resists necessary change.
D: Distilled: The requirement that reformers must always persuade before they legislate breaks down when the public lacks the knowledge to recognize a necessary change.
Notes: The 'popular saying' about persuading the state before legislating reflects actual Athenian legal procedure -- the graphe paranomon allowed any citizen to prosecute someone who proposed an unconstitutional law. The question about 'gentle violence' (bia) for the public good is provocative, directly confronting democratic legitimacy. This tension between expertise and consent remains central to debates about judicial review, administrative regulation, and public health mandates. Plato's Republic (VI.488-489) uses the analogous 'ship of state' metaphor to make the same point about the absurdity of democratic navigation.

D: Original: STRANGER: Suppose that a skilful physician has a patient, of whatever sex or age, whom he compels against his will to do something for his good which is contrary to the written rules; what is this compulsion to be called? Would you ever dream of calling it a violation of the art, or a breach of the laws of health? Nothing could be more unjust than for the patient to whom such violence is applied, to charge the physician who practises the violence with wanting skill or aggravating his disease.
D: Narrative: Suppose a skilled physician compels a patient -- of whatever sex or age -- to undergo a treatment contrary to the written rules but genuinely good for them. No one would call this a violation of the art of medicine or a breach of the laws of health. It would be supremely unjust for the patient to accuse the physician who applies such beneficial force of incompetence or of worsening the disease.
D: Refashioned: When a skilled doctor overrides protocol because she knows something better is needed, no one calls it malpractice. The doctor acted against written rules precisely because she possessed the knowledge to do better. The political implication: when a wise ruler acts against existing law for the public good, calling it tyranny is equally unjust.
D: Elegant: When the expert overrides the protocol because she knows better, we do not call it malpractice. We call it medicine. The same logic applies to governance: the ruler who transcends law out of genuine wisdom is not a tyrant but the only true statesman.
D: Summary: A skilled doctor who overrides written treatment rules for a patient's genuine benefit is practicing good medicine, not malpractice. By analogy, a wise ruler who acts against existing law for the public good is governing well, not tyrannically.
D: Distilled: Overriding the rules out of genuine expertise is not malpractice in medicine or tyranny in politics -- it is the highest expression of the art.
Notes: This passage directly anticipates the modern concept of clinical judgment overriding protocol-based medicine -- a live debate in contemporary healthcare. In political philosophy, it parallels Carl Schmitt's controversial argument (Political Theology, 1922) that 'sovereign is he who decides on the exception,' though Plato's version roots the exception in wisdom rather than mere power. The key Platonic distinction is that the legitimacy of overriding rules depends entirely on the quality of knowledge behind the decision.

D: Original:
YOUNG SOCRATES: Most true.

STRANGER: In the political art error is not called disease, but evil, or disgrace, or injustice.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Quite true.

STRANGER: And when the citizen, contrary to law and custom, is compelled to do what is juster and better and nobler than he did before, the last and most absurd thing which he could say about such violence is that he has incurred disgrace or evil or injustice at the hands of those who compelled him.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Very true.
D: Narrative: In politics, error is not called disease but rather evil, disgrace, or injustice. Yet when a citizen is compelled contrary to law and custom to do what is actually juster, better, and nobler than before, the most absurd thing the citizen could say is that they have suffered disgrace, evil, or injustice at the hands of those who compelled them.
D: Refashioned: In politics we call error 'injustice' rather than 'disease,' but the logic holds. When a citizen is compelled against law and custom to act more justly than before, the last thing they can credibly claim is that they have been wronged. You cannot be the victim of injustice when the result is that you act more justly.
D: Elegant: We call political error 'injustice' rather than 'disease,' but the principle holds. When someone is compelled against law and custom to act more justly than before, claiming victimhood is absurd. You cannot be wronged by being made to do right.
D: Summary: When citizens are compelled to act more justly than existing law requires, they cannot credibly claim to have been wronged, even though their existing legal rights were overridden.
D: Distilled: You cannot be the victim of injustice when the result is that you act more justly than before.
Notes: This argument is perhaps the most dangerous in the dialogue from a modern civil liberties perspective. It assumes that justice can be objectively determined by an expert, and that outcomes justify process violations. Modern constitutionalism generally rejects this -- procedural rights exist precisely because no ruler can be trusted to reliably identify what is 'more just.' The argument echoes Socrates' position in the Crito (49a-d) that one must never act unjustly, even in response to injustice, but applies it in a more authoritarian direction.

D: Original: STRANGER: And shall we say that the violence, if exercised by a rich man, is just, and if by a poor man, unjust? May not any man, rich or poor, with or without laws, with the will of the citizens or against the will of the citizens, do what is for their interest? Is not this the true principle of government, according to which the wise and good man will order the affairs of his subjects? As the pilot, by watching continually over the interests of the ship and of the crew,—not by laying down rules, but by making his art a law,—preserves the lives of his fellow-sailors, even so, and in the self-same way, may there not be a true form of polity created by those who are able to govern in a similar spirit, and who show a strength of art which is superior to the law? Nor can wise rulers ever err while they observing the one great rule of distributing justice to the citizens with intelligence and skill, are able to preserve them, and, as far as may be, to make them better from being worse.
D: Narrative:
The justice of this principle does not depend on whether the ruler exercising it is rich or poor. Any person, with or without laws, with or against the citizens' will, may do what is in their interest so long as this is done according to genuine knowledge. This is the true principle of government: the wise and good ruler orders the affairs of subjects according to knowledge.

The analogy of the pilot makes this vivid. The ship's captain preserves the lives of the crew not by laying down rules but by making the art itself a law -- watching continually over the interests of ship and sailors, adapting to every changing condition. In the same way, a true form of government can be created by those able to govern in this spirit, showing a strength of art superior to written law. Wise rulers who distribute justice with intelligence and skill, preserving citizens and making them better, cannot err so long as they hold to this one great rule.
D: Refashioned:
Rich or poor, with or without laws, with or against consent -- none of it matters. The true principle is this: the ruler with genuine knowledge governs in the interest of the governed.

A captain does not navigate by manual. She reads wind and water, adapts continuously, and makes her art a law. She preserves the crew through judgment, not rules. The ruler whose art is superior to written law -- who distributes justice with intelligence and skill -- cannot truly err. The art itself is the highest form of law.
D: Elegant: A captain does not navigate by manual. She reads wind and water, adapts to every change, and makes her art a law. Her crew survives because of her judgment, not her rulebook. The true statesman governs the same way: knowledge as law, adaptive wisdom as the constitution, the welfare of the governed as the only fixed star.
D: Summary: Like a ship's captain who preserves the crew through adaptive expertise rather than rigid rules, the true ruler governs by making knowledge itself the law. Genuine political art, exercised with wisdom, is superior to any written code.
D: Distilled: The captain who navigates by skill rather than manual, adapting to every change, is the model of true governance: knowledge itself is the highest law.
Notes: The pilot/ship-of-state metaphor is one of the oldest in Western political thought, appearing in Alcaeus (fr. 208), Plato's Republic (VI.488-489), and throughout subsequent political philosophy. Here it serves the specific argument that adaptive expertise is superior to codified rules. Aristotle (Politics III.16) later argued that even the best pilot must operate within some framework of law. The phrase 'making his art a law' (technen nomon) encapsulates Plato's ideal: governance as a living art rather than a dead letter. This passage directly anticipates the common-law tradition's emphasis on judicial discretion and equity.

D: Original:
YOUNG SOCRATES: No one can deny what has been now said.

STRANGER: Neither, if you consider, can any one deny the other statement.

YOUNG SOCRATES: What was it?

STRANGER: We said that no great number of persons, whoever they may be, can attain political knowledge, or order a State wisely, but that the true government is to be found in a small body, or in an individual, and that other States are but imitations of this, as we said a little while ago, some for the better and some for the worse.

YOUNG SOCRATES: What do you mean? I cannot have understood your previous remark about imitations.

STRANGER: And yet the mere suggestion which I hastily threw out is highly important, even if we leave the question where it is, and do not seek by the discussion of it to expose the error which prevails in this matter.

YOUNG SOCRATES: What do you mean?
D: Narrative:
No one can deny these conclusions. But equally undeniable is the earlier point: no great number of people can attain political knowledge. True government belongs to a small body or a single individual. All other states are merely imitations -- some better, some worse.

Young Socrates asks what is meant by these imitations. The Stranger emphasizes that this point, though introduced in passing, is extremely important. Even if the discussion moves on, the question of how other governments imitate the true one deserves serious attention.
D: Refashioned:
Two conclusions now stand together. First, the wise ruler who governs by knowledge rather than rigid law is the ideal. Second, genuine political knowledge is so rare that it can only reside in one person or a very small number. Every other form of government is an imitation of this ideal.

But the concept of imitation is critical. It is not a throwaway observation. How actual governments imitate -- and fail to imitate -- the ideal is the key to understanding which imperfect forms of government are more and less tolerable. This is where the argument turns practical.
D: Elegant: The ideal is a ruler who governs by knowledge. Such knowledge is vanishingly rare. Every actual government, therefore, is an imitation. The critical question is not whether your government is the ideal -- it is not -- but how well or how badly it imitates one.
D: Summary: Since genuine political knowledge is extremely rare, virtually all governments are imitations of the ideal. The crucial practical question becomes which imitations are better and which are worse.
D: Distilled: Since no actual government achieves the ideal of rule by genuine knowledge, the practical question is which imitation is least bad.
Notes: The concept of 'imitation' (mimesis) connects the political argument to Plato's broader metaphysics of Forms. Just as physical objects imperfectly imitate ideal Forms (Republic X), actual governments imperfectly imitate the ideal government. This transition -- from the impossible ideal to the practical ranking of imperfect alternatives -- is the argumentative pivot of the Statesman's political theory and marks Plato's most pragmatic engagement with real-world politics, distinguishing the Statesman from the more utopian Republic.

D: Original: STRANGER: The idea which has to be grasped by us is not easy or familiar; but we may attempt to express it thus:—Supposing the government of which I have been speaking to be the only true model, then the others must use the written laws of this—in no other way can they be saved; they will have to do what is now generally approved, although not the best thing in the world.
D: Narrative: The key idea, though unfamiliar, can be expressed as follows: if the government of genuine knowledge is the only true model, then all other governments must adopt the written laws of this ideal form in order to be preserved. They must do what is now generally approved -- follow established law -- even though this is not the best thing in the world.
D: Refashioned: Here is the practical turn. If rule by genuine knowledge is the ideal but essentially unattainable, then every real government must do the next best thing: follow written laws. Laws are imperfect. They are rigid where flexibility would be better. But they represent the codified wisdom of the best thinking available, and following them is the only reliable path to decent governance in the absence of a genuinely wise ruler.
D: Elegant: Since the ideal -- rule by pure knowledge -- is unattainable in practice, every real government must settle for the next best thing: faithful adherence to written law. Laws are imperfect, but they are the best substitute for wisdom we have.
D: Summary: Since genuinely wise rule is practically unattainable, all real governments must preserve themselves by adhering to written laws, which represent the best available substitute for perfect knowledge.
D: Distilled: In the absence of a genuinely wise ruler, faithful adherence to law is the second-best but most reliable path to decent governance.
Notes: This is the crucial pivot of the Statesman's political theory: the move from ideal to practical, from philosopher-king to rule of law. The concept of 'second-best' (deuteros plous, literally 'second sailing' -- using oars when the wind fails) became a key concept in Greek philosophy. This pragmatic turn distinguishes the Statesman from the Republic and anticipates Aristotle's more practical political philosophy. The idea that law is 'second-best' governance directly influenced Montesquieu's Spirit of the Laws and the entire tradition of constitutional government.

D: Original:
YOUNG SOCRATES: What is this?

STRANGER: No citizen should do anything contrary to the laws, and any infringement of them should be punished with death and the most extreme penalties; and this is very right and good when regarded as the second best thing, if you set aside the first, of which I was just now speaking. Shall I explain the nature of what I call the second best?

YOUNG SOCRATES: By all means.

STRANGER: I must again have recourse to my favourite images; through them, and them alone, can I describe kings and rulers.

YOUNG SOCRATES: What images?

STRANGER: The noble pilot and the wise physician, who 'is worth many another man'—in the similitude of these let us endeavour to discover some image of the king.

YOUNG SOCRATES: What sort of an image?
D: Narrative:
The principle of the second-best government is this: no citizen should do anything contrary to the laws, and any violation should be punished with death and the most extreme penalties. This is right and good when understood as the second-best option, inferior only to rule by genuine knowledge.

To explain this, the Stranger returns to his favorite analogies -- the noble pilot and the wise physician, who is worth many other men. Through these images, the nature of the true king can be made clearer.
D: Refashioned: The second-best principle is strict: no citizen may act contrary to the laws, and violations deserve the harshest penalties. The logic is defensive -- in the absence of genuine wisdom, the law is all that stands between order and chaos. The strictness is not about law being perfect but about the alternatives being worse.
D: Elegant: In the absence of genuine wisdom, the law must be inviolable -- not because it is perfect, but because the alternatives are worse. Strict adherence to imperfect law is not ideal governance. It is the last defense against chaos.
D: Summary: The second-best government demands strict obedience to law with severe penalties for violations -- not because law is perfect, but because in the absence of genuine wisdom, it is the only reliable safeguard against worse outcomes.
D: Distilled: Strict adherence to imperfect law is not the ideal, but it is the best defense against chaos when genuine wisdom is absent.
Notes: The phrase 'worth many another man' (pollon axios) echoes Homer (Iliad XI.514), where a physician is described as 'worth many men.' The advocacy for extreme penalties for lawbreaking marks a significant shift from the earlier argument that a wise ruler could legitimately override law. The tension between these two positions -- the ideal of wise rule above law and the practical necessity of rule of law -- is the central dialectic of the Statesman's political theory and remains unresolved in modern political philosophy.

D: Original: STRANGER: Well, such as this:—Every man will reflect that he suffers strange things at the hands of both of them; the physician saves any whom he wishes to save, and any whom he wishes to maltreat he maltreats—cutting or burning them; and at the same time requiring them to bring him payments, which are a sort of tribute, of which little or nothing is spent upon the sick man, and the greater part is consumed by him and his domestics; and the finale is that he receives money from the relations of the sick man or from some enemy of his, and puts him out of the way. And the pilots of ships are guilty of numberless evil deeds of the same kind; they intentionally play false and leave you ashore when the hour of sailing arrives; or they cause mishaps at sea and cast away their freight; and are guilty of other rogueries. Now suppose that we, bearing all this in mind, were to determine, after consideration, that neither of these arts shall any longer be allowed to exercise absolute control either over freemen or over slaves, but that we will summon an assembly either of all the people, or of the rich only, that anybody who likes, whatever may be his calling, or even if he have no calling, may offer an opinion either about seamanship or about diseases—whether as to the manner in which physic or surgical instruments are to be applied to the patient, or again about the vessels and the nautical implements which are required in navigation, and how to meet the dangers of winds and waves which are incidental to the voyage, how to behave when encountering pirates, and what is to be done with the old-fashioned galleys, if they have to fight with others of a similar build—and that, whatever shall be decreed by the multitude on these points, upon the advice of persons skilled or unskilled, shall be written down on triangular tablets and columns, or enacted although unwritten to be national customs; and that in all future time vessels shall be navigated and remedies administered to the patient after this fashion.
D: Narrative:
Consider how people actually experience doctors and pilots. Everyone suffers strange things at their hands. The physician saves whomever he wishes and maltreats others -- cutting, burning, causing pain -- while demanding payment, most of which goes to the doctor's own household rather than the patient. In the worst case, the doctor takes money from the patient's relatives or enemies and kills the patient. Ship captains are equally capable of treachery: they intentionally abandon passengers on shore when the ship is due to sail, cause shipwrecks at sea, and throw cargo overboard.

Now suppose that, bearing all this in mind, people decided that neither medicine nor navigation should be allowed to exercise unregulated authority over anyone. Instead, an assembly of all the people -- or just the wealthy -- would be convened, where anyone regardless of expertise could offer opinions about medicine and navigation. Whatever the assembly decided about treatment methods, surgical instruments, nautical equipment, and how to handle storms and pirates would be written down as permanent law. From that point on, all medicine would be practiced and all ships navigated according to these popular decrees.
D: Refashioned:
Think about what doctors and captains actually do with their unregulated authority. Doctors cut, burn, and cause pain. They charge fees that mostly benefit themselves, not their patients. In the worst cases, corrupt doctors take bribes and let patients die. Ship captains abandon passengers, cause wrecks, and dump cargo for their own advantage.

Seeing all this, imagine society decides: no more unregulated expertise. From now on, a popular assembly will vote on medical procedures, surgical techniques, navigation methods, and how to handle storms. Anyone can speak -- expert or not. Whatever the majority decides gets written into permanent law. All doctors must follow the assembly's medical protocols. All captains must navigate according to the assembly's nautical rules.

This is exactly what happens in politics. Distrusting the unregulated authority of individuals, we hand governance over to assemblies that vote on matters most participants do not understand, then codify the results as permanent law.
D: Elegant: Doctors abuse patients. Captains wreck ships. So imagine we strip both professions of autonomy and let popular assemblies vote on surgical techniques and navigation methods, with the results written into permanent law. This sounds insane for medicine and sailing. It is exactly what we do in politics.
D: Summary: Just as doctors and captains can abuse their authority, political rulers can too. But the proposed solution -- having popular assemblies vote on technical matters and codifying the results as permanent law -- would destroy medicine and navigation. Plato implies the same approach is equally destructive in politics.
D: Distilled: We would never let a popular vote determine surgical technique or navigation method, yet we routinely let it determine how to govern.
Notes: This extended analogy is one of Plato's most provocative anti-democratic arguments. The scenario of assemblies voting on medicine prefigures modern debates about whether democratic processes are appropriate for technical decisions (climate policy, pandemic response, monetary policy). The reference to 'triangular tablets and columns' (kyrbeis and axones) alludes to the actual physical format of Athenian laws, traditionally attributed to Solon and Draco. The mention of pirates and old-fashioned galleys grounds the analogy in real Athenian maritime experience.

D: Original:
YOUNG SOCRATES: What a strange notion!

STRANGER: Suppose further, that the pilots and physicians are appointed annually, either out of the rich, or out of the whole people, and that they are elected by lot; and that after their election they navigate vessels and heal the sick according to the written rules.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Worse and worse.
D: Narrative: Young Socrates finds this scenario absurd. But the Stranger pushes further: suppose that in addition to these assembly-made rules, the physicians and pilots are now appointed annually, either from the wealthy class or from the general population, selected by lot. These elected officials then practice medicine and navigate ships strictly according to the written rules. The situation goes from absurd to catastrophic.
D: Refashioned:
It gets worse. Not only are the rules made by popular vote, but the people who execute them are selected at random -- appointed annually by lottery from the general population or the wealthy class. These randomly chosen officials then practice medicine and navigate ships strictly according to the assembly's written rules.

We instinctively recognize this as catastrophic for medicine and navigation. Yet annual appointment by lot was exactly how Athens selected most of its magistrates, and strict adherence to written law was exactly what it demanded of them.
D: Elegant: Now add annual appointments by lottery. Randomly selected citizens practice medicine and captain ships, following the assembly's written rules to the letter. For medicine, this is obviously catastrophic. For governance, Athens called it democracy.
D: Summary: Adding random annual selection of physicians and captains to the absurd scenario of assembly-made rules makes it obviously catastrophic. Yet this mirrors exactly how Athenian democracy selected and constrained its magistrates.
D: Distilled: We would never select doctors by lottery and bind them to assembly-written protocols, yet that is essentially how democratic governance works.
Notes: This passage directly targets Athenian democratic practice. Most Athenian magistrates were selected by lot (sortition) from eligible citizens and served one-year terms. The kleroterion (allotment machine) was a key institution of Athenian democracy. Aristotle (Politics IV.9) later argued that selection by lot was characteristic of democracy while election was characteristic of aristocracy. The modern revival of sortition (citizens' assemblies, deliberative democracy) engages with these same questions about whether random selection produces competent governance.

D: Original:
STRANGER: But hear what follows:—When the year of office has expired, the pilot or physician has to come before a court of review, in which the judges are either selected from the wealthy classes or chosen by lot out of the whole people; and anybody who pleases may be their accuser, and may lay to their charge, that during the past year they have not navigated their vessels or healed their patients according to the letter of the law and the ancient customs of their ancestors; and if either of them is condemned, some of the judges must fix what he is to suffer or pay.

YOUNG SOCRATES: He who is willing to take a command under such conditions, deserves to suffer any penalty.
D: Narrative:
There is more. When the year of office expires, the pilot or physician must appear before a court of review, where judges are selected from the wealthy classes or chosen by lot from the whole people. Anyone who wishes may accuse them of not having navigated their vessels or healed their patients according to the letter of the law and ancestral custom during the past year. If condemned, the judges determine the punishment.

Young Socrates remarks that anyone willing to take command under such conditions deserves whatever punishment they get.
D: Refashioned: At year's end, every doctor and captain faces a public audit. The charge is not 'did you heal the patient?' but 'did you follow the written rules?' Competence is measured by obedience to procedure, not results. The system evaluates compliance, not outcomes.
D: Elegant: At year's end, the doctor faces trial. The charge: not whether patients were healed, but whether written rules were followed. Competence is measured by procedural compliance, not outcomes. Anyone willing to practice under such conditions, the interlocutor observes, deserves whatever punishment they receive.
D: Summary: After their term, officials face trial not for bad outcomes but for deviating from written rules. The system evaluates procedural compliance rather than actual results, measuring competence by obedience rather than effectiveness.
D: Distilled: A system that judges practitioners by compliance with rules rather than quality of outcomes has replaced competence with obedience.
Notes: This directly describes the Athenian institution of euthynai -- the mandatory public audit that every magistrate faced at the end of their term, where any citizen could bring charges. The graphe paranomon (indictment for unconstitutional proposals) similarly evaluated legality rather than wisdom. Modern parallels include performance reviews based on process compliance rather than outcomes, regulatory frameworks that prioritize rule-following over effectiveness, and the broader tension between accountability and innovation in bureaucratic systems.

D: Original: STRANGER: Yet once more, we shall have to enact that if any one is detected enquiring into piloting and navigation, or into health and the true nature of medicine, or about the winds, or other conditions of the atmosphere, contrary to the written rules, and has any ingenious notions about such matters, he is not to be called a pilot or physician, but a cloudy prating sophist;—further, on the ground that he is a corrupter of the young, who would persuade them to follow the art of medicine or piloting in an unlawful manner, and to exercise an arbitrary rule over their patients or ships, any one who is qualified by law may inform against him, and indict him in some court, and then if he is found to be persuading any, whether young or old, to act contrary to the written law, he is to be punished with the utmost rigour; for no one should presume to be wiser than the laws; and as touching healing and health and piloting and navigation, the nature of them is known to all, for anybody may learn the written laws and the national customs. If such were the mode of procedure, Socrates, about these sciences and about generalship, and any branch of hunting, or about painting or imitation in general, or carpentry, or any sort of handicraft, or husbandry, or planting, or if we were to see an art of rearing horses, or tending herds, or divination, or any ministerial service, or draught-playing, or any science conversant with number, whether simple or square or cube, or comprising motion,—I say, if all these things were done in this way according to written regulations, and not according to art, what would be the result?
D: Narrative:
The absurdity deepens further. A law must be enacted declaring that anyone who inquires into the true nature of piloting, navigation, health, or medicine contrary to the written rules is to be called not a physician or pilot but a 'cloudy prating sophist.' Furthermore, on the grounds that such a person corrupts the young by persuading them to practice medicine or piloting unlawfully and to exercise arbitrary authority over patients or ships, anyone may indict them in court. If found to be persuading anyone, young or old, to act contrary to written law, they face the most extreme punishment. For no one should presume to be wiser than the laws, since the nature of medicine, health, piloting, and navigation is known to all -- anyone can learn the written rules.

If this principle were applied to all arts and sciences -- generalship, hunting, painting, carpentry, agriculture, horsemanship, divination, every branch of mathematics -- requiring everything to be done by written regulation rather than genuine art, the result would be catastrophic.
D: Refashioned:
Now the final absurdity. Anyone who independently investigates how medicine or navigation actually works -- contrary to the official written rules -- must be labeled not a doctor or pilot but a 'sophist' and a corrupter of the young. Anyone who persuades others to practice these arts based on genuine knowledge rather than written law faces prosecution and extreme punishment. The reasoning: no one should presume to be wiser than the laws, since the rules are publicly available and anyone can read them.

Extend this to every field. Generalship, agriculture, engineering, mathematics, every art and science -- all governed by majority-approved written regulations, with independent inquiry punishable by law.

The charge of being a 'corrupter of the young who presumes to be wiser than the laws' is, of course, exactly what Athens charged Socrates with. The analogy is pointed: a society that criminalizes independent expertise and equates wisdom with rule-following will destroy not just politics but every field of human knowledge.
D: Elegant: Criminalize independent inquiry. Label the genuine expert a 'sophist' and 'corrupter of the young.' Punish anyone who presumes to be wiser than the written rules. Apply this to every art and science. The result? The death of all knowledge. The charge against the independent expert is, of course, exactly the charge Athens brought against Socrates.
D: Summary: A society that criminalizes independent expertise, labels genuine investigators as 'sophists,' and punishes anyone who acts on knowledge rather than written rules would destroy every art and science. This scenario directly mirrors the charges brought against Socrates.
D: Distilled: Criminalizing the expert who presumes to know better than the written rules does not protect the public -- it destroys the knowledge that makes progress possible.
Notes: The echo of Socrates' trial is unmistakable and surely deliberate. The formal charges against Socrates in 399 BC included 'corrupting the youth' and 'not believing in the gods of the city' -- here recast as 'corrupting the young' by teaching them to follow knowledge rather than law. The phrase 'cloudy prating sophist' (adolesches kai meterologos tis sophistes) uses language from Aristophanes' Clouds, the comedy that contributed to prejudice against Socrates. The declaration that 'anyone can learn the written laws' satirizes the democratic assumption that political knowledge is universally accessible.

D: Original:
YOUNG SOCRATES: All the arts would utterly perish, and could never be recovered, because enquiry would be unlawful. And human life, which is bad enough already, would then become utterly unendurable.

STRANGER: But what, if while compelling all these operations to be regulated by written law, we were to appoint as the guardian of the laws some one elected by a show of hands, or by lot, and he caring nothing about the laws, were to act contrary to them from motives of interest or favour, and without knowledge,—would not this be a still worse evil than the former?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Very true.

STRANGER: To go against the laws, which are based upon long experience, and the wisdom of counsellors who have graciously recommended them and persuaded the multitude to pass them, would be a far greater and more ruinous error than any adherence to written law?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly.

STRANGER: Therefore, as there is a danger of this, the next best thing in legislating is not to allow either the individual or the multitude to break the law in any respect whatever.

YOUNG SOCRATES: True.

STRANGER: The laws would be copies of the true particulars of action as far as they admit of being written down from the lips of those who have knowledge?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly they would.

STRANGER: And, as we were saying, he who has knowledge and is a true Statesman, will do many things within his own sphere of action by his art without regard to the laws, when he is of opinion that something other than that which he has written down and enjoined to be observed during his absence would be better.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Yes, we said so.

STRANGER: And any individual or any number of men, having fixed laws, in acting contrary to them with a view to something better, would only be acting, as far as they are able, like the true Statesman?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly.

STRANGER: If they had no knowledge of what they were doing, they would imitate the truth, and they would always imitate ill; but if they had knowledge, the imitation would be the perfect truth, and an imitation no longer.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Quite true.

STRANGER: And the principle that no great number of men are able to acquire a knowledge of any art has been already admitted by us.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Yes, it has.
D: Narrative:
Young Socrates agrees that all arts would perish under such conditions and could never be recovered, because inquiry itself would be unlawful. Human life, already difficult, would become utterly unendurable.

But the Stranger introduces a further consideration. What if, while compelling all operations to follow written law, we appoint a guardian of the laws -- elected by show of hands or by lot -- who then ignores the laws entirely and acts from motives of interest or personal favor, without knowledge? This would be even worse than rigid adherence to written law.

To violate laws that are based on long experience and the wisdom of counselors who recommended them to the multitude would be a far greater and more ruinous error than adhering strictly to written law. Therefore, the next-best thing after genuine wise rule is this: neither the individual nor the multitude should be allowed to break the law.

Laws are copies of the true particulars of action as far as they can be written down by those with knowledge. The true statesman, possessing genuine knowledge, will do many things within his sphere by his art without regard to laws, when something better than what he originally prescribed becomes apparent. Any individual or group acting contrary to fixed laws with a view to something better would only be acting like the true statesman -- if they had genuine knowledge. But the principle that no great number of people can acquire genuine knowledge of any art has already been established.

Therefore, if anyone acts contrary to law without knowledge, they imitate the truth badly. If they act with knowledge, the imitation becomes perfect truth and is no longer an imitation at all.
D: Refashioned:
Without independent inquiry, every field of knowledge dies. Life becomes unendurable. But there is something even worse than rigid rule-following: an elected official who ignores the laws entirely and acts from self-interest or personal favoritism, without any genuine knowledge.

Violating laws that represent the accumulated wisdom of experienced people is far more destructive than rigidly adhering to those same laws. Imperfect rules based on real experience are better than no rules in the hands of the ignorant.

This yields the practical principle: in the absence of a genuinely wise ruler, no one -- individual or group -- should be permitted to break the law.

Laws are the best written approximation of what genuinely wise governance would look like. A true statesman with real knowledge would override them when something better becomes clear -- and would be right to do so. Anyone else who overrides them in pursuit of something 'better' is imitating the statesman. If they have real knowledge, the imitation succeeds and ceases to be mere imitation. If they lack knowledge -- as most people do -- the imitation fails, and the results are worse than following the rules would have been.

Since genuine political knowledge is vanishingly rare, the practical conclusion is clear: follow the law.
D: Elegant: There is something worse than rigid obedience to imperfect rules: a leader who ignores the rules out of self-interest while lacking the knowledge to improve on them. Laws represent accumulated wisdom. Breaking them requires genuine expertise, not just confidence. Since almost no one possesses that expertise, the practical rule is simple: follow the law. The wise ruler who truly knows better is the exception. Everyone else who claims to know better is a danger.
D: Summary: While genuine expertise can legitimately override law, most people who claim to know better than the law actually do not. Since lawbreaking by the ignorant is far more destructive than rigid adherence to imperfect rules, the practical conclusion is that law should be inviolable for everyone except the vanishingly rare genuine expert.
D: Distilled: The case for rule of law rests not on law being perfect, but on the fact that almost no one who claims to know better than the law actually does.
Notes: This is the central argument of the Statesman's political theory and one of the most influential passages in the history of political philosophy. The distinction between legitimate and illegitimate law-breaking -- based on whether the lawbreaker possesses genuine knowledge -- anticipates the modern debate over civil disobedience (Thoreau, MLK Jr.), judicial review, and executive prerogative. Aristotle (Politics III.15-16) developed this into his argument that law should rule because 'he who bids law rule may be deemed to bid God and Reason alone rule, but he who bids man rule adds an element of the beast.' Locke's entire Second Treatise can be read as an attempt to resolve this tension between expertise and law.

D: Original:
STRANGER: Then the royal or political art, if there be such an art, will never be attained either by the wealthy or by the other mob.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Impossible.

STRANGER: Then the nearest approach which these lower forms of government can ever make to the true government of the one scientific ruler, is to do nothing contrary to their own written laws and national customs.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Very good.

STRANGER: When the rich imitate the true form, such a government is called aristocracy; and when they are regardless of the laws, oligarchy.

YOUNG SOCRATES: True.

STRANGER: Or again, when an individual rules according to law in imitation of him who knows, we call him a king; and if he rules according to law, we give him the same name, whether he rules with opinion or with knowledge.

YOUNG SOCRATES: To be sure.

STRANGER: And when an individual truly possessing knowledge rules, his name will surely be the same—he will be called a king; and thus the five names of governments, as they are now reckoned, become one.

YOUNG SOCRATES: That is true.

STRANGER: And when an individual ruler governs neither by law nor by custom, but following in the steps of the true man of science pretends that he can only act for the best by violating the laws, while in reality appetite and ignorance are the motives of the imitation, may not such an one be called a tyrant?

YOUNG SOCRATES: Certainly.
D: Narrative:
The royal or political art will never be attained by the wealthy or by any large group of people. The nearest approach that inferior forms of government can make to the true government of the one scientific ruler is to do nothing contrary to their own written laws and national customs.

When the rich imitate the true form while observing laws, the government is called aristocracy. When they disregard laws, it becomes oligarchy. When an individual rules according to law in imitation of the wise ruler, he is called a king -- the same title whether he rules with genuine knowledge or merely with opinion. When an individual truly possesses knowledge, his name remains king, and the five conventional names of government reduce to one.

But when an individual ruler governs without law or custom, pretending to follow the model of the true statesman by claiming he can only act for the best, while in reality his motives are appetite and ignorance -- that ruler is called a tyrant.
D: Refashioned:
Genuine political expertise will never reside in the wealthy class or in any large group. The best that imperfect governments can do is obey their own laws.

This produces the classification. When the wealthy few govern lawfully, imitating the ideal, we call it aristocracy. When they govern lawlessly, it is oligarchy. When one person rules lawfully, we call him a king -- the same title regardless of whether he rules with true knowledge or merely competent judgment. And when one person truly possesses political knowledge, the five conventional labels collapse into one: all legitimate governance is simply wise rule.

The tyrant is the dark mirror of the true statesman. He claims to act above the law for the public good -- just as the genuine expert would -- but his real motives are appetite and ignorance. He imitates the form of wise rule without possessing its substance.
D: Elegant: Aristocracy is the wealthy few governing lawfully. Oligarchy is the wealthy few without law. A king rules alone under law. A tyrant rules alone without it, claiming wisdom he does not possess. The tyrant is the dark mirror of the true statesman: same form, opposite substance. One transcends law through knowledge. The other violates it through appetite.
D: Summary: The classification of governments emerges from how well they imitate rule by genuine knowledge. Aristocracy and kingship are lawful imitations; oligarchy and tyranny are lawless ones. The tyrant imitates the form of the wise ruler while lacking the substance -- motivated by appetite rather than knowledge.
D: Distilled: The tyrant is the false image of the true statesman: both claim to act above the law, but one is driven by knowledge and the other by appetite.
Notes: This sixfold classification (monarchy/tyranny, aristocracy/oligarchy, lawful/lawless democracy) plus the 'seventh' ideal was systematized by Aristotle in Politics III.7 and became the dominant framework for political taxonomy in Western thought through Polybius, Cicero, Machiavelli, and Montesquieu. The distinction between king and tyrant based on lawfulness (rather than, say, consent or heredity) was innovative and influential. The observation that the tyrant 'imitates' the true ruler while inverting the substance anticipates Machiavelli's analysis of the prince who must appear virtuous while exercising power.

D: Original: STRANGER: And this we believe to be the origin of the tyrant and the king, of oligarchies, and aristocracies, and democracies,—because men are offended at the one monarch, and can never be made to believe that any one can be worthy of such authority, or is able and willing in the spirit of virtue and knowledge to act justly and holily to all; they fancy that he will be a despot who will wrong and harm and slay whom he pleases of us; for if there could be such a despot as we describe, they would acknowledge that we ought to be too glad to have him, and that he alone would be the happy ruler of a true and perfect State.
D: Narrative: This is believed to be the origin of all these forms of government -- the tyrant, the king, oligarchies, aristocracies, and democracies. People are offended by the idea of a single ruler. They can never be brought to believe that anyone could be worthy of such unlimited authority, or would be able and willing to rule justly and wisely toward all, acting with virtue and knowledge. They assume that any such ruler would be a despot who wrongs, harms, and kills whomever he pleases. Yet if such a ruler as described actually existed, everyone would acknowledge that he alone should rule and that his would be the only truly happy and perfect state.
D: Refashioned: This explains why imperfect governments exist. People cannot trust a single ruler with unlimited power -- history gives them every reason to expect tyranny. And yet: if someone with genuine political wisdom existed, everyone would welcome their authority. The problem is not the ideal. It is that such a person almost certainly does not exist, and no method can reliably identify them.
D: Elegant: People distrust one-person rule because history shows what unchecked power does. If a genuinely wise and just ruler existed, everyone would welcome their authority. The problem is not the ideal -- it is that such a person almost certainly does not exist, and no one can reliably identify them if they do.
D: Summary: People reject one-person rule because they rightly distrust unlimited power. If a genuinely wise and just ruler existed, their authority would be welcome. But the impossibility of finding or verifying such a person is why imperfect forms of government exist.
D: Distilled: The case against absolute rule is not that a wise ruler would be bad, but that such a ruler almost certainly does not exist and cannot be reliably identified.
Notes: This passage is remarkably honest about the practical impossibility of the ideal it has spent the dialogue constructing. It anticipates the core insight of modern constitutionalism: institutions must be designed for the rulers we are likely to get, not the ones we wish we had. Lord Acton's 'power tends to corrupt' (1887) and James Madison's 'if men were angels, no government would be necessary' (Federalist No. 51, 1788) express the same insight. The tension between the theoretical best (wise absolute rule) and the practical best (constrained imperfect rule) is the permanent contribution of this dialogue to political thought.

D: Original:
YOUNG SOCRATES: To be sure.

STRANGER: But then, as the State is not like a beehive, and has no natural head who is at once recognized to be the superior both in body and in mind, mankind are obliged to meet and make laws, and endeavour to approach as nearly as they can to the true form of government.

YOUNG SOCRATES: True.
D: Narrative: The state is not like a beehive with a natural head who is at once recognized as superior in body and mind. Since no such naturally obvious leader exists among humans, people are obliged to come together, make laws, and endeavor to approach as nearly as possible the true form of government.
D: Refashioned:
Human communities have no natural, self-evident leader -- no queen bee whose authority everyone instinctively recognizes. Since nature does not provide an obvious ruler, people must do the work themselves: come together, deliberate, make laws, and try to approximate wise governance as closely as imperfect institutions allow.

This is the foundation of constitutional government. Not because it is ideal, but because nature gave us no alternative.
D: Elegant: A beehive has a natural queen. Human societies do not. Since nature provides no self-evident ruler, we must make laws and build institutions -- not because this is ideal, but because it is all we have.
D: Summary: Unlike a beehive with a natural queen, human communities have no self-evident leader. People must therefore create laws and institutions to approximate good governance as closely as possible.
D: Distilled: Because nature provides no self-evident ruler, humans must construct laws and institutions to approximate the wise governance that no single person can reliably provide.
Notes: The beehive analogy was common in Greek political thought; Aristotle (Politics I.2, History of Animals V.21) also used it, though he drew different conclusions. The recognition that humans lack a 'natural head' is a remarkable concession from Plato, whose Republic argued for philosopher-kings as something close to natural rulers. This passage is often cited as Plato's most democratic moment -- acknowledging that the absence of natural authority necessitates collective political construction. It anticipates social contract theory (Hobbes, Locke, Rousseau) and the modern democratic premise that legitimate authority must be constructed, not discovered.

D: Original: STRANGER: And when the foundation of politics is in the letter only and in custom, and knowledge is divorced from action, can we wonder, Socrates, at the miseries which there are, and always will be, in States? Any other art, built on such a foundation and thus conducted, would ruin all that it touched. Ought we not rather to wonder at the natural strength of the political bond? For States have endured all this, time out of mind, and yet some of them still remain and are not overthrown, though many of them, like ships at sea, founder from time to time, and perish and have perished and will hereafter perish, through the badness of their pilots and crews, who have the worst sort of ignorance of the highest truths—I mean to say, that they are wholly unaquainted with politics, of which, above all other sciences, they believe themselves to have acquired the most perfect knowledge.
D: Narrative: When the foundation of politics rests on written law and custom alone, with knowledge divorced from action, the resulting miseries in states are predictable and perpetual. Any other art built on such a foundation would ruin everything it touched. Yet we ought to marvel at the natural strength of the political bond: states have endured despite this fundamental flaw, time out of mind. True, many states founder and perish like ships at sea, through the incompetence of their leaders -- people wholly unacquainted with genuine politics yet fully convinced they possess the most perfect knowledge of it.
D: Refashioned:
Governance built on rules and tradition rather than genuine understanding would destroy any other field. Yet states endure -- a testament to the extraordinary resilience of the political bond, which somehow survives leaders who know nothing about governance but are certain they do.

Many states still sink, like ships captained by confident incompetents. The most dangerous political ignorance is the kind that believes itself expertise.
D: Elegant: Governance built on rules without understanding would destroy any other field. Yet states endure -- testimony to the extraordinary resilience of the political bond, which survives despite leaders who know nothing about governance but are certain they know everything. Many states still sink, like ships captained by confident incompetents. The most dangerous ignorance is the kind that believes itself wisdom.
D: Summary: States governed by written law without genuine political knowledge inevitably suffer, yet the political bond is resilient enough that many endure despite incompetent leadership. The greatest danger comes from leaders who are wholly ignorant of governance yet completely confident in their expertise.
D: Distilled: The most dangerous political ignorance is the kind that believes itself expertise, and the most remarkable political fact is that states survive it as often as they do.
Notes: The ship-of-state metaphor returns here in its most pessimistic form -- most ships of state are captained by confident incompetents. The observation that states endure 'despite' bad governance anticipates modern research on institutional resilience and path dependence. The Dunning-Kruger effect (1999) -- the cognitive bias where incompetent people overestimate their competence -- maps precisely onto Plato's observation about rulers 'wholly unacquainted with politics, of which they believe themselves to have acquired the most perfect knowledge.' Thucydides (History II.65) made a similar observation about post-Periclean Athenian leadership.

D: Original:
YOUNG SOCRATES: Very true.

STRANGER: Then the question arises:—which of these untrue forms of government is the least oppressive to their subjects, though they are all oppressive; and which is the worst of them? Here is a consideration which is beside our present purpose, and yet having regard to the whole it seems to influence all our actions: we must examine it.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Yes, we must.

STRANGER: You may say that of the three forms, the same is at once the hardest and the easiest.

YOUNG SOCRATES: What do you mean?

STRANGER: I am speaking of the three forms of government, which I mentioned at the beginning of this discussion—monarchy, the rule of the few, and the rule of the many.

YOUNG SOCRATES: True.

STRANGER: If we divide each of these we shall have six, from which the true one may be distinguished as a seventh.

YOUNG SOCRATES: How would you make the division?

STRANGER: Monarchy divides into royalty and tyranny; the rule of the few into aristocracy, which has an auspicious name, and oligarchy; and democracy or the rule of the many, which before was one, must now be divided.

YOUNG SOCRATES: On what principle of division?

STRANGER: On the same principle as before, although the name is now discovered to have a twofold meaning. For the distinction of ruling with law or without law, applies to this as well as to the rest.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Yes.

STRANGER: The division made no difference when we were looking for the perfect State, as we showed before. But now that this has been separated off, and, as we said, the others alone are left for us, the principle of law and the absence of law will bisect them all.

YOUNG SOCRATES: That would seem to follow, from what has been said.

STRANGER: Then monarchy, when bound by good prescriptions or laws, is the best of all the six, and when lawless is the most bitter and oppressive to the subject.

YOUNG SOCRATES: True.
D: Narrative:
The question now becomes: among these imperfect, imitative forms of government, which is least oppressive and which is worst? Though this question is somewhat beside the main purpose of defining the statesman, it influences all practical political action and must be examined.

The three basic forms -- monarchy, rule of the few, and rule of the many -- are the starting point. Each can be divided by the criterion of lawfulness, producing six forms from three, with the true government of knowledge standing apart as a seventh.

Monarchy divides into royalty and tyranny. The rule of the few divides into aristocracy and oligarchy. Democracy, though previously treated as a single category, must now also be divided: lawful democracy and lawless democracy, distinguished by the same principle of adherence to or violation of law.

Monarchy bound by good laws is the best of all six imperfect forms. Lawless monarchy -- tyranny -- is the most bitter and oppressive.
D: Refashioned:
Which imperfect government is least bad? This is the practical question that matters for real life.

Start with the three basic forms: one ruler, few rulers, many rulers. Divide each by whether they follow law or not. This produces six forms: kingship and tyranny, aristocracy and oligarchy, lawful democracy and lawless democracy. The ideal government of genuine knowledge stands apart as a seventh -- theoretically perfect but practically unattainable.

Now rank them. Monarchy bound by good laws is the best of the six. Tyranny -- lawless monarchy -- is the worst. This makes concentrated power a double-edged sword: the best and the worst governments are both forms of one-person rule.
D: Elegant: Divide each form of government -- one, few, many -- by whether it follows law. You get six: kingship, tyranny, aristocracy, oligarchy, lawful democracy, lawless democracy. The seventh, rule by genuine knowledge, stands apart as unattainable. Among the six, lawful monarchy is best and tyranny is worst. Concentrated power cuts both ways: the best and worst governments are both the rule of one.
D: Summary: The six forms of government -- kingship/tyranny, aristocracy/oligarchy, lawful/lawless democracy -- are ranked by quality. Lawful monarchy is the best imperfect form, tyranny the worst. Concentrated power is a double-edged sword, producing both the best and worst outcomes.
D: Distilled: Concentrated power is the most dangerous variable in politics: lawful monarchy is the best imperfect government, and tyranny is the worst.
Notes: This ranking -- best monarchy, worst tyranny, with democracy in the middle -- was adopted and modified by Aristotle (Politics III.7), Polybius (Histories VI), and Cicero (De Re Publica I). Polybius added the concept of anacyclosis (cyclical revolution between forms), while Montesquieu (Spirit of the Laws, 1748) replaced the classification with his own three types (republic, monarchy, despotism). The insight that concentrated power amplifies both good and bad governance remains central to constitutional design -- it is the fundamental argument for separation of powers and checks and balances.

D: Original: STRANGER: The government of the few, which is intermediate between that of the one and many, is also intermediate in good and evil; but the government of the many is in every respect weak and unable to do either any great good or any great evil, when compared with the others, because the offices are too minutely subdivided and too many hold them. And this therefore is the worst of all lawful governments, and the best of all lawless ones. If they are all without the restraints of law, democracy is the form in which to live is best; if they are well ordered, then this is the last which you should choose, as royalty, the first form, is the best, with the exception of the seventh, for that excels them all, and is among States what God is among men.
D: Narrative:
The government of the few occupies a middle position between the one and the many, intermediate in both good and evil. The government of the many is in every respect weak, unable to accomplish either great good or great evil compared to the others, because power is too minutely subdivided among too many holders.

This produces a paradoxical ranking. Democracy is the worst of all lawful governments because its diffusion of power prevents it from doing much good. But democracy is the best of all lawless governments because that same diffusion of power prevents it from doing much harm. If all governments are well-ordered and follow law, democracy is the least desirable. If all are lawless, democracy is the most livable.

The overall ranking thus runs: the ideal government of knowledge stands above all, as God among men. Below it, lawful monarchy is best, then aristocracy, then lawful democracy. Among the lawless forms, lawless democracy is least bad, then oligarchy, then tyranny at the bottom.
D: Refashioned:
Rule by the few falls in the middle. Rule by the many is weakest -- power so fragmented that nothing great, good or bad, gets done.

The paradox: democracy is the worst lawful government because diffused power prevents effective action, but the best lawless one because diffused power prevents concentrated abuse. When institutions work, you want decisive leadership. When they collapse, you want power too thin for anyone to do much damage.

Democracy is always mediocre -- never best, never worst. That mediocrity is its greatest practical virtue.
D: Elegant: Democracy is the worst lawful government because diffused power prevents decisive good. It is the best lawless government because diffused power prevents concentrated evil. When institutions work, democracy underperforms. When they fail, democracy is the safest place to be. Its mediocrity is its greatest practical virtue.
D: Summary: Democracy ranks last among lawful governments because fragmented power prevents effective action, but first among lawless ones because that same fragmentation prevents concentrated abuse. Its consistent mediocrity -- never best, never worst -- is paradoxically its greatest practical strength.
D: Distilled: Democracy's weakness -- power too fragmented for decisive action -- is also its greatest safety: no one can do much harm.
Notes: This ranking of democracy as 'least bad when things go wrong' has been enormously influential. Winston Churchill's famous quip that 'democracy is the worst form of government, except for all the others' (1947) essentially restates Plato's argument. The insight that diffused power limits both good and bad outcomes anticipates Madison's argument in Federalist No. 10 about faction and the advantages of extended republics. Aristotle (Politics IV.11) similarly argued for a 'mixed' constitution that balances the virtues and vices of different forms. The comparison of the ideal government to 'God among men' elevates the theoretical ideal to a transcendent plane, consistent with Plato's broader metaphysics.

D: Original:
YOUNG SOCRATES: You are quite right, and we should choose that above all.

STRANGER: The members of all these States, with the exception of the one which has knowledge, may be set aside as being not Statesmen but partisans,—upholders of the most monstrous idols, and themselves idols; and, being the greatest imitators and magicians, they are also the greatest of Sophists.

YOUNG SOCRATES: The name of Sophist after many windings in the argument appears to have been most justly fixed upon the politicians, as they are termed.

STRANGER: And so our satyric drama has been played out; and the troop of Centaurs and Satyrs, however unwilling to leave the stage, have at last been separated from the political science.

YOUNG SOCRATES: So I perceive.
D: Narrative:
The members of all these imperfect states -- with the sole exception of the one governed by genuine knowledge -- should be set aside as not true statesmen but mere partisans. They are upholders of monstrous idols and are themselves idols. Being the greatest imitators and magicians, they are also the greatest of sophists.

Young Socrates observes that the name 'sophist,' after many twists in the argument, has been most justly applied to these so-called politicians.

The Stranger concludes this section by noting that the satyric drama has been played out. The troop of centaurs and satyrs -- the rival claimants to political wisdom -- have at last been separated from genuine political science, however unwilling they were to leave the stage.
D: Refashioned:
Every political leader in every imperfect state is not a statesman but a partisan -- performing the role of wise ruler without possessing the substance. Politicians are the greatest sophists: masters of the appearance of knowledge who lack the reality.

The term 'sophist,' defined across an entire companion dialogue, applies most precisely to the people we call leaders. The false claimants have been exposed -- though they resist leaving the stage, as pretenders always do.
D: Elegant: Politicians in every imperfect state are not statesmen but partisans -- performers of governance, not practitioners of it. They are the greatest sophists: masters of the appearance of political knowledge who lack its substance. The word 'sophist,' pursued through an entire companion dialogue, finds its truest application in the people we call our leaders.
D: Summary: Political leaders in all imperfect states are ultimately sophists -- imitators who perform the appearance of wise governance without possessing genuine political knowledge. The term 'sophist' applies most precisely to those we conventionally call politicians.
D: Distilled: The greatest sophists are not the philosophers accused of sophistry, but the politicians who perform the appearance of wisdom while possessing none of it.
Notes: The reference to the 'satyric drama' invokes Greek theatrical convention: after three tragedies, a lighter satyr play was performed. The Stranger treats the exposure of false political claimants as this satyric fourth act following the more serious theoretical discussion. The 'centaurs and satyrs' are the demagogues, oligarchs, and other political pretenders. The connection to the Sophist -- the companion dialogue that defines the sophist as a maker of false images -- is explicit: politicians are the political equivalent of the sophists defined there. This closing move, equating democratic politicians with sophists, is one of Plato's most provocative claims and reflects his deep skepticism toward Athenian democratic practice after the trial and execution of Socrates.

D: Original:
STRANGER: There remain, however, natures still more troublesome, because they are more nearly akin to the king, and more difficult to discern; the examination of them may be compared to the process of refining gold.

YOUNG SOCRATES: What is your meaning?

STRANGER: The workmen begin by sifting away the earth and stones and the like; there remain in a confused mass the valuable elements akin to gold, which can only be separated by fire,—copper, silver, and other precious metal; these are at last refined away by the use of tests, until the gold is left quite pure.

YOUNG SOCRATES: Yes, that is the way in which these things are said to be done.
D: Narrative: Having stripped away the obviously alien arts from statesmanship, the Stranger warns that the hardest work remains.
